def check_plan_stability(df, pipeline_name, spark):
    """Compare current plan fingerprint against baseline."""
    current_fp = compute_plan_fingerprint(df)
    
    baseline = (spark.read.format('delta')
        .load('gridunion_prod.monitoring.plan_baselines')
        .filter(col('pipeline_name') == pipeline_name)
        .orderBy(col('recorded_at').desc())
        .limit(1)
        .collect()
    )
    
    if baseline and baseline[0]['fingerprint'] != current_fp:
        send_alert(
            f"Plan changed for {pipeline_name}: "
            f"baseline={baseline[0]['fingerprint']}, current={current_fp}"
        )
    
    # Record the current fingerprint
    record_fingerprint(spark, pipeline_name, current_fp)
Plan Regression During Spark Upgrades

Spark upgrades are the highest-risk event for plan stability. New optimization rules, changed cost model weights, and updated AQE thresholds can change plans across every pipeline simultaneously. GridUnion's Spark upgrade process includes a plan stability gate: before upgrading production, the platform team runs every pipeline against a staging cluster with the new Spark version and compares the plan fingerprints against the current production baselines. Fingerprint mismatches are triaged: beneficial changes (AQE finds a better strategy) are approved, neutral changes (cosmetic reordering) are approved, and harmful changes (lost broadcast, new shuffle) are investigated. The investigation determines whether the change is caused by a new optimization rule (expected, evaluate the trade-off), a changed cost model (expected, may need statistics refresh), or a regression in the new version (unexpected, report upstream). GridUnion's Spark 3.4 to 3.5 upgrade produced 7 plan changes across 50 pipelines: 4 beneficial, 2 neutral, and 1 harmful (a new rule prevented a broadcast in the billing pipeline, resolved by adjusting the broadcast threshold).

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Plan stability is not about freezing plans — it is about controlling change. A platform that never allows plan changes misses optimizations. A platform that allows uncontrolled plan changes risks production outages. The fingerprint system provides the middle ground: plans are allowed to change, but every change is detected, evaluated, and either approved or investigated. The baseline is updated after approval, and the system returns to monitoring for the next change. This model treats plan changes like code changes: they go through review before they reach production.

When you see *(1) or *(2) prefixes in a physical plan, the asterisk indicates that those operators are compiled into a single Java method through whole-stage code generation (codegen). Instead of each operator calling the next through virtual method dispatch (a function call per row per operator), codegen fuses the entire operator chain into tight, JIT-compiled loops that process rows without the overhead of generic operator interfaces. For a simple pipeline (scan → filter → project), the non-codegen path calls three virtual methods per row: read, evaluate filter, evaluate projection. The codegen path generates a single method that reads the row, evaluates the filter condition inline, and writes the projected columns — one method call per row instead of three, with no boxing, no virtual dispatch, and no intermediate object creation. The performance difference is typically 2–5x for CPU-bound operations and is the reason Spark outperforms most interpreted query engines on analytical workloads.
When Codegen Activates

Codegen activates when all operators in a stage support code generation. The supported operators include FileScan (Parquet, ORC), Filter, Project, HashAggregate, BroadcastHashJoin, SortMergeJoin, and most arithmetic and comparison expressions. The unsupported cases cause codegen to fall back to interpreted execution for the entire stage: Python UDFs (opaque to the JVM), extremely complex expressions (exceeding the JVM method size limit of 8000 bytes of bytecode), and certain uncommon operators (some window function configurations, external data sources that do not support the V2 scan API). In the physical plan, stages with codegen show the *(n) prefix. Stages without codegen show just the stage number without the asterisk. GridUnion monitors codegen coverage: the percentage of stages that use codegen. A drop in coverage after a code change indicates that a new UDF or unsupported expression was introduced, and the performance impact should be evaluated.
The JVM Method Size Limit

The JVM limits individual methods to 8000 bytes of bytecode. A codegen method that fuses a long operator chain (20+ operators with complex expressions) can exceed this limit, causing codegen to fall back to interpreted execution for the entire stage. The fallback is logged as a warning: 'Whole-stage codegen disabled for this plan because the generated code exceeds the bytecode limit.' GridUnion encountered this on a pipeline that computed 50 derived columns in a single withColumn chain — each column added expressions to the generated method until the 8000-byte limit was exceeded. The fix was splitting the computation into two stages with a repartition boundary between them, allowing each stage to generate its own method within the size limit. The repartition added a shuffle cost, but the codegen benefit on both sides more than compensated.
Codegen and Tungsten Memory

Codegen is part of Spark's broader Tungsten engine, which also includes off-heap memory management and cache-friendly data layouts. Tungsten stores rows in a compact binary format (UnsafeRow) that avoids Java object overhead: a row with 5 columns is stored as a contiguous byte array rather than 5 Java objects on the heap. Codegen generates code that operates directly on this binary format, avoiding the serialization/deserialization cost of converting between UnsafeRow and Java objects. This is why codegen provides such significant speedups: it eliminates both the virtual dispatch overhead and the object creation overhead. The combination of codegen and Tungsten is what makes Spark's DataFrame API dramatically faster than the RDD API for structured data processing — the RDD API cannot benefit from either optimization because it operates on opaque Java objects.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Codegen is Spark's answer to the 'abstraction penalty' of query engines: the cost of implementing operators as modular, composable components (clean engineering) versus implementing them as hand-written fused loops (fast execution). Codegen provides both: operators are written as modular components for maintainability, and the code generator fuses them into tight loops at runtime for performance. The practical takeaway for platform engineers: avoid operations that disable codegen (Python UDFs, excessively complex expression chains) in hot paths. If a UDF is necessary, isolate it in its own stage so the surrounding stages benefit from codegen.

The mid-level track taught you to verify pushdown by checking PushedFilters and PartitionFilters in the plan. This lesson goes inside the mechanism: how the PushDownPredicates rule decomposes compound filter conditions into pushable and non-pushable components, how the DataSource V2 API negotiates which predicates the source can handle, and how the Parquet reader evaluates pushed predicates against row group statistics. Understanding these internals lets you design filters that maximize pushdown, write custom data sources that support pushdown, and diagnose the subtle cases where a filter that should push down does not — not because of a UDF or a complex expression, but because of a type mismatch, a negation pattern, or a predicate decomposition limitation.
Predicate Decomposition

When a Filter node contains a compound condition (A AND B AND C), Catalyst decomposes it into individual conjuncts (A, B, C) and evaluates each for pushability independently. A conjunct is pushable if it references only one data source, uses only pushable operators (=, <, >, <=, >=, IN, IS NULL, IS NOT NULL, LIKE with a prefix pattern), and operates on leaf-level columns (not expressions or UDF results). A disjunction (A OR B) is pushable only if both A and B are pushable and reference the same data source. A negation (NOT A) is pushable only for simple cases: NOT (col = value) becomes col != value, which is pushable. NOT (col > value AND col < value2) is not decomposable into a single pushable predicate. This decomposition logic explains why some seemingly simple filters do not push down: NOT (region IN ('DE', 'FR')) is not decomposed into region NOT IN ('DE', 'FR') by all Spark versions, causing a pushdown failure that reads the entire table.
The DataSource V2 Pushdown API
Parquet Row Group Evaluation

When a predicate is pushed to the Parquet reader, the reader evaluates it at two levels. First, at the row group level: each row group's footer contains min/max statistics per column. If the predicate is col > 1000 and a row group's max value for col is 500, the entire row group (typically 128MB of data, containing millions of rows) is skipped without reading any data bytes. Second, at the page level (Parquet 2.0+ with page-level statistics): within a row group that passed the row-group check, individual pages can be skipped if their statistics prove no rows match. The combination of row-group and page-level skipping can reduce I/O by 90%+ for selective filters on sorted or clustered data. GridUnion's Z-ORDERed Silver tables produce especially effective row-group skipping because Z-ORDER clusters similar values into the same row groups, making the min/max ranges narrow and the predicates highly selective at the row-group level.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Pushdown is a negotiation, not a guarantee. The optimizer proposes predicates. The data source accepts or rejects each one. The Parquet reader evaluates accepted predicates against statistics. At each step, the predicate can be partially or fully handled, and the remaining evaluation falls to Spark's in-memory Filter operator. Understanding this chain lets you optimize at every level: design filters that decompose cleanly (conjunctions of simple predicates), write data sources that accept the maximum set of predicates, and organize data (Z-ORDER, sorting) so that row-group statistics are narrow enough for effective skipping. A filter that pushes down but has wide row-group statistics (because the data is unsorted) provides less benefit than a filter that pushes down on Z-ORDERed data where each row group contains a narrow value range.

The mid-level track verified column pruning by checking ReadSchema in the FileScan operator: if the schema lists only the needed columns, pruning is working. At senior scale, column pruning becomes more nuanced because GridUnion's data includes nested schemas: the SCADA telemetry table has a measurements struct with 200+ fields (voltage, current, frequency, temperature, humidity, wind_speed, and 194 more sensor readings), and a metadata struct with 30 fields (turbine_id, model, firmware_version, calibration_date, etc.). A query that needs only measurements.voltage and metadata.turbine_id should read only those 2 fields out of 230+, not the entire struct. Spark's NestedColumnPruning rule handles this, but it has limitations that the top-level pruning rule does not, and understanding those limitations is essential for optimizing wide nested schemas.
Nested Pruning Mechanics

The NestedColumnPruning rule traces which struct fields are actually referenced by downstream operators and pushes a pruned schema into the scan. For a query that selects measurements.voltage and metadata.turbine_id, the ReadSchema shows struct<measurements:struct<voltage:double>,metadata:struct<turbine_id:string>> — only the referenced nested fields, not the full 230-field struct. This pruning operates at the Parquet column chunk level: each struct field is stored as a separate column chunk in the Parquet file, so pruning a field means skipping its column chunk entirely. For GridUnion's 230-field SCADA table where a typical query references 5–10 fields, nested pruning reduces I/O by 95%+. The query reads approximately 5/230 ≈ 2% of the file data, compared to 100% without pruning.
When Nested Pruning Fails

rows: Entire struct passed to a UDF
The UDF takes the full measurements struct as input. Spark must read all 200 fields because it cannot determine which ones the UDF accesses.
Extract the needed fields before passing to the UDF: udf(col('measurements.voltage')) instead of udf(col('measurements'))
Struct used in a join condition
If the join condition references the struct (rare but possible with map-type keys), the entire struct is read for the comparison.
Extract the join key field before the join: withColumn('join_key', col('metadata.turbine_id')).join(...)
Schema evolution with struct fields
A new field added to the struct via mergeSchema may cause the pruner to read all fields if the schema metadata is inconsistent across files.
Ensure all files have consistent struct schemas. Run OPTIMIZE after schema evolution to rewrite files with the unified schema.
Explode on an array of structs
explode(col('measurements_array')) reads the full array including all struct fields, even if downstream operations reference only one field.
Select the needed fields from the exploded result immediately: explode('arr').alias('item').select('item.voltage')

blocks.content: GridUnion’s SCADA Telemetry Optimization

GridUnion's SCADA telemetry table holds 5,000 turbines × 365 days × 96 readings/day (15-minute intervals) = 175 million rows per year. Each row has a flat section (turbine_id, timestamp, region) and a nested measurements struct with 200 fields. The unoptimized analytics query that computed average wind speed per region read the full 200-field struct (4TB of data) to extract one field (wind_speed). After three optimizations — nested pruning (reading only measurements.wind_speed reduces I/O to 20GB), Z-ORDER on region and timestamp (improves row-group skipping for region-filtered queries), and partition pruning on the date partition column (reads only the requested month) — the same query reads 600MB instead of 4TB. The 6,600x I/O reduction turned a 12-minute query into a 4-second query. The three optimizations are independent and multiplicative: nested pruning provided 200x (200 fields to 1), partition pruning provided 12x (12 months to 1), and Z-ORDER skipping provided ~3x (row groups with out-of-range regions skipped).

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: For wide nested schemas, the choice of schema design determines query performance more than any runtime optimization. Flattening the 200-field struct into 200 top-level columns would make every column independently prunable without relying on NestedColumnPruning. But flattening makes the schema unmanageable — 200 columns in a SELECT * query, 200 fields in the golden schema file, 200 fields to validate. The struct provides organizational clarity at the cost of requiring nested pruning. The trade-off is acceptable when the pruning works (which it does for most query patterns). The senior-level skill is recognizing the query patterns where nested pruning fails (UDFs on structs, explode on arrays) and restructuring those specific queries to enable pruning, rather than flattening the entire schema.

This lesson synthesizes the module into a diagnostic toolkit for optimizer-related production issues. The mid-level track provided a five-question plan review checklist (Module 1, lesson 10). The senior-level toolkit goes deeper: it traces plan decisions to specific optimizer rules, compares CBO estimates against runtime actuals to detect estimation drift, identifies AQE adaptations in the Spark UI to verify that runtime corrections are working, and provides the incident retrospective template that GridUnion uses when an optimizer-related issue causes an SLA breach. The toolkit is designed for the scenario where the mid-level checklist says 'the plan looks wrong' but does not explain why it is wrong or how to fix it.
The Diagnostic Decision Tree

blocks.title: Optimizer diagnostic decision tree
blocks.content:
The plan uses SortMergeJoin where BroadcastHashJoin was expected
│
├─ Is the broadcast table > autoBroadcastJoinThreshold?
│   ├─ YES: The table is actually too large. Increase threshold or pre-filter.
│   └─ NO: Estimated size > threshold but actual size < threshold.
│       │
│       ├─ Are catalog statistics present? (DESCRIBE EXTENDED)
│       │   ├─ NO: Run ANALYZE TABLE. Recheck.
│       │   └─ YES: Statistics are stale or selectivity is mispredicted.
│       │       │
│       │       ├─ Does the table have a post-query filter?
│       │       │   ├─ YES: CBO uses pre-filter size. Add broadcast() hint.
│       │       │   └─ NO: Statistics are wrong. Re-run ANALYZE.
│       │       │
│       │       └─ Is AQE enabled? If yes, did AQE convert at runtime?
│       │           ├─ YES: AQE fixed it. The compile-time plan was wrong but execution was optimal.
│       │           └─ NO: AQE threshold may also be too low. Check adaptive threshold.
│       │
│       └─ Is this a custom data source?
│           └─ YES: The data source may not report accurate size estimates.
│                  Check the DataSource V2 estimateStatistics() implementation.
blocks.caption: The diagnostic tree traces a suboptimal join strategy back to its root cause: table size, statistics accuracy, selectivity estimation, AQE behavior, or data source reporting.

blocks.content: Comparing Estimates vs Actuals

def compare_estimates_vs_actuals(spark, query_df, query_name):
    """Compare CBO estimates against actual execution metrics.
    
    Run this after the query to log estimation accuracy to the monitoring table.
    """
    # Get the optimized plan's estimated row count
    optimized = query_df._jdf.queryExecution().optimizedPlan()
    estimated_rows = optimized.stats().rowCount()
    est_rows = int(estimated_rows.get()) if not estimated_rows.isEmpty() else -1
    
    # Execute the query and capture the actual row count
    actual_rows = query_df.count()
    
    ratio = est_rows / actual_rows if actual_rows > 0 and est_rows > 0 else None
    
    result = {
        'query_name': query_name,
        'estimated_rows': est_rows,
        'actual_rows': actual_rows,
        'ratio': ratio,
        'assessment': (
            'accurate' if ratio and 0.5 <= ratio <= 2.0 else
            'moderate_error' if ratio and 0.1 <= ratio <= 10.0 else
            'severe_error' if ratio else 'no_estimate'
        ),
    }
    
    if result['assessment'] == 'severe_error':
        send_alert(
            f"CBO estimation error for {query_name}: "
            f"estimated {est_rows:,}, actual {actual_rows:,} ({ratio:.1f}x)"
        )
    
    # Log to monitoring table
    log_estimation_metrics(spark, result)
    return result
The Optimizer Incident Retrospective Template

When an optimizer-related issue causes an SLA breach, GridUnion conducts an optimizer-specific retrospective that goes beyond the standard blameless post-mortem. The template asks five optimizer-specific questions: (1) What was the CBO's estimate at the failing operator, and what was the actual? This quantifies the estimation error. (2) Which optimization rule (or absence of rule) caused the suboptimal plan decision? This identifies whether the issue is a missing rule, a wrong cost model weight, or a statistics problem. (3) Did AQE attempt to correct the decision at runtime, and if so, why was the correction insufficient? This evaluates whether AQE's thresholds need adjustment. (4) Would refreshing statistics (ANALYZE TABLE) have prevented the issue? This determines whether the root cause is operational (stale statistics) or structural (the cost model's assumptions are wrong for this data). (5) What monitoring change would detect this issue earlier in the future? This closes the feedback loop with a concrete action — typically a new estimation accuracy alert or a plan fingerprint baseline update.
What Comes Next

This module provided the implementation-level understanding of Catalyst and AQE that senior engineers need to diagnose optimizer failures, evaluate upgrade risks, and design custom rules. Module 3 applies this understanding to the most impactful physical data organization decision: partition strategy at multi-terabyte scale. The partition strategy module builds directly on the statistics propagation knowledge from this module (partition sizing depends on accurate statistics), the AQE adaptation mechanics (partition coalescing interacts with your partition design), and the plan stability practices (partition scheme changes affect every downstream plan). Where this module asked 'how does the optimizer decide?' Module 3 asks 'how do you design the data layout so the optimizer can decide well?'

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: The optimizer is your colleague, not your adversary. It has a clear cost model, documented rules, and predictable behavior — when you understand its internals. Most optimizer 'failures' are not bugs in the optimizer but information deficits: stale statistics, missing column histograms, unpredictable filter selectivity, or post-filter size reductions that the compile-time model cannot see. The diagnostic toolkit from this lesson treats optimizer issues as information problems: identify the information deficit, supply the missing information (statistics, hints, materialization points), and verify that the optimizer's decision improves. This approach is more reliable and more maintainable than a collection of hints that override the optimizer based on guesswork.

chapters.title: Partition Strategy at Scale
chapters.description: Design, implement, and maintain partition strategies for multi-terabyte datasets — covering column selection, granularity trade-offs, dynamic partition pruning, small files remediation, and the operational burden of repartitioning a live production pipeline.

blocks.content: The mid-level track designed partition strategies for tables consumed by one team with a predictable set of query patterns. At GridUnion scale, the Silver meter readings table is consumed by 12 pipelines across 6 teams, and their query patterns conflict. The German billing pipeline filters by date first (monthly billing periods), then by region within Germany. The regulatory export pipeline filters by country first (BNetzA needs all German data isolated), then by date. The analytics team's demand forecasting model filters by region first (it trains per-region models), then by date range (12-month lookback windows). The ML team's feature engineering pipeline scans the entire table with no partition filter at all (it needs cross-country, cross-date features). No single partition scheme optimizes for all four patterns. The partition design decision becomes a negotiation: which consumers' query patterns should the partition scheme favor, and which consumers must accept suboptimal scan performance?
The Consumer Survey

GridUnion's partition design process starts with a structured consumer survey. Every registered consumer of the table (from the data product manifest, Module 1 lesson 5) is asked three questions: what columns appear in your most common filter condition, how selective is that filter (what fraction of the table does it return), and how frequently does your pipeline run (daily, hourly, ad-hoc). The answers are compiled into a consumer query profile table. For the Silver meter readings table, the survey revealed: billing (filters on date, monthly, returns ~8% of data), regulatory (filters on country, quarterly, returns ~25%), analytics (filters on region, daily, returns ~3%), ML (no partition filter, weekly, returns 100%), operations dashboard (filters on region and date, every 15 minutes via streaming, returns ~0.3%). The streaming dashboard is the most latency-sensitive and the most selective; the ML pipeline is the least selective but runs infrequently.
The Partition Column Scoring Matrix

rows: country
25% (4 countries, roughly equal)
High — low cardinality, frequently filtered, good selectivity
date
0.1% (one day of 1,095)
High — most frequently filtered, excellent selectivity per day
region
3.3% (1 of 30 regions)
Medium — good selectivity but produces 30 partition directories per date
meter_id
N/A
Reject — no consumer filters on meter_id; 3.5M directories is catastrophic
customer_type
varies (residential: 70%, commercial: 20%, industrial: 8%, government: 2%)
Low — only one consumer filters on it; skewed distribution

blocks.content: GridUnion’s Partition Decision

The scoring matrix pointed to country and date as the two partition columns. Country is low-cardinality (4 values), satisfies the regulatory isolation requirement (country-level directories are auditable), and is filtered by 60% of consumers. Date is high-cardinality but provides excellent per-day selectivity and is filtered by 80% of consumers. The partition hierarchy is country/date, producing 4 × 1,095 = 4,380 directories for the three-year archive. Region was considered as a third level (country/region/date) but rejected: it would produce 4 × 30 × 1,095 = 131,400 directories, which exceeds the 100,000-directory guideline for cloud storage metadata performance. Instead, region-based queries rely on Z-ORDER clustering within files (lesson 3) and dynamic partition pruning (lesson 4) rather than partition-level directory isolation. The ML team, which scans the full table, accepted the country/date scheme because their weekly job can afford the 8TB scan and does not benefit from any partition filter.

The decision was documented in an Architecture Decision Record (Module 1, lesson 10) with the consumer survey results, the scoring matrix, the rejected alternatives (date-only, region/date, country/region/date), and the conditions that would trigger a redesign: if the region count exceeds 50 (making Z-ORDER insufficient for region-based queries), if a new high-frequency consumer requires region-level partition pruning, or if the table exceeds 20TB (requiring finer-grained partitioning for storage management). The ADR was reviewed by the governance committee and approved with the caveat that the Z-ORDER strategy for region (lesson 3) must be validated against the analytics team's query latency requirements before the partition scheme is deployed.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Partition design for multi-consumer tables is a multi-objective optimization problem with no globally optimal solution. The scoring matrix provides a structured framework for making the trade-off explicit: which consumers benefit, which consumers are disadvantaged, and what complementary techniques (Z-ORDER, DPP, materialized views) mitigate the disadvantages. The worst outcome is not choosing a suboptimal partition scheme — it is choosing without consulting the consumers and discovering the mismatch after 8TB of data has been written.

The mid-level track provided the partition sizing formula: data_size / target_partition_size, targeting 128–256MB per partition. At 3.2TB, this formula worked well. At 8TB and growing 500K rows/day, the formula is necessary but not sufficient. The sizing decision must account for the interplay between partition directory count (too many directories degrade cloud storage listing performance), file count per directory (too many files per directory degrade read performance), individual file size (too small wastes metadata I/O, too large prevents parallelism), and the growth trajectory (a partition scheme designed for 8TB may be wrong at 20TB in two years). This lesson provides the complete sizing model that GridUnion uses to design partition schemes with a 2-year planning horizon, including the directory count projections, file size targets, and the triggers that indicate when the current scheme needs revision.
The Sizing Model

import math
from datetime import date

def partition_sizing_model(
    current_size_tb: float,
    daily_growth_gb: float,
    partition_columns: list[dict],  # [{name, cardinality}]
    planning_horizon_years: int = 2,
    target_file_size_mb: int = 256,
    max_directories: int = 100_000,
    max_files_per_directory: int = 100,
) -> dict:
    """Model the partition scheme's health over the planning horizon.
    
    Returns sizing metrics and warnings for the proposed partition scheme.
    """
    # Total data at end of planning horizon
    future_size_tb = current_size_tb + (daily_growth_gb * 365 * planning_horizon_years / 1024)
    future_size_gb = future_size_tb * 1024
    
    # Total directory count = product of all partition column cardinalities
    # Account for cardinality growth (e.g., date adds 365 per year)
    total_cardinality = 1
    for col in partition_columns:
        future_card = col['cardinality']
        if col.get('grows_per_year'):
            future_card += col['grows_per_year'] * planning_horizon_years
        total_cardinality *= future_card
    
    # Average data per directory
    avg_dir_size_gb = future_size_gb / total_cardinality
    avg_dir_size_mb = avg_dir_size_gb * 1024
    
    # Files per directory (targeting target_file_size_mb per file)
    files_per_dir = max(1, math.ceil(avg_dir_size_mb / target_file_size_mb))
    total_files = total_cardinality * files_per_dir
    
    warnings = []
    if total_cardinality > max_directories:
        warnings.append(f"Directory count {total_cardinality:,} exceeds {max_directories:,} limit")
    if files_per_dir > max_files_per_directory:
        warnings.append(f"Files per directory {files_per_dir} exceeds {max_files_per_directory} limit")
    if avg_dir_size_mb < 10:
        warnings.append(f"Avg directory size {avg_dir_size_mb:.0f}MB is very small (tiny partitions)")
    if total_files > 500_000:
        warnings.append(f"Total file count {total_files:,} exceeds 500K (cloud listing overhead)")
    
    return {
        'future_size_tb': round(future_size_tb, 1),
        'total_directories': total_cardinality,
        'avg_directory_size_mb': round(avg_dir_size_mb, 0),
        'files_per_directory': files_per_dir,
        'total_files': total_files,
        'warnings': warnings,
    }

# GridUnion's Silver meter readings: country/date partitioning
result = partition_sizing_model(
    current_size_tb=8.0,
    daily_growth_gb=8.0,
    partition_columns=[
        {'name': 'country', 'cardinality': 4},
        {'name': 'date', 'cardinality': 1095, 'grows_per_year': 365},
    ],
)
print(result)
# {'future_size_tb': 13.7, 'total_directories': 7300, 'avg_directory_size_mb': 1923,
#  'files_per_directory': 8, 'total_files': 58400, 'warnings': []}
Interpreting the Model

The model projects that in 2 years, the table will be 13.7TB with 7,300 directories (well under the 100,000 limit), 8 files per directory (well under the 100 limit), and 58,400 total files (well under the 500,000 cloud listing threshold). All metrics are healthy, confirming that the country/date partition scheme will sustain the projected growth. The model also evaluated the rejected country/region/date scheme: 131,400 directories at current scale, growing to 219,000 in 2 years — exceeding the 100,000-directory limit and triggering the cloud metadata overhead warning. The rejected scheme's average directory size would be 64MB (acceptable) but the sheer directory count makes it unsuitable for cloud storage. The model provides the quantitative evidence that the governance committee uses to approve or reject partition scheme proposals.
Growth Triggers

GridUnion's partition design includes explicit growth triggers — the conditions under which the current scheme must be re-evaluated. For the Silver meter readings table, the triggers are: total directory count exceeds 50,000 (re-evaluate whether cloud listing performance is degrading), total file count exceeds 300,000 (re-evaluate compaction frequency), average file size drops below 64MB (re-evaluate the compaction target), or a new country is added to the platform (recalculate all sizing metrics). The triggers are monitored by the partition health dashboard (lesson 9) and generate alerts when thresholds are approached. The alerts give the platform team months of lead time to plan a partition scheme revision rather than reacting to a production incident caused by degraded listing performance.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: A partition scheme that is correct today may be wrong in two years. The sizing model projects future state to prevent designing for the current data volume and discovering the problem only after the table has grown past the scheme's capacity. The model is not a prediction — it is a planning tool. The actual growth may differ from the projection, which is why the growth triggers provide an early warning system that detects when reality diverges from the plan. Design for 2 years, monitor continuously, and revise when the triggers fire.

Partitioning organizes data into directories; Z-ORDER organizes data within files. Partitioning enables directory-level skipping: a filter on country = 'DE' skips all non-German directories. Z-ORDER enables row-group-level skipping: a filter on region = 'BERLIN' skips row groups within the German partition whose min/max statistics prove they contain no Berlin data. The two techniques are complementary: partitioning provides coarse-grained skipping at the filesystem level, and Z-ORDER provides fine-grained skipping at the Parquet statistics level. GridUnion's Silver meter readings table is partitioned by country and date (lesson 1) but not by region (rejected due to directory count). Z-ORDER on region provides the region-level skipping that the analytics team needs without the directory explosion that region-level partitioning would cause.
How Z-ORDER Works

Z-ORDER clustering uses a space-filling curve (specifically, a Z-order curve or Morton code) to interleave the bits of the Z-ORDER column values, producing a single sort key that co-locates rows with similar values across all Z-ORDER columns. When the data is sorted by this interleaved key and split into Parquet row groups, each row group contains rows from a narrow range of Z-ORDER column values. The Parquet footer's min/max statistics for each row group then reflect this narrow range, enabling the reader to skip row groups whose range does not overlap the filter condition. For a single Z-ORDER column (region), the effect is equivalent to sorting by region within each partition: all Berlin rows are in the same row groups, all Munich rows are in the same row groups, and a filter on region = 'BERLIN' reads only the Berlin row groups. For multiple Z-ORDER columns (region, meter_type), the interleaving co-locates rows that are similar on both columns, providing moderate skipping on either column but not as effective as single-column sorting on one specific column.
Z-ORDER Column Selection

rows: Single column (region)
Excellent for that column. Row groups contain narrow ranges.
One column dominates filter patterns across consumers
Silver meter readings: region is the most common non-partition filter
Two columns (region, meter_type)
Good for both, but each column's ranges are wider than single-column Z-ORDER
Two columns are roughly equally important in filter patterns
SCADA telemetry: both turbine_model and region are commonly filtered
Three+ columns
Diminishing returns. Each column's range becomes wider, reducing skip effectiveness.
Rarely justified. Consider whether partitioning handles one of the columns instead.
Not used at GridUnion — two Z-ORDER columns is the maximum per table

blocks.content: Z-ORDER vs Partitioning — The Trade-Off

The choice between partitioning and Z-ORDER on a column depends on the column's cardinality and the tolerance for directory proliferation. Low-cardinality columns (country: 4 values) are ideal for partitioning: 4 directories, perfect directory-level skipping. Medium-cardinality columns (region: 30 values) are borderline: 30 directories per date partition is manageable, but combined with date and country it produces 131,400 directories (too many). Z-ORDER on region provides 70–90% of the skipping benefit of partitioning with zero directory overhead. High-cardinality columns (meter_id: 3.5M values) cannot use either partitioning or Z-ORDER effectively: partitioning creates 3.5M directories, and Z-ORDER on a column with 3.5M distinct values cannot co-locate enough rows per value to make row-group skipping useful (each value appears in only 1–2 rows per file). For such columns, bucketing (lesson 6) is the appropriate strategy.
Measuring Z-ORDER Effectiveness

from delta.tables import DeltaTable
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, count, input_file_name

# Apply Z-ORDER on the Silver readings table
dt = DeltaTable.forPath(spark, 'gridunion_prod.silver_de.meter_readings')
dt.optimize().executeZOrderBy('region')

# Measure skipping effectiveness: count files read with and without Z-ORDER
def measure_skipping(spark, table_path, filter_col, filter_value):
    """Compare files read with a filter before and after Z-ORDER."""
    df = (spark.read.format('delta').load(table_path)
        .filter(col(filter_col) == filter_value)
        .select(input_file_name().alias('file'))
        .distinct()
    )
    files_read = df.count()
    
    total_files = (spark.read.format('delta').load(table_path)
        .select(input_file_name().alias('file'))
        .distinct()
        .count()
    )
    
    skip_pct = (1 - files_read / total_files) * 100
    print(f"Filter: {filter_col}={filter_value}")
    print(f"Files read: {files_read} of {total_files} ({skip_pct:.0f}% skipped)")
    return skip_pct

# Before Z-ORDER: ~5% skipped (random distribution across files)
# After Z-ORDER:  ~87% skipped (Berlin data concentrated in ~13% of files)
measure_skipping(spark, 'gridunion_prod.silver_de.meter_readings', 'region', 'BERLIN')

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Z-ORDER is not a replacement for partitioning — it is the optimization you use when partitioning is inappropriate due to cardinality or directory count constraints. The effectiveness depends on data volume per Z-ORDER value: if the value has enough rows to fill multiple row groups (thousands of rows), Z-ORDER produces tight min/max ranges and excellent skipping. If the value has only a few rows scattered across the table, Z-ORDER cannot concentrate them into dedicated row groups and the skipping benefit is minimal. Measure the skip percentage after Z-ORDER (the code above) to verify that the technique provides sufficient benefit for your query patterns.

The mid-level track introduced dynamic partition pruning (DPP) as a feature that uses the filter side of a join to prune partitions on the probe side at runtime. This lesson goes inside the mechanism: how Spark generates the partition filter from the build side's output, how the filter is injected into the probe side's scan, the conditions that prevent DPP from activating, and the interaction between DPP and AQE. Understanding DPP at the implementation level lets you design table structures and join patterns that enable DPP, diagnose cases where DPP silently fails, and estimate the I/O savings before running the query. GridUnion's billing pipeline demonstrates both the power and the fragility of DPP: a single missing broadcast hint caused DPP to deactivate, turning a 200GB scan into a 6TB scan without any error or warning.
The DPP Mechanism

DPP works in three steps. Step 1: during the build side of a join (typically a broadcast hash join), Spark collects the distinct values of the join key from the build side's output. These values become a runtime filter. Step 2: before the probe side's scan begins, Spark injects the runtime filter as a partition filter on the probe table. If the join key is a partition column on the probe side, the filter prunes directories at the filesystem level. Step 3: the probe side's scan reads only the partitions whose key values appear in the build side's output. The result is that the probe side reads a fraction of the table proportional to the selectivity of the build side's filter. If the build side selects 3 out of 30 regions, DPP prunes the probe side to 3/30 = 10% of the partitions. The pruning happens before any data is read, so the I/O reduction is realized immediately.
Conditions for DPP Activation

rows: Probe table is partitioned on the join key
DPP works by injecting a partition filter. Without partition directories, there is nothing to prune.
DPP is not applied. The probe side performs a full scan. No error or warning.
Build side is small enough to broadcast (or AQE converts to broadcast)
DPP requires the build side's values to be collected before the probe scan. Broadcast collects them; sort-merge does not.
DPP is not applied. The sort-merge join shuffles both sides without pruning.
spark.sql.optimizer.dynamicPartitionPruning.enabled = true (default)
The master switch for DPP.
DPP is globally disabled. Very rare in practice.
The join key is not wrapped in a function or cast
DPP requires the join key to match the partition column exactly. A cast(partition_col AS string) prevents the match.
DPP is not applied. The partition column and join key are semantically equivalent but syntactically different.
The build side has a filter that reduces its output
DPP provides value only when the build side is selective. If the build side returns all values, DPP prunes nothing.
DPP activates but provides no benefit (all partition values are included in the filter).

blocks.content: GridUnion’s Silent DPP Failure

GridUnion's quarterly billing pipeline joins the 8TB Silver readings table with a 120-row regulatory compliance table filtered to the 3 active compliance rules for Germany. The join key is country (a partition column on Silver). With DPP active, the scan reads only the Germany partition (2TB of 8TB = 75% reduction). Without DPP, the scan reads the entire 8TB. The pipeline originally used a broadcast hint on the compliance table, enabling DPP. During a code refactoring, the broadcast hint was accidentally removed. The CBO estimated the compliance table at 120 rows and should have broadcast it automatically, but the compliance table was loaded from a JDBC source without catalog statistics, and the CBO's file-metadata fallback estimated it at 15MB (above the 10MB default threshold). Catalyst chose sort-merge. DPP deactivated silently. The pipeline's scan stage went from 45 seconds (2TB) to 8 minutes (8TB). Nobody noticed for 6 weeks because the pipeline still completed within its 30-minute SLA. The quarterly review caught the regression through the scan volume monitoring metric (lesson 9). Restoring the broadcast hint re-enabled DPP and the scan dropped back to 2TB.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: DPP is one of the most impactful optimizations in Spark — and one of the most fragile. It activates silently when conditions are met and deactivates silently when any condition fails. There is no error, no warning, and no notification. The only signal is the scan volume metric: a DPP-enabled scan reads a fraction of the table, and a non-DPP scan reads the entire table. GridUnion's monitoring dashboard tracks scan volume as a percentage of table size, and an alert fires when the percentage exceeds the expected value by more than 2x. This monitoring is the only reliable way to detect DPP deactivation, because the Spark UI does not flag the absence of DPP — it simply shows a larger scan.

The mid-level track addressed small files as a maintenance concern: a nightly compaction job that merges files in the Silver layer. At enterprise scale, small files become a systemic problem that affects every consumer, every pipeline, and the cloud storage bill. GridUnion's Silver meter readings table, with 4 countries × 1,095 dates × 8 files per directory = 35,040 files for the initial write, seemed manageable. But the daily batch pipeline appends 4 new files per country per day (one per Spark partition), and the streaming pipeline appends 96 files per country per day (one per 15-minute trigger interval, 4 partitions each). After 90 days without compaction, the file count grew to 35,040 + (90 × 4 × 4) + (90 × 96 × 4) = 35,040 + 1,440 + 34,560 = 71,040 files. The listing latency on ADLS increased from 200ms to 4 seconds. Every pipeline that read the table spent 4 seconds on listing before reading a single byte of data.
The Cost of Small Files on Cloud Storage

Cloud object stores (S3, ADLS, GCS) are designed for large objects accessed sequentially, not for millions of small objects accessed through directory listings. Each LIST request returns up to 5,000 objects and takes 50–200ms. A directory with 100,000 files requires 20 LIST requests at 100ms each = 2 seconds of listing latency before any data is read. Each GET request for a small file (1–5MB) has a fixed overhead of 10–50ms for the HTTP round trip, which dominates the actual data transfer time for small files. And each file has a minimum billing unit: S3 charges for a minimum of 128KB per GET request, so a 1KB file costs the same as a 128KB file. GridUnion's cost analysis showed that small-file metadata overhead (listing + GET latency) accounted for 22% of the Silver table's read latency, while the actual data transfer accounted for only 18%. The remaining 60% was Spark processing. Compaction to the target file size (256MB) reduced the metadata overhead to 3%.
OPTIMIZE Strategies

Delta Lake's OPTIMIZE command offers two compaction strategies: bin-packing (the default) and Z-ORDER. Bin-packing merges small files into larger files without changing the data order. It is fast, deterministic, and produces files of approximately the target size (spark.databricks.delta.optimize.maxFileSize, default 1GB on Databricks, 256MB in open-source Delta). Z-ORDER compaction additionally sorts the data by the Z-ORDER columns, producing files with narrow min/max ranges for the specified columns. Z-ORDER compaction is more expensive (it must read, sort, and rewrite all data in the affected partitions) but provides the skipping benefit from lesson 3. GridUnion's compaction policy: daily bin-pack compaction for partitions that received new data in the last 24 hours (fast, addresses the streaming pipeline's small files), and weekly Z-ORDER compaction for the entire table (slower, refreshes the Z-ORDER clustering that daily bin-packing disrupts).
GridUnion’s Automated Maintenance Framework

from delta.tables import DeltaTable
from datetime import date, timedelta
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger('compaction')

def run_daily_compaction(spark, table_path, lookback_days=1):
    """Daily bin-pack compaction for recently modified partitions.
    
    Targets partitions that received data in the last N days.
    Fast: bin-packing only, no Z-ORDER rewrite.
    """
    dt = DeltaTable.forPath(spark, table_path)
    
    for day_offset in range(lookback_days):
        target_date = str(date.today() - timedelta(days=day_offset))
        for country in ['DE', 'FR', 'NL', 'LU']:
            partition_filter = f"country = '{country}' AND date = '{target_date}'"
            logger.info(f"Compacting: {partition_filter}")
            dt.optimize().where(partition_filter).executeCompaction()

def run_weekly_zorder(spark, table_path, zorder_columns=['region']):
    """Weekly Z-ORDER compaction for the entire table.
    
    Rewrites all data with Z-ORDER clustering.
    Expensive but refreshes the clustering degraded by daily bin-packing.
    Scheduled Saturday 2 AM when cluster load is lowest.
    """
    dt = DeltaTable.forPath(spark, table_path)
    logger.info(f"Starting weekly Z-ORDER on {zorder_columns}")
    dt.optimize().executeZOrderBy(*zorder_columns)
    logger.info("Weekly Z-ORDER complete")

def run_vacuum(spark, table_path, retention_hours=720):
    """Remove files older than retention period.
    
    GridUnion retention: 720 hours (30 days) for Silver, 2160 hours (90 days) for Gold.
    WARNING: Irreversible. Time travel past the retention window fails after VACUUM.
    """
    dt = DeltaTable.forPath(spark, table_path)
    logger.info(f"Vacuuming with {retention_hours}h retention")
    dt.vacuum(retention_hours)

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Compaction is not a one-time cleanup — it is a continuous operational process that must be scheduled, monitored, and budgeted. GridUnion's daily compaction takes 5–10 minutes per table and consumes cluster resources. The weekly Z-ORDER takes 30–60 minutes for the 8TB Silver table. The VACUUM takes 2–5 minutes. These costs are small relative to the read performance benefit (22% latency reduction) but they are recurring and must be included in the cluster capacity plan. A platform that designs a perfect partition scheme but neglects compaction will degrade within weeks as small files accumulate from streaming and append workloads.

The mid-level track introduced bucketing as a write-time strategy that pre-partitions data by a key column, enabling shuffle-free joins. At enterprise scale, bucketing becomes an architectural decision that affects multiple tables, multiple teams, and the entire join topology. When GridUnion buckets the Silver meter readings table by meter_id with 512 buckets, every downstream table that joins on meter_id can benefit from shuffle-free joins — but only if it is also bucketed with the same column and the same bucket count. This cross-table coordination requirement is bucketing's greatest strength (shuffle elimination across the entire join chain) and its greatest weakness (a single table with a different bucket count breaks the alignment for every downstream join). This lesson covers the enterprise concerns: bucket count selection for 8TB tables, bucket evolution as data grows, cross-table alignment for multi-hop joins, and GridUnion's bucket inheritance pattern.
Bucket Count Selection at Scale

The bucket count determines the number of files per partition directory and the size of each bucket file. For GridUnion's Silver meter readings table (8TB, 4,380 partition directories, ~1.8GB per directory), a bucket count of 512 produces 512 files per directory at ~3.6MB each — far too small (small file problem). A bucket count of 8 produces 8 files per directory at ~230MB each — within the 128–512MB target. The bucket count must be a power of 2 (for even hash distribution) and must produce files within the target size range. The formula: bucket_count = max(1, round_to_power_of_2(avg_partition_size_mb / target_file_size_mb)). For GridUnion: 1,800MB / 256MB ≈ 7, rounded to 8. But the bucket count must also consider the cluster's parallelism: 8 buckets on a 32-core cluster means each task processes 4 bucket pairs in the join — acceptable but not maximally parallel. GridUnion chose 16 buckets as a compromise between file size (113MB per file, slightly below the 128MB minimum) and parallelism.
Cross-Table Bucket Alignment

A shuffle-free bucketed join requires both sides to have the same bucket column and the same bucket count. GridUnion's daily enrichment pipeline joins Silver readings (bucketed by meter_id, 16 buckets) with the meter metadata table (also bucketed by meter_id, 16 buckets). The shuffle-free join works perfectly. But the SCADA telemetry table was bucketed by meter_id with 32 buckets (chosen independently by the SCADA team before the bucketing standard was established). Joining Silver readings (16 buckets) with SCADA telemetry (32 buckets) triggers a shuffle despite both tables being bucketed on the same column — because the bucket counts differ. The fix required the SCADA team to rewrite their table with 16 buckets, coordinating with the platform team to schedule the rewrite during a maintenance window. This incident prompted GridUnion to establish the bucket registry: a catalog table that records the bucket column and count for every bucketed table, with a CI check that validates bucket count consistency before a new bucketed table is created.
The Bucket Registry

# GridUnion's bucket registry — ensures cross-table alignment
# Stored in gridunion_prod.monitoring.bucket_registry

BUCKET_REGISTRY_SCHEMA = StructType([
    StructField('catalog',        StringType(), False),
    StructField('schema',         StringType(), False),
    StructField('table_name',     StringType(), False),
    StructField('bucket_column',  StringType(), False),
    StructField('bucket_count',   IntegerType(), False),
    StructField('sort_columns',   StringType(), True),   # JSON array
    StructField('registered_at',  TimestampType(), False),
])

def validate_bucket_alignment(spark, new_table_fqn, bucket_column, bucket_count):
    """Validate that a new bucketed table aligns with existing bucketed tables.
    
    Called by CI before CREATE TABLE with bucketBy().
    Returns violations if the bucket count conflicts with existing tables
    that share the same bucket column.
    """
    registry = spark.read.format('delta').load(
        'gridunion_prod.monitoring.bucket_registry'
    )
    
    conflicts = (registry
        .filter(col('bucket_column') == bucket_column)
        .filter(col('bucket_count') != bucket_count)
        .collect()
    )
    
    if conflicts:
        conflict_tables = [f"{r['catalog']}.{r['schema']}.{r['table_name']} "
                          f"(count={r['bucket_count']})" for r in conflicts]
        return [f"Bucket count {bucket_count} for {new_table_fqn} conflicts with: "
                f"{', '.join(conflict_tables)}. "
                f"All tables bucketed on '{bucket_column}' must use the same count."]
    return []  # no conflicts

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Bucketing is an infrastructure decision, not a pipeline decision. Once a bucket column and count are established for a key (meter_id: 16 buckets), every table in the join chain must conform. This makes bucketing a platform-level standard, enforced by the bucket registry and validated in CI, not a per-pipeline optimization that individual teams apply independently. GridUnion's bucketing standard: meter_id uses 16 buckets, turbine_id uses 8 buckets, and any new bucket column requires governance committee approval to ensure cross-table alignment before the first table is written.

Partition schemes are designed for a specific set of query patterns, data volumes, and consumer requirements. All three change over time. A new regulatory requirement adds a country column that must be a partition key. A data volume increase makes an existing partition granularity too coarse (daily partitions become too large and need to be split into hourly). A consumer team is decommissioned and its query pattern no longer needs to be optimized for. Each of these scenarios requires evolving the partition scheme on a live table without downtime, without data loss, and without breaking the 12 downstream consumers. This lesson covers the three evolution scenarios — adding a partition level, splitting a partition column, and removing a partition level — with consumer impact analysis for each.
Scenario 1: Adding a Partition Level

GridUnion's original Silver table was partitioned by date only. The regulatory requirement to isolate country data added country as a first-level partition column, changing the scheme from date to country/date. Adding a partition level changes the directory structure: every existing directory (date=2026-03-01/) must be reorganized into country subdirectories (country=DE/date=2026-03-01/). All 12 consumers must update their queries to include the new partition column in their filters (or accept a full scan if they do not filter by country). The consumer impact is moderate: queries that already filter by country gain a performance improvement (partition pruning), queries that do not filter by country see no performance change (they scan all country partitions, same data volume), and queries that use direct file paths break (the paths have changed). This is the scenario covered in lesson 8 (Repartitioning a Live Table) and is the most common partition evolution at GridUnion.
Scenario 2: Splitting a Partition Column

When daily partitions grow too large (>5GB per partition, causing spill during joins that process single-day partitions), the partition granularity can be refined from date to date and hour, or from date to year/month/day. This is less common than adding a partition level but more disruptive: every existing file path contains date=2026-03-01, and the new scheme has year=2026/month=03/day=01. The consumer impact is high: every query that references the date partition column must be rewritten to reference the new columns. GridUnion has not needed this evolution because the daily data volume (2GB per country per day) is within the target range. The sizing model (lesson 2) projects that this will remain the case for 3+ years.
Scenario 3: Removing a Partition Level

Removing a partition level collapses a directory level: country/date becomes date only (removing the country partition). This is the rarest scenario and occurs when the partition column's value becomes obsolete (a country is divested) or when the partition causes more overhead than benefit (the directory count exceeds limits). The consumer impact is moderate: queries that filtered on the removed column must use data-level filters instead of partition filters (losing partition pruning but still producing correct results). GridUnion has a documented process for this but has not executed it — the scenario is included in the partition governance framework (lesson 10) as a contingency.
Consumer Impact Analysis

rows: Add partition level
New subdirectory level added
Queries gain partition pruning on new column. No queries break unless they use direct file paths.
No, unless the consumer uses direct paths. Catalog-based queries work unchanged.
Split partition column
Existing partition column replaced by finer-grained columns
All queries referencing the old partition column must be updated to the new columns.
Yes — every query with a partition filter must be updated.
Remove partition level
A directory level is collapsed
Queries that filtered on the removed column lose partition pruning. Results are still correct.
No — but performance degrades for queries that relied on the removed column.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Partition evolution is always more expensive than getting the partition scheme right initially. Adding a partition level requires repartitioning 8TB of data (lesson 8). Splitting a partition column requires every consumer to update their queries. Removing a partition level degrades performance for some consumers. The sizing model from lesson 2, combined with the consumer survey from lesson 1, reduces the probability of needing an evolution by designing for the 2-year projected state rather than the current state. When evolution is unavoidable, the impact analysis framework above determines which consumers are affected and which migration approach (lesson 8) minimizes the disruption.

GridUnion Continental's Silver meter readings table was originally partitioned by date only. The table holds 8TB of historical data spanning 3 years. Twelve downstream pipelines consume it daily — the billing aggregation, the regulatory export, four regional dashboards, the anomaly detection model, the forecasting model, and four team-specific analytical notebooks. Forty Power BI reports refresh against its SQL endpoint. The table processes 500,000 new rows per day from 30 regions across 4 countries.
The Regulatory Driver

After the acquisition of three subsidiaries, the EU regulatory team requires country-level data isolation. The German regulator (BNetzA) demands that German meter data be physically separable from French, Dutch, and Luxembourg data within the storage layer — not just logically filterable, but stored in distinct directory paths that can be independently audited, backed up, and access-controlled. The current date-only partition scheme stores all countries' data in the same partition folders. The partition strategy must change to country/date. This is not optional — the regulatory deadline is 90 days away, and non-compliance carries financial penalties.

Repartitioning an 8TB table that 12 pipelines and 40 reports depend on, with zero downtime, under a 90-day deadline, is the most stressful data engineering task you will face. It is also one of the most common — every mature data platform eventually needs to change a partition scheme. The difference between a smooth migration and a disaster is planning. This lesson covers three migration approaches, their trade-offs, the decision framework for choosing between them, and the operational playbook for executing each one safely.
The Three Migration Approaches

There are three approaches to repartitioning a live table, each with fundamentally different trade-offs between downtime risk, implementation complexity, storage cost, and migration duration. The choice depends on whether you are using Delta Lake, how much historical data exists, how many consumers must be coordinated, and how much tolerance there is for temporary storage cost increases. All three approaches have been battle-tested at GridUnion scale — none is theoretical.
Approach 1: Dual-Write Migration

The dual-write approach creates a new table with the target partition scheme alongside the existing one. The write pipeline is modified to write every batch to both tables simultaneously — the old table partitioned by date, the new table partitioned by country/date. Both tables receive identical data from the moment the dual write begins. Consumers are migrated from the old table to the new table one at a time, on their own schedules. Once all consumers have switched, the old table's historical data is backfilled into the new table, the old table is decommissioned, and the dual-write code is removed.

The dual-write approach is the safest option because it introduces no downtime at any point in the migration. The old table continues to work exactly as before — consumers that have not migrated yet are completely unaffected. If a problem is discovered with the new table (wrong partition scheme, performance regression, schema mismatch), you can pause the migration and roll back by simply deleting the new table and removing the dual-write code. The old table was never touched. This safety comes at a cost: you are writing every batch twice and storing the data twice for the duration of the migration, which at GridUnion's scale means an additional 8TB of storage and approximately 2x the write pipeline duration.

# Dual-write pipeline modification
# Before: write to one table
def write_daily_batch(batch_df, batch_date):
    """Original pipeline — writes to date-partitioned table only."""
    batch_df.write \
        .mode('append') \
        .partitionBy('date') \
        .parquet('silver/meter_readings/')

# After: write to both tables simultaneously
def write_daily_batch_dual(batch_df, batch_date):
    """Migration pipeline — writes to both old and new tables.
    
    The batch_df must contain the 'country' column, derived from region.
    Both writes must succeed for the batch to be considered complete.
    If either write fails, the entire batch is retried.
    """
    # Derive country from region if not already present
    if 'country' not in batch_df.columns:
        batch_df = batch_df.withColumn('country',
            when(col('region').startswith('DE-'), lit('DE'))
            .when(col('region').startswith('FR-'), lit('FR'))
            .when(col('region').startswith('NL-'), lit('NL'))
            .when(col('region').startswith('LU-'), lit('LU'))
            .otherwise(lit('UNKNOWN'))
        )
    
    # Cache the batch to avoid recomputation for the second write
    batch_df.cache()
    batch_count = batch_df.count()  # materialize the cache
    
    # Write 1: Old table (existing partition scheme)
    batch_df.write \
        .mode('append') \
        .partitionBy('date') \
        .parquet('silver/meter_readings/')
    
    # Write 2: New table (target partition scheme)
    batch_df.write \
        .mode('append') \
        .partitionBy('country', 'date') \
        .parquet('silver/meter_readings_v2/')
    
    batch_df.unpersist()  # release the cache
    
    print(f"Dual write complete: {batch_count:,} rows to both tables for {batch_date}")

The cache() call is critical. Without it, Spark would recompute the batch DataFrame from source for each write, doubling not just the write cost but the read and transformation cost. With cache, the batch is computed once and written twice from memory. The unpersist() at the end releases the memory — important in a pipeline that runs daily, because cached DataFrames accumulate across runs if not explicitly released.
Approach 2: View Abstraction

The view abstraction approach works when all consumers read through a catalog table name or a SQL view rather than a direct file path. Instead of writing to two physical tables, you create a single logical view that UNION ALLs data from two physical locations: new data written with the target partition scheme, and historical data still in the old partition scheme. As the historical data is gradually backfilled into the new scheme, the view shifts — the proportion from the new location grows while the proportion from the old location shrinks. When the backfill is complete, the view points entirely to the new location, and the old data is deleted.

This approach has a strict prerequisite: every consumer must read through the view name, not a hardcoded file path. If any pipeline reads from 'silver/meter_readings/' directly (bypassing the catalog), it will miss the data in the new location. This is why platform standards at GridUnion mandate catalog-based access for all shared tables. If your platform does not enforce this, the view abstraction approach is risky because you cannot guarantee all consumers will see the migrated data.

# Step 1: Start writing new data to the new partition scheme
# (from today forward, all new batches go to the v2 location)
def write_daily_batch_v2(batch_df, batch_date):
    batch_df.write \
        .mode('append') \
        .partitionBy('country', 'date') \
        .parquet('silver/meter_readings_v2/')

# Step 2: Create a UNION ALL view that combines both locations
spark.sql("""
    CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW silver.meter_readings_migrating AS
    
    -- New data: country/date partitioned (today forward)
    SELECT meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, country
    FROM parquet.`silver/meter_readings_v2/`
    
    UNION ALL
    
    -- Historical data: date-only partitioned (being backfilled gradually)
    -- Derive country from region for schema compatibility
    SELECT 
        meter_id, daily_kw, date, region,
        CASE
            WHEN region LIKE 'DE-%' THEN 'DE'
            WHEN region LIKE 'FR-%' THEN 'FR'
            WHEN region LIKE 'NL-%' THEN 'NL'
            WHEN region LIKE 'LU-%' THEN 'LU'
        END AS country
    FROM parquet.`silver/meter_readings/`
    WHERE date < '2026-03-01'  -- only historical dates, shrinks as backfill progresses
""")

# Step 3: Point the canonical table name to the migration view
spark.sql("""
    -- Consumers read from silver.meter_readings
    -- During migration, this is an alias for the UNION view
    CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW silver.meter_readings AS
    SELECT * FROM silver.meter_readings_migrating
""")

# Step 4: Backfill historical data in batches (run as a nightly job)
def backfill_month(year, month):
    """Backfill one month of historical data from old to new partition scheme."""
    historical = spark.read.parquet('silver/meter_readings/') \
        .filter(f"year(date) = {year} AND month(date) = {month}")
    
    historical = historical.withColumn('country',
        when(col('region').startswith('DE-'), lit('DE'))
        .when(col('region').startswith('FR-'), lit('FR'))
        .when(col('region').startswith('NL-'), lit('NL'))
        .when(col('region').startswith('LU-'), lit('LU'))
    )
    
    historical.write \
        .mode('append') \
        .partitionBy('country', 'date') \
        .parquet('silver/meter_readings_v2/')
    
    print(f"Backfilled {year}-{month:02d}: {historical.count():,} rows")

# Step 5: After all months are backfilled, update the view to point only to v2
# Step 6: Delete the old data and rename v2 to the canonical path

The view abstraction approach is more complex to implement than dual-write but has a significant advantage: consumers do not need to change their code or switch table names. They read from silver.meter_readings throughout the migration — the view transparently routes to the right physical data. The gradual backfill can proceed at whatever pace is comfortable: one month per night over 36 nights for 3 years of history, or one year per night over 3 nights if the cluster has spare capacity. The WHERE clause in the UNION ALL view shrinks as each month is backfilled, until eventually all data is in the v2 location and the old location's contribution is empty.
Approach 3: Delta Lake CRAS

If the table uses Delta Lake format (and at GridUnion's scale, it should), the simplest approach is CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE AS SELECT (CRAS). This rewrites the entire table — all 8TB — in a single atomic operation with the new partition scheme. Delta's MVCC (multi-version concurrency control) ensures that all readers see the pre-migration snapshot until the CRAS operation commits. The moment it commits, all readers instantly see the new partition scheme. There is no dual-write period, no UNION view, no gradual backfill. One command, one atomic switch.

The trade-off is time. Rewriting 8TB of Parquet data takes 4-8 hours on GridUnion's standard cluster configuration (20 executors, 8 cores each, 32GB memory each). During the rewrite, the cluster is heavily loaded — other jobs competing for resources may slow down. The rewrite also temporarily doubles storage because Delta retains the old files until VACUUM runs (default retention: 7 days). At 8TB, that means 16TB of storage for a week. These costs are significant but predictable, and the operational simplicity of a single atomic command often outweighs them.

# Delta Lake CRAS — atomic repartitioning in one command
# WARNING: This rewrites ALL 8TB of data. Budget 4-8 hours.
# Schedule during the weekend maintenance window.

spark.sql("""
    CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE silver.meter_readings
    USING DELTA
    PARTITIONED BY (country, date)
    TBLPROPERTIES (
        'delta.autoOptimize.optimizeWrite' = 'true',
        'delta.autoOptimize.autoCompact' = 'true',
        'delta.tuneFileSizesForRewrites' = 'true'
    )
    AS SELECT
        meter_id,
        daily_kw,
        date,
        region,
        CASE
            WHEN region LIKE 'DE-%' THEN 'DE'
            WHEN region LIKE 'FR-%' THEN 'FR'
            WHEN region LIKE 'NL-%' THEN 'NL'
            WHEN region LIKE 'LU-%' THEN 'LU'
            ELSE 'UNKNOWN'
        END AS country,
        customer_type,
        substation_id,
        ingestion_timestamp
    FROM silver.meter_readings
""")

# After CRAS commits (atomically):
# - All readers immediately see the new country/date partition scheme
# - Old files retained for time travel (VACUUM after 7 days)
# - Transaction log records the schema and partition change
# - No consumer code changes needed — same table name, same columns
# - The country column is now a partition column, enabling partition pruning
#   for all country-filtered queries

# Run OPTIMIZE on the new partitions to ensure file sizes are optimal
spark.sql("""
    OPTIMIZE silver.meter_readings
    ZORDER BY (meter_id)
""")

# After 7 days, reclaim the old file storage
spark.sql("VACUUM silver.meter_readings RETAIN 168 HOURS")
The Decision Framework

The three approaches are not interchangeable — each fits a specific combination of constraints. The decision depends on five factors: whether you use Delta Lake, how many consumers exist and whether they use catalog names, how much temporary storage cost is acceptable, how long the migration window can last, and whether you need the ability to roll back without data loss.

rows: Downtime
Zero at all times
Zero at all times
Zero — Delta MVCC ensures readers see consistent state
Duration
2-6 weeks (consumer-by-consumer migration)
1-4 weeks (backfill pace determines duration)
4-8 hours (rewrite time for 8TB)
Temporary storage cost
2x for entire migration period (weeks)
1.5x during backfill (declining as old data is removed)
2x for 7 days (until VACUUM)
Implementation complexity
High — dual-write logic, cache management, consumer coordination
Medium — view SQL, backfill job, WHERE clause management
Low — one SQL command plus OPTIMIZE and VACUUM
Rollback capability
Trivial — delete new table, remove dual-write code
Moderate — drop view, point consumers back to old table
Built-in — Delta time travel to pre-migration state
Requires Delta Lake
No — works with any format
No — works with any format and a SQL catalog
Yes — Delta CRAS is a Delta-specific operation
Consumer code changes
Yes — each consumer must switch table name or path
No — view abstracts the change
No — same table name, same columns
Works at 100TB+
Yes, but 2x storage is expensive
Yes, backfill can be throttled to available capacity
Challenging — 100TB rewrite may take 40+ hours and require dedicated cluster

blocks.content: GridUnion's Decision

For GridUnion's specific situation — 8TB Delta table, 12 downstream pipelines reading through catalog names, 90-day regulatory deadline, and a weekend maintenance window available — the Delta CRAS approach was selected. The reasoning: 8TB rewrites in 6 hours on the standard cluster, fitting comfortably within the Saturday maintenance window. The 90-day deadline leaves no room for the 2-6 week consumer migration that dual-write requires. All consumers read through the silver.meter_readings catalog name, so no code changes are needed. The temporary 16TB storage cost for 7 days was approved by the platform budget owner. And Delta's time travel provides an automatic rollback path — if the CRAS produces incorrect results, a RESTORE command reverts the table to its pre-migration state.

The decision would have been different under different constraints. If the table were raw Parquet instead of Delta, CRAS would not be available and the dual-write approach would be the safest choice. If some consumers hardcoded the file path (bypassing the catalog), the view abstraction approach would fail for those consumers and dual-write with explicit consumer migration would be necessary. If the table were 100TB instead of 8TB, even CRAS might require a dedicated rewrite cluster and a multi-day execution window. Architecture decisions are always context-dependent — the framework above helps you evaluate your specific constraints rather than blindly applying a pattern.
The Operational Playbook

Regardless of which approach you choose, the operational execution follows the same disciplined sequence. This playbook has been refined through four partition migrations at GridUnion over the past 18 months. Each step has a specific owner, a verification criterion, and a rollback action.

items: Pre-migration audit (Week 1): Inventory all consumers of the table — pipelines, reports, notebooks, API endpoints. For each consumer, determine whether it reads through the catalog name or a direct path. Identify the consumer owners. Document the current partition scheme, data volume, and partition sizes. Model the target partition scheme using the partition sizing formula from lesson 2. Obtain budget approval for temporary storage costs.

Communication (Week 2): Notify all consumer owners of the migration timeline, the expected impact (zero downtime for CRAS/view, code change required for dual-write), and the rollback plan. Schedule the maintenance window. Notify the on-call rotation that the migration will occur during their shift.

Dry run (Week 3): Execute the migration in the staging environment using a 1TB sample of production data. Verify that all downstream pipelines produce identical results against the new partition scheme. Measure the rewrite duration and resource consumption. Identify and resolve any issues before touching production.

Production execution (Week 4): Execute the chosen migration approach during the maintenance window. Monitor the Spark UI throughout. Verify the new partition scheme with a series of validation queries: row count matches, sample checksums match, partition pruning works correctly for country-filtered queries.

Post-migration validation (Week 4-5): Run every downstream pipeline against the new table. Compare outputs to the previous day's outputs. Verify that Power BI reports refresh correctly. Confirm with the regulatory team that the country-level isolation meets their requirements. Monitor pipeline runtimes for one full week to catch any performance regressions.

Cleanup (Week 6): Run VACUUM to reclaim old file storage. Remove any temporary dual-write code or migration views. Update documentation to reflect the new partition scheme. Close the migration ticket.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: The best repartitioning strategy is the one you never need to execute. Invest the time upfront — during initial table design — to model partition strategies for projected data volumes 2-3 years out. Run the partition sizing formula against current and future data. Consult downstream consumers about their query patterns before writing the first row. A 30-minute design review prevents a 6-week migration project. And when a repartitioning is unavoidable, the operational playbook above reduces it from a crisis to a routine maintenance task — planned, communicated, dry-run tested, and executed with confidence.
What the Migration Does NOT Change

A common misconception is that repartitioning changes everything downstream. In reality, a partition scheme change is a physical storage reorganization — it changes how files are laid out on disk, not what data they contain. Column names, data types, row contents, and the logical schema are all identical before and after the migration. Queries that worked before will work after. The only behavioral difference is performance: queries that filter on the new partition column (country) will be faster because Spark can skip irrelevant partitions at the filesystem level. Queries that do not filter on country will see no performance change — they still scan the same data, just organized differently on disk. This is important to communicate to consumer teams during the migration: the data is identical, only the physical layout changed, and the performance impact is either neutral or positive.

Partition design and compaction are not one-time activities — they require continuous monitoring. A well-designed partition scheme degrades over time: small files accumulate from streaming appends, Z-ORDER clustering degrades as new data is bin-packed without re-ordering, partition skew develops as data distribution shifts, and the directory count grows as new date partitions are added daily. Without monitoring, these degradations accumulate silently until a consumer's query latency exceeds its SLA and the on-call engineer discovers that the Silver table has 500,000 files instead of the expected 60,000. GridUnion's partition health dashboard tracks five metrics per table and generates alerts when any metric exceeds its threshold.
