The dashboard is the operational complement to the sizing model from lesson 2: the model predicts future state, and the dashboard verifies that reality matches the prediction.
The Five Partition Health Metrics

rows: Total file count
COUNT(DISTINCT file) from Delta log
< 100,000 for the table
> 200,000: listing overhead becomes significant on cloud storage
Average file size
Total bytes / file count from Delta log
< 64MB: small files accumulate; compaction is failing or insufficient
Maximum partition size
MAX(partition_bytes) across all partitions
< 5GB per partition
> 10GB: individual partitions too large; consider finer-grained partitioning
Partition count
COUNT(DISTINCT partition_values) from Delta log
< 50,000 per table
> 80,000: approaching cloud listing limits; evaluate partition scheme
Partition skew ratio
MAX(partition_bytes) / MEDIAN(partition_bytes)
> 5x: data is unevenly distributed; Z-ORDER or partition redesign needed

blocks.content: The Partition Health Dashboard

from delta.tables import DeltaTable
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, count, sum as spark_sum, max as spark_max, \
    percentile_approx, input_file_name, length

def collect_partition_health(spark, table_path, table_name):
    """Collect partition health metrics for a Delta table.
    
    Writes results to the monitoring.partition_health Delta table.
    """
    df = spark.read.format('delta').load(table_path)
    
    # File-level metrics
    file_stats = (df
        .select(input_file_name().alias('file'))
        .distinct()
        .agg(
            count('file').alias('total_files'),
        )
        .collect()[0]
    )
    
    # Get file sizes from Delta log
    dt = DeltaTable.forPath(spark, table_path)
    detail = spark.sql(f"DESCRIBE DETAIL delta.`{table_path}`").collect()[0]
    total_bytes = detail['sizeInBytes']
    num_files = detail['numFiles']
    avg_file_mb = (total_bytes / num_files / 1024 / 1024) if num_files > 0 else 0
    
    metrics = {
        'table_name': table_name,
        'total_files': num_files,
        'total_size_gb': round(total_bytes / 1024**3, 1),
        'avg_file_size_mb': round(avg_file_mb, 0),
    }
    
    # Log and alert
    if num_files > 200_000:
        send_alert(f"File count alert: {table_name} has {num_files:,} files")
    if avg_file_mb < 64:
        send_alert(f"Small file alert: {table_name} avg file size is {avg_file_mb:.0f}MB")
    
    log_partition_metrics(spark, metrics)
    return metrics

The partition health dashboard aggregates metrics across all 40+ Delta tables on the GridUnion platform. The dashboard collection job runs every 6 hours (more frequently than the daily compaction but less frequently than the streaming pipeline) and writes results to monitoring.partition_health. A weekly summary report highlights tables whose metrics are trending toward alert thresholds: 'Silver DE meter readings: file count increased 12% this week, now at 68,000. Current trajectory hits 200,000 threshold in 11 months.' This trend detection gives the platform team months of lead time to schedule compaction adjustments or partition scheme reviews, converting potential incidents into planned maintenance tasks.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Partition monitoring is the feedback loop that validates partition design decisions. The sizing model (lesson 2) predicts the trajectory. The monitoring dashboard measures reality. When reality diverges from prediction — file count growing faster than modeled, partition skew developing due to data distribution shift, average file size shrinking because the compaction job failed silently — the monitoring generates the alert that triggers the response. A partition scheme without monitoring is a design without feedback: it works until it does not, and nobody knows when the transition happened.

This lesson synthesizes the module into GridUnion's partition governance framework: the process that every new table goes through before its first row is written, the ongoing health monitoring that detects degradation, the escalation path when partition problems are detected, and the quarterly audit that reviews the entire platform's partition health. The framework exists because partition decisions are expensive to reverse (lesson 8) and invisible when wrong (a suboptimal partition scheme causes slow queries but no errors). Without governance, each team makes independent partition decisions — some good, some bad — and the bad decisions are discovered only when a consumer's SLA is breached. The governance framework shifts partition design from reactive (fix after the incident) to proactive (design correctly, monitor continuously, revise before the incident).
The Partition Design Review Process

items: Consumer survey (lesson 1): The table owner surveys all registered and anticipated consumers. The survey collects filter columns, selectivity, and frequency. The results are documented in the table's data product manifest.

Partition column scoring (lesson 1): The candidate partition columns are scored using the scoring matrix. The top-scoring columns are selected, with cardinality and directory count constraints applied.
Sizing model projection (lesson 2): The sizing model is run for the selected partition scheme with a 2-year planning horizon. All metrics must be within healthy ranges. Growth triggers are defined.
Z-ORDER evaluation (lesson 3): If any consumer filters on a column that is not a partition column, Z-ORDER on that column is evaluated. The skip percentage is estimated or measured on sample data.
Bucket alignment check (lesson 6): If the table will participate in bucketed joins, the bucket column and count are validated against the bucket registry.

Architecture Decision Record: The partition decision, the alternatives considered, the sizing model output, and the growth triggers are documented in an ADR. The ADR is reviewed by the governance committee.
Post-deployment monitoring (lesson 9): After the first data write, the partition health dashboard is configured with the table's expected metrics and alert thresholds.

blocks.content: The Quarterly Partition Audit

Every quarter, the platform team reviews the partition health of all tables on the platform. The review follows the partition health dashboard's weekly summary report, focusing on tables where any metric has trended toward its alert threshold. For each flagged table, the team re-runs the sizing model with updated data volume projections and compares the results against the original ADR's predictions. If the model shows that a growth trigger will fire within the next two quarters, the team schedules a partition scheme review with the table owner and the consumer teams. The quarterly audit also checks for orphaned tables (tables with no registered consumers whose partition maintenance is being performed for no benefit) and for tables that were created without going through the design review process (shadow tables created by individual engineers without governance approval). The audit typically flags 2–3 tables for review and identifies 1–2 shadow tables per quarter.
What Comes Next

Modules 1–3 form the scaling patterns phase of the senior-level track. You can now design platform architectures for multi-team data engineering (Module 1), diagnose optimizer behavior at the implementation level (Module 2), and design, implement, and govern partition strategies for multi-terabyte tables with many consumers (this module). The three modules together address the structural decisions that determine whether a 50-pipeline platform operates smoothly or collapses under organizational and technical complexity. Module 4 shifts to the extensibility phase with advanced join architecture: the join topology analysis, bucketed joins at enterprise scale, range joins for SCD lookups, join elimination, and the multi-hop join optimization strategies that determine whether a 5-join pipeline takes 10 minutes or 10 hours.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Partition governance is not bureaucracy — it is the cheapest form of partition optimization. A 30-minute design review prevents a 6-week repartitioning project. A 2-hour quarterly audit prevents the slow degradation that turns a healthy table into a performance crisis. The governance framework makes the design review and the audit routine: scheduled, templated, and lightweight. The alternative — no governance, letting each team make independent decisions — works until the platform has 40+ tables and the interactions between partition schemes, bucketing standards, and consumer query patterns become too complex for any single team to reason about. Governance scales the partition design capability of the platform team across 200+ tables without requiring the platform team to design every partition scheme themselves.

chapters.title: Advanced Join Architecture
chapters.description: Design join strategies for multi-terabyte pipelines with complex topologies — bucketed joins, range joins, join elimination, semi-join pushdown, and the architectural decisions that determine whether a multi-hop join pipeline takes 10 minutes or 10 hours.

blocks.content: The mid-level track optimized individual joins: broadcast versus sort-merge, skew detection, AQE tuning. At senior scale, the unit of optimization shifts from the individual join to the join topology — the graph of tables and join relationships that forms the pipeline's data flow. GridUnion's daily enrichment pipeline joins 7 tables in sequence: Silver meter readings → meter metadata → substations → regulatory regions → pricing tiers → weather data → grid topology. Optimizing any single join in isolation misses the interactions between joins: the order in which they execute determines the intermediate data volumes, the join strategies available at each step, and the total shuffle cost. A join that is optimal in isolation may be suboptimal in context — for instance, broadcasting a small table early in the chain reduces the intermediate volume for all downstream joins, even if the broadcast itself is not the bottleneck.
Three Join Topologies

rows: Star schema
One large fact table joined to multiple small dimension tables
Broadcast all dimensions. Order does not matter because each join is independent (fact → dim).
Gold billing: fact (meter readings) joined to 5 dimension tables (meters, substations, regions, pricing, calendar)
Snowflake schema
Star schema with dimension-to-dimension joins (dimension → sub-dimension)
Broadcast dimensions. Pre-join dimensions that are always used together (flatten the snowflake). Join the flattened dimension to the fact.
Regulatory export: substations → regulatory regions → compliance rules is a dimension chain that is pre-joined into a flattened reference table
Multi-hop enrichment chain
A sequence of fact-to-fact joins where each join's output feeds the next join's input
Order matters critically. Each join's output becomes the next join's input, so reducing volume early cascades through the chain. Materialization points break the chain for statistics refresh.
Daily enrichment: readings → metadata → substations → regions → pricing → weather → grid (7 hops)

blocks.content: Why Topology Determines Strategy

In a star schema, each dimension join is independent: the fact table is joined to dimension A, then to dimension B, then to dimension C, and the order does not change the intermediate volume because each join adds columns but does not change the row count (assuming unique keys on the dimension side). The optimization is straightforward: broadcast every dimension that fits, sort-merge the rest. In a multi-hop enrichment chain, each join potentially changes the row count: a join with a filter reduces rows (fewer rows for downstream joins), while a many-to-many join increases rows (more rows for downstream joins). The order determines the intermediate volume at every step, and the CBO's join reordering (Module 2, lesson 3) optimizes this order based on estimated cardinalities. The accuracy of these estimates determines whether the reordering is beneficial or harmful, which is why the statistics propagation knowledge from Module 2 is essential for multi-hop optimization.
Mapping Your Pipeline’s Topology

# GridUnion's join topology mapper
# Extracts join relationships from a pipeline's logical plan

def map_join_topology(df):
    """Extract the join topology from a DataFrame's logical plan.
    
    Returns a list of join edges: (left_table, right_table, join_type, join_keys).
    Use this to visualize the join graph and identify optimization opportunities.
    """
    plan_str = df._jdf.queryExecution().analyzed().toString()
    
    edges = []
    for line in plan_str.split('\n'):
        line = line.strip()
        if 'Join' in line and ('Inner' in line or 'LeftOuter' in line or 'LeftSemi' in line):
            # Parse join type
            join_type = 'inner'
            if 'LeftOuter' in line: join_type = 'left_outer'
            if 'LeftSemi' in line: join_type = 'left_semi'
            
            # Extract join condition (simplified parsing)
            condition = line.split('(')[-1].rstrip(')') if '(' in line else 'unknown'
            edges.append({
                'join_type': join_type,
                'condition': condition,
                'plan_line': line,
            })
    
    print(f"Join topology: {len(edges)} joins detected")
    for i, edge in enumerate(edges):
        print(f"  Join {i+1}: {edge['join_type']} on {edge['condition']}")
    
    return edges

# Usage: map the 7-hop enrichment pipeline
df_enriched = build_enrichment_pipeline(spark, config)
edges = map_join_topology(df_enriched)
# Output:
# Join topology: 7 joins detected
#   Join 1: inner on (meter_id = meter_id)
#   Join 2: left_outer on (substation_id = substation_id)
#   Join 3: left_outer on (region = region_code)
#   Join 4: inner on (tier = tier_code AND country = country)
#   Join 5: left_outer on (region = weather_region AND date = weather_date)
#   Join 6: left_outer on (substation_id = grid_node_id)
#   Join 7: left_semi on (compliance_status = 'active')

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Before optimizing any individual join, understand the topology. A star schema needs different techniques than a multi-hop chain. The topology mapper extracts the join graph from the plan without running the query, letting you analyze the structure, identify the join strategies Catalyst chose, and plan your optimization approach. The most common mistake at senior scale is optimizing the wrong join: spending hours tuning join 5 when join 2 is the bottleneck because its output volume determines the input volume for joins 3 through 7.

Module 3, lesson 6 covered the bucketing infrastructure: bucket count selection, cross-table alignment, and the bucket registry. This lesson covers the join-side mechanics: how Spark executes a bucketed join, the conditions that enable or prevent shuffle elimination, the performance characteristics compared to sort-merge joins, and the enterprise-scale complication of maintaining bucket alignment across schema evolution, table rebuilds, and multi-team ownership. A shuffle-free bucketed join between two 4TB tables saves approximately 8TB of shuffle I/O — the single largest performance optimization available for large-table joins. But the optimization is fragile: a single misalignment (different bucket count, different sort order, or a query that prevents bucket pruning) silently degrades the bucketed join to a sort-merge join with full shuffle, and the 8TB shuffle penalty appears without any error message.
How Bucketed Joins Execute

When both sides of a join are bucketed on the join key with the same bucket count, Spark can skip the shuffle entirely. Each bucket on the left side contains only rows whose join key hashes to that bucket's ID. The corresponding bucket on the right side contains only rows with matching hash values. Spark pairs the buckets by ID and joins each pair locally on the same executor — no shuffle, no network transfer, no sort (if the buckets are also sorted). The join execution looks like N independent small joins running in parallel, where N is the bucket count. For GridUnion's meter_id-bucketed tables with 16 buckets, each bucketed join processes approximately 500MB per bucket instead of shuffling 8TB across the network. The join stage drops from 12 minutes (sort-merge with shuffle) to 45 seconds (bucketed, no shuffle).
Conditions for Shuffle Elimination

rows: Same bucket column on both sides
Hash values must match so that corresponding rows land in the same bucket pair
Check the CREATE TABLE statements for both tables' CLUSTERED BY columns
Same bucket count
Bucket ID pairing requires the same number of buckets. 16 buckets on one side and 32 on the other produces hash misalignment.
Check the bucket registry (Module 3, lesson 6) or DESCRIBE EXTENDED
Both tables are sorted within buckets on the join key
Sorted buckets enable sort-merge within each pair without an in-bucket sort step
Check SORTED BY in the CREATE TABLE. If missing, the join still works but with an in-bucket sort.
The join key matches the bucket column exactly
A join on meter_id works, but a join on UPPER(meter_id) does not because the hash is computed on the raw column value
Check the join condition in the plan. Any function wrapping the bucket column prevents bucket pruning.
spark.sql.sources.bucketing.enabled = true (default)
The master switch for bucketed join optimization
Check SparkSession configuration
spark.sql.sources.bucketing.autoBucketedScan.enabled = true (Spark 3.4+)
Auto-detection of compatible bucket schemas
Check configuration; defaults to true in Spark 3.4+

blocks.content: Maintaining Alignment During Schema Evolution

The hardest operational challenge with bucketed joins is maintaining alignment when tables evolve. Adding a new column to a bucketed table requires rewriting the table (because the Parquet files must include the new column in every bucket file). If the rebuild uses a different Spark version with a different hash function (rare but has occurred in Spark 2.x to 3.x upgrades), the bucket assignment changes and the tables are no longer aligned despite having the same bucket count and column. GridUnion prevents this by including the Spark version in the bucket registry and requiring that all bucket-aligned tables be rebuilt with the same Spark version. When Spark is upgraded, all bucketed tables are rebuilt in a coordinated weekend window. The rebuild takes 4 hours for the full set of 8 bucketed fact tables (8TB total), scheduled during the Saturday maintenance window. The cost is high but the alternative — silent shuffle degradation across every join that depends on bucket alignment — is more expensive.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Bucketed joins are the highest-reward and highest-maintenance join optimization. The reward is shuffle elimination on multi-terabyte joins. The maintenance cost is cross-table coordination, schema evolution discipline, and coordinated rebuilds during Spark upgrades. The break-even point depends on join frequency: if the bucketed tables are joined daily (GridUnion's enrichment pipeline), the shuffle savings over a year vastly exceed the maintenance cost. If the tables are joined monthly, sort-merge with AQE may be cheaper in total cost of ownership. The decision is not 'is bucketing faster?' (it always is) but 'is the maintenance cost justified by the frequency of the fast path?'

Every join in the mid-level track used equality conditions: readings.meter_id = metadata.meter_id. Range joins use inequality conditions: event_time BETWEEN start_time AND end_time. This seemingly small difference has a dramatic impact on join performance because Spark's shuffle and hash strategies are designed for equality: hash-based partitioning groups rows with the same key together, but cannot group rows whose keys fall within a range of another row's values. A range join between two 1TB tables can produce a Cartesian-like explosion if the optimizer cannot find an efficient strategy, turning a 10-minute equality join into a 10-hour range join. GridUnion encounters range joins in two critical patterns: SCD Type 2 point-in-time lookups (find the dimension record whose effective period contains the event timestamp) and time-windowed event correlation (find all grid events that occurred within 15 minutes of a meter event).
The SCD Type 2 Point-in-Time Lookup

# SCD Type 2 lookup: find the pricing tier that was active at the time of each reading
# The pricing table has effective_from and effective_to columns

# NAIVE approach: range join (works but extremely slow at scale)
df_naive = readings.join(
    pricing,
    (readings.country == pricing.country) &
    (readings.tier == pricing.tier) &
    (readings.date >= pricing.effective_from) &
    (readings.date < pricing.effective_to),
    'inner'
)
# This produces a BroadcastNestedLoopJoin or CartesianProduct in the plan
# because the range condition prevents hash-based join strategies.
# At 8TB readings × 500 pricing records: ~40 minutes

# OPTIMIZED approach: broadcast + range join
# Broadcast the small pricing table and use a UDF or conditional logic
from pyspark.sql.functions import broadcast

df_optimized = readings.join(
    broadcast(pricing),  # force broadcast: pricing is small (500 rows)
    (readings.country == pricing.country) &
    (readings.tier == pricing.tier) &
    (readings.date >= pricing.effective_from) &
    (readings.date < pricing.effective_to),
    'inner'
)
# BroadcastNestedLoopJoin with the small table broadcast: ~3 minutes
# The broadcast eliminates the shuffle; the nested loop is fast because
# the broadcast side is tiny (500 rows × 6 columns = ~50KB)
The Bin-Based Approximation

When both sides of a range join are large (neither can be broadcast), the nested loop becomes intractable. The bin-based approximation converts the range join into an equality join by discretizing the continuous range into bins. For a time-range join on event_time BETWEEN start_time AND end_time, you add a bin column (hour or day) to both sides and join on the equality condition (bin = bin) plus the range condition. The equality condition enables hash-based partitioning: all events in the same hourly bin are co-located on the same executor, and the range condition is evaluated locally within each bin. This reduces the comparison space from N × M (full cross-product) to N × (M / bin_count) per bin. The trade-off is that events near bin boundaries may match records in the adjacent bin, requiring a one-bin overlap in the join. GridUnion's event correlation pipeline uses 15-minute bins with a 1-bin overlap, reducing the correlation join from 4 hours (nested loop) to 12 minutes (binned equality join).
When Range Joins Are Unavoidable

Not every range join can be converted to an equality join. The bin-based approximation works when the range is bounded (event_time BETWEEN start AND end with known start/end) but not when the range is open-ended (all events AFTER a timestamp with no upper bound) or when the granularity of the bins exceeds the granularity of the data (1-second events in 1-hour bins produce too many comparisons per bin). In these cases, the options are: broadcast the smaller side if it fits (the GridUnion pricing lookup pattern), pre-join and materialize if the range condition is stable (compute the join once and store the result as a Delta table), or accept the nested loop cost and schedule the pipeline during off-peak hours. GridUnion's quarterly historical correlation job uses the third approach: a nested loop join between two 2TB tables that runs for 6 hours on a dedicated cluster during the Saturday maintenance window. The cost is accepted because the join runs once per quarter and the alternative (maintaining a materialized join table updated daily) would be more expensive in total engineering effort.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Range joins are where Spark's distributed architecture shows its weakest performance characteristic. Hash-based partitioning, which makes equality joins fast, cannot help with range conditions because there is no hash function that groups ranges together. Every range join strategy is a workaround: broadcast (small side), binning (convert to equality), or brute force (nested loop). The architectural decision is which workaround fits the specific join: broadcast for dimension lookups (small side), binning for event correlation (bounded ranges), and scheduled brute force for unbounded historical analysis. Trying to optimize a range join using equality-join techniques (repartition, bucketing) is the most common senior-level mistake.

Join elimination is the optimizer's ability to remove a join entirely when it can prove that the join does not change the query's result. This occurs when a table is joined but none of its columns (other than the join key) appear in the output, and the join condition guarantees a one-to-one or many-to-one relationship (no row duplication). The eliminated join produces zero shuffle cost, zero I/O for the eliminated table, and a simpler plan. Join elimination is subtle because the join is present in the code but absent in the plan — the engineer writes a 7-table join, but the plan shows only 5 tables because 2 were eliminated. Understanding when elimination activates lets you design view layers that include 'safety' joins (referential integrity checks) without incurring the cost, and diagnose cases where a join you expected to be eliminated was not.
Conditions for Elimination

Catalyst eliminates a join when three conditions are met simultaneously. First, no column from the eliminated table appears in the SELECT, WHERE, GROUP BY, or ORDER BY of the query — only the join key is referenced, and it is available from the other side of the join. Second, the join is provably non-duplicating: the join key is a primary key or unique constraint on the eliminated table, guaranteeing that each row from the surviving side matches at most one row from the eliminated side. Without this guarantee, eliminating the join could change the row count. Third, the join is an inner join or a left join where the eliminated table is on the inner side (the side that would produce nulls if no match exists, but since the join is being eliminated, the nulls would be wrong). Left joins where the eliminated table is the outer side cannot be eliminated because the join's presence guarantees that all outer rows survive.
GridUnion’s View Layer

-- GridUnion's 'wide view' joins 7 tables for maximum flexibility
-- Consumers SELECT only the columns they need
-- Catalyst eliminates joins for columns that are not selected

CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW gridunion_prod.gold_billing.enriched_readings AS
SELECT
    r.meter_id, r.daily_kw, r.date, r.region, r.country,
    m.customer_type, m.install_date, m.firmware_version,
    s.substation_name, s.capacity_kw, s.grid_zone,
    rg.regulatory_code, rg.reporting_frequency,
    p.rate_per_kwh, p.peak_multiplier,
    w.temperature, w.wind_speed, w.cloud_cover,
    g.grid_node_type, g.redundancy_level
FROM gridunion_prod.silver_de.meter_readings r
INNER JOIN gridunion_prod.silver_de.meter_metadata m ON r.meter_id = m.meter_id
INNER JOIN gridunion_prod.silver_de.substations s ON m.substation_id = s.substation_id
INNER JOIN gridunion_prod.gold_regulatory.regions rg ON r.region = rg.region_code
INNER JOIN gridunion_prod.gold_billing.pricing p ON r.tier = p.tier AND r.country = p.country
LEFT JOIN gridunion_prod.silver_de.weather w ON r.region = w.region AND r.date = w.date
LEFT JOIN gridunion_prod.silver_de.grid_topology g ON s.substation_id = g.grid_node_id;

-- Consumer A: SELECT meter_id, daily_kw, rate_per_kwh FROM enriched_readings
-- Catalyst eliminates: metadata, substations, regions, weather, grid_topology
-- Only readings and pricing are scanned (2 tables instead of 7)

-- Consumer B: SELECT meter_id, temperature, wind_speed FROM enriched_readings
-- Catalyst eliminates: metadata, substations, regions, pricing, grid_topology
-- Only readings and weather are scanned (2 tables instead of 7)

The view layer provides a single, wide interface that every consumer queries. Each consumer selects only the columns they need, and Catalyst automatically eliminates the joins for tables whose columns are not selected. Consumer A's billing query touches only 2 of 7 tables. Consumer B's weather analysis touches a different 2 of 7 tables. Neither consumer knows about the eliminated joins — they query the same view and the optimizer handles the rest. This pattern decouples the logical data model (all 7 tables joined into one wide view) from the physical execution (only the needed tables are scanned and joined). The wide view was adopted at GridUnion after a refactoring effort where 12 consumers each maintained their own join logic, resulting in subtle inconsistencies (different join types, missing tables, incorrect join conditions). The view centralizes the join logic and lets the optimizer eliminate what each consumer does not need.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Join elimination turns views from a convenience into a performance optimization. A view that joins 7 tables looks expensive, but if the optimizer eliminates 5 of them for a given query, the actual cost is a 2-table join. This means you can design views for maximum flexibility (include every possible enrichment) without paying the cost of maximum complexity (7-way join). The prerequisite is that Catalyst can prove the elimination is safe: the eliminated table's join key must be unique (primary key constraint or unique index). Without this proof, Catalyst cannot eliminate the join because it cannot rule out row duplication. If your tables lack declared constraints, Catalyst falls back to executing all joins.

Dynamic partition pruning (Module 3, lesson 4) uses the build side's output to prune partitions on the probe side. Semi-join pushdown extends this concept below the partition level: instead of pruning entire partition directories, it pushes a Bloom filter to the probe side's Parquet reader, enabling row-group skipping for non-partition columns. The Bloom filter encodes the set of join key values from the build side. The Parquet reader checks each row group's column statistics against the Bloom filter: if no value in the row group could match the filter, the entire row group is skipped. For a 6TB probe table where only 10% of the data matches the build side's keys, Bloom filter pushdown can skip 90% of the I/O at the Parquet row-group level — even when the join key is not a partition column.
How Bloom Filter Pushdown Works

Bloom filter pushdown operates in three phases. Phase 1: during the build side of a broadcast hash join, Spark collects the distinct join key values and constructs a Bloom filter — a probabilistic data structure that can answer 'is this value possibly in the set?' with no false negatives (if the filter says no, the value is definitely not in the set) and a small false positive rate (if the filter says yes, the value might not be in the set). Phase 2: the Bloom filter is serialized and sent to each executor alongside the broadcast hash table. Phase 3: the Parquet reader on the probe side evaluates each row group's min/max statistics and dictionary against the Bloom filter. If the row group's value range does not overlap with any value in the Bloom filter, the row group is skipped entirely. This combines the selectivity of a semi-join (only matching keys survive) with the I/O efficiency of predicate pushdown (row groups are skipped at the storage layer before decompression).
The DPP + Bloom Filter Interaction

DPP and Bloom filter pushdown are complementary optimizations that operate at different granularities. DPP prunes at the partition directory level (filesystem): it skips entire directories whose partition values do not appear in the build side. Bloom filter pushdown prunes at the row group level (within files): it skips individual row groups whose column statistics indicate no matching keys. When both are active, DPP eliminates irrelevant partitions first (coarse-grained), and Bloom filter pushdown eliminates irrelevant row groups within the surviving partitions (fine-grained). GridUnion's SCADA pipeline demonstrates the multiplicative benefit: DPP on the country partition prunes 75% of directories (3 of 4 countries), and Bloom filter pushdown on the turbine_model column prunes 60% of the remaining row groups (the build side selects only 'V164' turbines). Combined: 75% + (25% × 60%) = 90% of total I/O eliminated before any data is decompressed.
Configuration and Verification

# Bloom filter pushdown is controlled by the runtime filter configuration
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.optimizer.runtime.bloomFilter.enabled', 'true')  # Spark 3.3+
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.optimizer.runtime.bloomFilter.creationSideThreshold', '10MB')
# The creation side (build side) must be below this threshold for the filter to be created

# Verify Bloom filter pushdown in the plan:
df = readings.join(
    broadcast(filtered_scada.filter(col('turbine_model') == 'V164')),
    'turbine_id',
    'inner'
)
df.explain(True)
# Look for: 'BloomFilter' in the physical plan's scan operator
# Or in the SQL tab: 'RuntimeFilter' with 'BloomFilter' annotation

# Measure the I/O reduction:
# Before Bloom filter: FileScan reads 6TB (all row groups)
# After Bloom filter: FileScan reads 600GB (90% of row groups skipped)
# The SQL tab's 'number of files read' and 'size of files read' metrics
# show the actual I/O after Bloom filter application

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Bloom filter pushdown is the optimization that bridges the gap between partitioning and fine-grained data skipping. Partitioning provides I/O reduction for low-cardinality columns (country: 4 values). Z-ORDER provides I/O reduction for medium-cardinality columns (region: 30 values). Bloom filter pushdown provides I/O reduction for high-cardinality columns (turbine_id: 5,000 values) where neither partitioning nor Z-ORDER is practical. The three techniques form a layered I/O reduction strategy: partitions for coarse filtering, Z-ORDER for medium filtering, and Bloom filters for fine filtering. Designing your tables and joins to enable all three layers maximizes the I/O reduction before any data reaches the Spark execution engine.

The mid-level track treated skew as a runtime problem: detect it in the Spark UI, apply salting or AQE, and re-run. At senior scale, skew is an architectural problem that should be solved at the data model level rather than patched at the query level. GridUnion's persistent skew problem — the Berlin region containing 22% of German meter readings while 7 other German regions each contain 8–13% — was patched with AQE skew splitting for 14 months. Every pipeline that joined on region triggered AQE's skew handler, splitting Berlin's partition and replicating the other side. The replication cost (reading the same data 3x for Berlin) was paid by every join, every day, across 8 pipelines. The senior-level solution eliminated the skew at the source: a composite key redesign that replaced region with region_zone (a finer-grained key derived from region plus postal code prefix), distributing Berlin's data across 12 zones instead of 1 partition.
Four Skew Resolution Strategies

rows: AQE skew splitting (runtime)
Automatic partition splitting at runtime. No code changes.
First response. Use when skew is moderate (< 5x median) and affects few pipelines.
Default for all pipelines. Handles routine skew automatically.
Salting (query-level)
Add random suffix to skewed key, explode the other side, join, drop the salt.
When AQE is insufficient (skew > 10x median) and the skew is temporary or unpredictable.
Used for the monthly billing aggregation during the Berlin fleet expansion.
Pre-aggregation (pipeline-level)
Aggregate the skewed side before the join to reduce its volume. Join on the aggregated result.
When the downstream query does not need row-level detail from the skewed side.
Analytics team's regional summary joins pre-aggregated Silver readings instead of raw readings.
Key redesign (architectural)
Change the join key to a finer-grained key that distributes data more evenly.
When skew is persistent, affects many pipelines, and the key can be decomposed without losing semantics.
Replaced region (8 German values, Berlin = 22%) with region_zone (96 values, max zone = 3%).

blocks.content: The Key Redesign at GridUnion

# Before: join on region (8 values, Berlin = 22% of data)
df_skewed = readings.join(reference, 'region', 'inner')
# AQE splits Berlin's partition into 3 sub-partitions every run
# Replication cost: reference table read 3x for Berlin
# Daily cost across 8 pipelines: ~4 minutes of wasted compute

# After: join on region_zone (96 values, max zone = 3%)
# region_zone = region + '_' + postal_prefix (first 2 digits of postal code)
# Berlin (previously 1 partition) is split into 12 zones: BERLIN_10, BERLIN_12, ...

# Step 1: Add region_zone to Silver table (one-time migration)
df_silver = (df_silver
    .withColumn('region_zone',
        concat(col('region'), lit('_'), col('postal_code').substr(1, 2))
    )
)

# Step 2: Add region_zone to reference tables (one-time)
# Each reference row is expanded: one BERLIN row becomes 12 BERLIN_XX rows
df_reference = (df_reference
    .join(zone_mapping, 'region', 'inner')  # zone_mapping has region → [zone1, zone2, ...]
    .select('*')
)

# Step 3: All pipelines join on region_zone instead of region
df_balanced = readings.join(reference, 'region_zone', 'inner')
# No AQE intervention needed. Max partition is 3% of data.
# Daily savings across 8 pipelines: ~4 minutes + no AQE overhead

The key redesign required coordinating 8 pipelines across 3 teams, migrating the Silver table with the new column (using the dual-write approach from Module 3, lesson 8), and updating the reference tables to include the zone mapping. The migration took 3 weeks of planning and 1 weekend of execution. The payoff: 14 months of AQE skew splitting across 8 pipelines was replaced by a permanently balanced key distribution. The AQE skew handler still runs as a safety net (in case a new zone becomes disproportionately large) but has not triggered since the redesign. The composite key approach works because the business semantics of region are preserved — any query that needs region-level results aggregates across zones: GROUP BY region is replaced by GROUP BY region with a region column derived from region_zone. The derivation is a simple split on the underscore delimiter.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: The four strategies form an escalation path: AQE (automatic, zero effort) → salting (query-level, moderate effort) → pre-aggregation (pipeline-level, moderate effort) → key redesign (architectural, high effort). Each step requires more coordination and more planning but provides a more permanent solution. The mistake is to stay at the salting level for years when the skew is persistent and the cost compounds across every pipeline. The decision to escalate from runtime patches to architectural redesign is a senior-level judgment call that weighs the one-time migration cost against the ongoing per-pipeline overhead.

GridUnion's 7-hop enrichment pipeline chains joins in sequence: each join's output becomes the next join's input. The total runtime is not the sum of the individual join times — it is dominated by the intermediate data volumes, the shuffle costs, and the CBO's ability to choose the right strategy at each hop. The mid-level insight is 'reduce data early by filtering before joining.' The senior-level insight is that materialization points — places where you write an intermediate result to a Delta table and read it back — can dramatically improve the CBO's decisions by providing fresh statistics for the downstream joins. GridUnion's enrichment pipeline was originally a single DataFrame chain with 7 joins. Splitting it into two stages (joins 1–4 written to an intermediate table, then joins 5–7 reading from that table) reduced the total runtime from 22 minutes to 10 minutes — a 55% improvement — because the CBO had accurate statistics for the second stage's joins.
The Materialization Decision

A materialization point costs I/O (write the intermediate result to Delta, then read it back) but provides three benefits: fresh statistics (the CBO operates on actual row counts instead of compounded estimates), a cache boundary (the intermediate result is stored and can be reused by other pipelines), and a checkpoint (if the second stage fails, only the second stage needs to re-run). The decision to materialize depends on the ratio of materialization cost to the CBO improvement benefit. For GridUnion's enrichment pipeline, the intermediate table after join 4 contains 3.2 million rows at 1.5GB (down from the 8TB raw Silver input). Writing and reading 1.5GB takes approximately 20 seconds. The CBO improvement from accurate statistics saves 12 minutes in the second stage (because join 5 switches from sort-merge to broadcast). The 20-second materialization cost buys a 12-minute improvement: a 36x return on investment.
Where to Place Materialization Points

The optimal placement of materialization points depends on where the CBO's estimation errors are largest and where the intermediate data volume is smallest (minimizing the materialization I/O cost). The heuristic: place a materialization point after the join that produces the largest reduction in row count, because that is where the CBO's compounded error is most likely to cause a wrong strategy choice for the downstream joins. In GridUnion's pipeline, join 4 (readings × pricing tier, filtered to active tiers) reduces the row count from 3.5M to 3.2M — a modest reduction. But join 3 (readings × regulatory region, filtered to German regions only) is where the CBO's estimate diverges most (estimated 3.5M rows, actual 850K rows, a 4x overestimate). The materialization point after join 3 would be more beneficial than after join 4, but the intermediate table after join 3 is larger (850K rows at 800MB vs 3.2M rows at 1.5GB after join 4 adds pricing columns). GridUnion tested both placements and found that materializing after join 4 gave the better total runtime because the 1.5GB write is more expensive but the subsequent joins (5, 6, 7) all benefit from the statistics reset.
The CBO’s Join Reordering Limits

The CBO's dynamic programming algorithm (Module 2, lesson 3) evaluates all possible orderings for up to 12 tables. GridUnion's 7-table enrichment pipeline is within this limit, so the CBO considers all 5,040 orderings. But the CBO's reordering assumes that all joins are independent (the order does not affect correctness), which is true for inner joins but not for outer joins. Left outer joins in the chain constrain the ordering: the outer side must be preserved, limiting the CBO's freedom to reorder. GridUnion's pipeline has 2 left outer joins (weather and grid topology), which reduces the reorderable combinations from 5,040 to approximately 720. The CBO evaluates these 720 orderings and selects the one with the lowest estimated cost. When the estimates are accurate (after materialization provides fresh statistics), the selected ordering is usually optimal. When the estimates are wrong, the selected ordering can be dramatically suboptimal, which is why materialization points exist: they reset the estimation errors at strategic points in the chain.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Materialization points are not a sign of failure — they are a tool for managing the CBO's fundamental limitation: statistics degrade through plan depth. A 7-join chain with no materialization asks the CBO to estimate cardinalities through 7 operators, producing compounded errors. Two stages of 4 and 3 joins, separated by a materialized intermediate, ask the CBO to estimate through 4 and 3 operators respectively, with fresh statistics at the boundary. The total computation is the same (7 joins plus one write-read cycle), but the optimization quality is dramatically better. Treat materialization as a statistics refresh mechanism, not as a performance penalty.

Not all data lives in Delta Lake. GridUnion's customer reference data resides in a PostgreSQL database managed by the CRM team. The German regulatory reporting codes are in an Oracle database maintained by the compliance team. The real-time grid topology is in a REST API served by the operations team. Joining Spark Delta tables with these external systems is called federation, and it introduces challenges that do not exist in Spark-to-Spark joins: network latency between Spark and the external system, limited pushdown capabilities (the external system may not support all filter types), and the mismatch between Spark's distributed execution model and the external system's single-node architecture. Federated joins are common at enterprise scale because consolidating all data into Delta Lake is organizationally impractical — the CRM team will not migrate their PostgreSQL database to Delta just to make the billing pipeline faster.
JDBC Join Patterns

# Pattern 1: Read the entire JDBC table into Spark, then join
# Simple but wasteful: reads all rows even if only a fraction are needed
df_customers = (spark.read
    .format('jdbc')
    .option('url', config['pg_url'])
    .option('dbtable', 'crm.customers')
    .option('user', config['pg_user'])
    .option('password', config['pg_password'])
    .load()
)
df_joined = readings.join(df_customers, 'customer_id', 'left')

# Pattern 2: Push a filter to the JDBC source
# Better: only reads matching rows from PostgreSQL
df_customers_filtered = (spark.read
    .format('jdbc')
    .option('url', config['pg_url'])
    .option('dbtable', '(SELECT * FROM crm.customers WHERE country = \'DE\') AS t')
    .option('user', config['pg_user'])
    .option('password', config['pg_password'])
    .load()
)
df_joined = readings.join(broadcast(df_customers_filtered), 'customer_id', 'left')

# Pattern 3: Nightly snapshot to Delta (GridUnion's recommended pattern)
# Decouple the JDBC read from the pipeline execution
# A nightly job reads PostgreSQL and writes to Delta
# The pipeline reads from the Delta snapshot with full Spark optimization
df_customers_snapshot = spark.read.format('delta').load(
    'gridunion_prod.silver_de.customer_snapshot'
)
df_joined = readings.join(broadcast(df_customers_snapshot), 'customer_id', 'left')
The Snapshot Pattern

GridUnion's recommended pattern for federated joins is the nightly snapshot: a dedicated pipeline reads the external system's data every night, writes it to a Delta table, and production pipelines join against the Delta snapshot rather than the external system directly. This decoupling provides four benefits: the production pipeline is not affected by external system outages or slowdowns (it reads from the local Delta snapshot), the Delta snapshot supports full Spark optimization (predicate pushdown, column pruning, Z-ORDER, bucketing), the snapshot provides a consistent view of the external data (no partial updates during the pipeline's execution), and the external system is queried once per day rather than once per pipeline run (reducing load on the CRM database from 8 queries/day to 1). The cost is latency: the snapshot is up to 24 hours stale. For GridUnion's customer data (which changes infrequently), 24-hour staleness is acceptable. For the real-time grid topology, a 15-minute streaming snapshot is used instead.

The snapshot pattern also solves the pushdown limitation. When Spark reads from JDBC, predicate pushdown depends on the JDBC driver's capabilities and the query syntax — complex predicates may not push down, and Spark falls back to reading all rows. With a Delta snapshot, all Spark optimizations are available: partition pruning, column pruning, predicate pushdown, and Z-ORDER skipping. The one-time nightly read from PostgreSQL is unoptimized (it reads the full table), but this cost is paid once per day. Every subsequent pipeline read is fully optimized against the Delta snapshot. GridUnion's customer reference table has 3.5 million rows (one per meter). The nightly JDBC read takes 90 seconds. Eight pipelines that read the Delta snapshot each take 2–5 seconds. The total daily cost is 90 + (8 × 5) = 130 seconds. Without the snapshot, each pipeline would read from JDBC: 8 × 90 = 720 seconds. The snapshot pattern saves 590 seconds per day while providing better optimization.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Federated joins are a coupling problem, not a performance problem. Every direct JDBC join creates a runtime dependency on the external system's availability, performance, and schema. The snapshot pattern breaks this coupling: the nightly snapshot job is the only component that depends on the external system, and the production pipelines depend only on the Delta snapshot. If the external system is slow, unavailable, or changes its schema, only the snapshot job is affected — and the production pipelines continue running against the previous day's snapshot. This decoupling is why GridUnion mandates the snapshot pattern for all federated joins: direct JDBC joins in production pipelines are prohibited by the platform team's architecture standards.

Most of the mid-level and senior-level join discussion has focused on tables in the 2–8TB range. When both sides of a join exceed 10TB, new constraints emerge that require different strategies. The sort-merge join's shuffle phase moves 20TB+ of data across the network, which at 10Gbps takes 30+ minutes for the shuffle alone. The sort phase within each partition requires enough executor memory to hold the largest partition in memory for sorting — and at 200 shuffle partitions, each partition averages 100GB. Even at 2,000 shuffle partitions, each partition is 10GB, requiring executors with 16GB+ heap to avoid spill. Bucketed joins eliminate the shuffle but require the tables to be bucketed with aligned counts, and rewriting 10TB+ tables to add bucketing takes 4–6 hours on a dedicated cluster. This lesson covers the unique challenges of 10TB+ joins and the strategies GridUnion uses for its quarterly historical analysis job.
Memory Planning for Large Sort-Merge Joins

The sort-merge join's memory requirement is determined by the largest partition on either side. With 2,000 shuffle partitions and a 10TB table, the average partition is 5GB. But data skew (the persistent Berlin problem from lesson 6) can produce partitions 3–5x the average. A 25GB partition requires enough execution memory to sort the partition without excessive spill. The memory formula: executor_memory × spark.memory.fraction × (1 - spark.memory.storageFraction) gives the execution memory available for sorting. With 16GB executor memory, 0.6 memory fraction, and 0.5 storage fraction, the execution memory is 16 × 0.6 × 0.5 = 4.8GB — far below the 25GB partition size. The sort will spill heavily to disk. GridUnion's quarterly join uses 32GB executors with 4,000 shuffle partitions, producing average partitions of 2.5GB and maximum partitions of ~8GB. The 9.6GB of execution memory (32 × 0.6 × 0.5) handles the maximum partition with moderate spill.
The External Shuffle Service

At 10TB+, the shuffle data volume exceeds what the executors' local disks can hold. A 20TB shuffle produces 20TB of shuffle write data distributed across executors. If each executor has 500GB of local SSD, a 40-executor cluster has 20TB of total local storage — but the shuffle data is not evenly distributed (it depends on the hash function), and the executors also need local storage for spill, temp files, and the OS. The external shuffle service (ESS) addresses this by storing shuffle data on a shared, durable storage layer (typically the cluster's HDFS or the cloud provider's managed shuffle service) rather than on executor-local disks. This allows executors to be released after the shuffle write (important for dynamic allocation) and provides fault tolerance (if an executor crashes, its shuffle data is not lost). GridUnion enables ESS for all jobs exceeding 5TB of shuffle data, configured via spark.shuffle.service.enabled and the cluster's YARN NodeManager shuffle handler.
Disk I/O as the Bottleneck

At 10TB+, the bottleneck shifts from network bandwidth to disk I/O bandwidth. A sort-merge join on a 10TB table reads the data from cloud storage (scan), writes it to local disk (shuffle write), reads it from local disk (shuffle read), sorts it (with possible spill to disk), and merges it. Each byte of data touches the disk 3–4 times. With 40 executors each having a single SSD at 500MB/s read throughput, the aggregate disk bandwidth is 20GB/s. Processing 10TB through 4 disk touches requires 40TB of disk I/O at 20GB/s = 33 minutes of pure disk time. This sets the floor for the join's duration regardless of CPU, memory, or network optimization. GridUnion's quarterly historical join takes 35 minutes on a 64-executor cluster with NVMe SSDs — close to the theoretical disk I/O floor, indicating that the join is well-optimized and the remaining optimization opportunities are in disk hardware (faster SSDs, more executors) rather than in Spark configuration.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: At 10TB+, the optimization strategy changes from 'avoid shuffle' to 'manage shuffle.' Shuffle cannot always be avoided — bucketing 10TB+ tables is operationally expensive, and broadcast is impossible for large tables. The focus shifts to minimizing the per-byte cost of the shuffle: enough partitions to keep partition sizes within executor memory, the external shuffle service for fault tolerance and storage, NVMe SSDs for disk bandwidth, and dynamic allocation to scale the cluster for the duration of the join. GridUnion schedules the quarterly 10TB+ join on a dedicated cluster with 64 executors and NVMe SSDs, provisioned only for the 35-minute job and then released. The cluster cost is $180 per run. Attempting to run the same join on the shared daily cluster (32 executors, HDD) would take 4 hours and impact other pipelines.

This lesson synthesizes the module into GridUnion's join architecture decision framework. Every new multi-table pipeline undergoes a join architecture review before deployment. The review evaluates the join topology, the strategy for each join, the skew risk, the materialization points, and the federated join pattern. The review produces a join architecture document that is attached to the pipeline's data product manifest (Module 1, lesson 5) and reviewed during the quarterly performance audit. The framework is not bureaucracy — it is the accumulated experience of 18 months of production operation, 4 join-related SLA breaches, and 2 complete pipeline rewrites that could have been avoided with an upfront architecture review.
The Join Strategy Decision Tree

blocks.title: GridUnion join strategy decision tree
blocks.content:
New join between Table A and Table B
│
├─ Is either side < 200MB after filters?
│   └─ YES: Broadcast the small side. Verify with broadcast() hint.
│       └─ Is DPP available? (join key is probe-side partition column)
│           ├─ YES: Excellent. Broadcast + DPP. Expected I/O: < 20% of probe table.
│           └─ NO: Broadcast only. Expected I/O: 100% of probe table.
│
├─ Are both sides bucketed on the join key with the same count?
│   └─ YES: Shuffle-free bucketed join. Verify in plan (no Exchange operator).
│
├─ Is the join condition a range (BETWEEN, <, >)?
│   ├─ One side < 200MB: Broadcast + nested loop.
│   ├─ Both sides large, bounded range: Bin-based approximation.
│   └─ Both sides large, unbounded: Schedule on dedicated cluster.
│
├─ Is data skew expected on the join key?
│   ├─ Moderate (< 5x median): AQE handles it. No action needed.
│   ├─ Severe (> 10x median), temporary: Apply salting.
│   └─ Severe, persistent: Evaluate composite key redesign.
│
└─ Default: Sort-merge join with AQE.
    └─ Partition count = data_size_mb / 256
    └─ Monitor shuffle volume and spill in pipeline metrics.
blocks.caption: The decision tree is evaluated top-to-bottom. The first matching branch determines the strategy. Broadcast is always preferred when the size constraint is met. Bucketed joins are preferred when alignment exists. Sort-merge with AQE is the default fallback.

blocks.content: The Skew Escalation Path

rows: Level 0: AQE
Skew < 5x median
AQE automatically splits skewed partitions. No action needed.
Zero
Level 1: Threshold tuning
AQE not triggering on moderate skew
Lower spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.skewedPartitionFactor from 5 to 3.
5 minutes (config change)
Level 2: Salting
Skew > 10x median, AQE insufficient
Apply salting with factor = max_partition_size / median_partition_size.
1–2 hours (code change)
Level 3: Pre-aggregation
Skewed side does not need row-level detail downstream
Aggregate the skewed side before the join. Join on the aggregated result.
2–4 hours (pipeline redesign)
Level 4: Key redesign
Persistent skew affecting 3+ pipelines for 3+ months
Decompose the join key into a finer-grained composite key.
2–4 weeks (cross-team migration)

blocks.content: What Comes Next

Modules 3–4 complete the extensibility phase of the senior-level track. You can now design partition strategies for multi-consumer tables at terabyte scale (Module 3) and architect join strategies for complex multi-hop pipelines with skew, range conditions, and federated data sources (this module). Module 5 shifts to the second extensibility topic: schema registries and data contracts. Where Module 4 addressed how tables are physically joined, Module 5 addresses how tables are organizationally connected: the schema contracts between producer and consumer teams, the compatibility rules that govern schema evolution, the breaking change negotiation process, and the automated drift detection that catches unplanned schema changes before they break downstream pipelines.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: The join architecture review is the most impactful 30-minute meeting in the pipeline lifecycle. A review that identifies a missing broadcast hint saves 4 minutes per run × 365 runs/year = 24 hours of compute annually. A review that identifies the need for a materialization point can save 55% of total runtime. A review that catches a federated join pattern and recommends the snapshot approach prevents a runtime dependency on an external system. The review costs 30 minutes of 2–3 engineers' time. The savings compound over the pipeline's lifetime. GridUnion requires the review for every pipeline with 3+ joins and recommends it for every pipeline with 2+ joins.

chapters.title: Schema Registries and Data Contracts
chapters.description: Implement cross-team schema governance at scale — schema registries, producer-consumer contracts, breaking change negotiation, contract testing across team boundaries, and the organizational processes that prevent schema changes from breaking downstream pipelines.

blocks.content: The mid-level track treated schema enforcement as a pipeline concern: the Silver pipeline validates incoming data against a golden schema file, rejects non-conforming rows, and writes the validated output to a Delta table with schema enforcement enabled. This works when one team controls the entire chain from source to consumer. At GridUnion scale, the chain crosses organizational boundaries: the French subsidiary's meter collection system (operated by a vendor) sends CSV files to the Bronze layer (ingested by the platform team), which feeds the French Silver pipeline (operated by the French DE team), which is consumed by the German billing pipeline (operated by the German billing team) and the Platinum analytics layer (operated by the analytics team). A schema change at any point in this chain can break systems operated by teams that had no knowledge of the change and no opportunity to prepare.
GridUnion’s Acquisition Schema Crisis

Three weeks after the acquisition closed, the French meter vendor added a firmware_diagnostics column to their CSV export. The column was a JSON string containing nested diagnostic data from the meter's latest firmware update. The French Bronze ingestion pipeline used inferSchema, which silently added the new column. The French Silver pipeline's schema validation caught the new column and, because it was configured in strict mode, rejected every row in the batch — dropping 850,000 French meter readings for the day. The German billing pipeline, which depended on the French Silver table for cross-country totals, produced a billing report that was short by 15% of the French readings. The regulatory export pipeline, which joined German and French Silver tables, failed with a schema mismatch error because the French Silver table's schema now differed from the expected golden schema. Four teams were affected by a change that one vendor made without notification. The incident took 14 hours to diagnose and resolve because no team had visibility into the full dependency chain.
The Three Schema Failure Modes

rows: Additive change (new column)
Source adds a column that no consumer expects
Consumers with strict schema validation reject the data. Consumers with lenient validation silently ignore the column.
Golden schema file rejects unknown columns
Only catches the failure at the Silver pipeline. Does not prevent the source from making the change or notify downstream consumers.
Type change (column type widened or narrowed)
Source changes a column's type (e.g., integer to string, or string to double)
Every consumer that casts or validates the column type fails or produces wrong results.
Schema enforcement in Delta rejects the write
Rejects at write time, but the pipeline has already read and transformed the data. The failure is late and the error message is opaque.
Breaking change (column removed or renamed)
Source removes or renames a column that consumers depend on
Every consumer that references the column fails with AnalysisException: column not found.
Schema contract test in CI catches missing columns
Only catches the failure in the affected consumer's CI, not in the producer's CI. The producer deploys the change before any consumer knows.

blocks.content: The common thread across all three failure modes is information asymmetry: the producer knows about the change but the consumers do not. The mid-level mitigation catches the failure at the consumer's boundary (the pipeline rejects bad data), but it does not prevent the failure or notify the affected parties before the change reaches production. The senior-level solution addresses the information asymmetry directly: a schema registry that tracks every schema version, data contracts that formalize the producer-consumer agreement, compatibility rules that prevent breaking changes from being deployed without coordination, and a drift detection system that catches unplanned changes before they propagate. This module covers each component.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Schema enforcement is necessary but not sufficient for multi-team platforms. Enforcement catches failures at the boundary. Governance prevents failures at the source. The difference is the feedback loop: enforcement tells the consumer 'the data is wrong,' while governance tells the producer 'this change will break 5 consumers — here is the impact analysis and the coordination plan.' The schema crisis at GridUnion could not have been prevented by stricter enforcement (the enforcement worked — it rejected the bad data). It could only have been prevented by governance: the vendor change should have been caught before it reached Bronze, analyzed for downstream impact, and coordinated with the affected teams.

A schema registry is a centralized service that stores, versions, and validates schemas for every table on the platform. When a producer writes data, the registry records the schema version. When a consumer reads data, the registry provides the expected schema. When a producer attempts to change a schema, the registry checks whether the change is compatible with the registered consumers' expectations. The registry is the single source of truth for 'what does this table look like?' — replacing the mid-level pattern of golden schema files scattered across pipeline repositories. At GridUnion scale, 200+ tables across 6 teams require a centralized registry because the alternative — each team maintaining its own schema files — produces inconsistency (the German team's copy of the French Silver schema diverges from the French team's actual schema), staleness (the schema file was updated 3 months ago but the table has evolved since), and no cross-reference (there is no way to answer 'which consumers will break if I change this column?').
Registry Options

rows: Confluent Schema Registry
Avro, Protobuf, JSON Schema
Full compatibility modes (backward, forward, full, none)
Native Kafka integration. Spark reads via kafka + schema registry. No native Delta integration.
Best for Kafka-centric architectures. GridUnion uses it for the streaming Kafka topics but not for Delta tables.
AWS Glue Schema Registry
Avro, JSON Schema
Backward, forward, full, none
Integrated with Glue Catalog. Spark reads via Glue Data Catalog.
Good for AWS-native platforms. GridUnion's ADLS-based architecture limits the fit.
Unity Catalog (Databricks)
Spark StructType (Delta native)
Schema enforcement at write time. No explicit compatibility modes.
Native Delta/Spark integration. Schema is the table's metadata.
Best for Delta-centric platforms. GridUnion's primary registry for Delta tables.
Custom registry (Delta-backed)
Any (stored as JSON)
Custom compatibility logic implemented by the platform team
Reads and writes via Spark. Full control over compatibility rules.
GridUnion's supplementary registry for cross-system schemas (JDBC sources, Kafka topics) not covered by Unity Catalog.

blocks.content: GridUnion’s Dual Registry Architecture

GridUnion uses two registries. Unity Catalog is the primary registry for all Delta tables: it stores the schema as table metadata, tracks schema evolution through the Delta transaction log, and enforces schema at write time. The custom Delta-backed registry supplements Unity Catalog for schemas that Unity Catalog does not manage: the CSV schemas of external vendor files (the French meter vendor, the Dutch SCADA vendor), the Kafka topic schemas for the streaming pipelines, and the JDBC source schemas for the PostgreSQL and Oracle dimension tables. The custom registry stores each schema as a JSON document in a versioned Delta table (gridunion_prod.monitoring.schema_registry), with columns for the source system, the schema version, the compatibility mode, the schema JSON, and the registration timestamp. A Python library provides the API for registering, retrieving, and comparing schemas. The dual-registry approach is a pragmatic compromise: Unity Catalog handles the 80% case (Delta tables) natively, and the custom registry covers the 20% that falls outside Unity Catalog's scope.
The Schema Registration API

from pyspark.sql.types import StructType
import json
from datetime import datetime
from delta.tables import DeltaTable

class SchemaRegistry:
    """GridUnion's custom schema registry for non-Delta sources.
    
    Stores schema versions in a Delta table with compatibility checking.
    Unity Catalog handles Delta table schemas natively.
    """
    
    REGISTRY_PATH = 'gridunion_prod.monitoring.schema_registry'
    
    def __init__(self, spark):
        self.spark = spark
    
    def register(self, source_system: str, schema: StructType, 
                 compatibility: str = 'backward') -> int:
        """Register a new schema version. Returns the version number.
        
        Checks compatibility against the latest registered version.
        Raises ValueError if the new schema is incompatible.
        """
        latest = self.get_latest(source_system)
        if latest:
            violations = self.check_compatibility(
                latest['schema'], schema, compatibility
            )
            if violations:
                raise ValueError(
                    f"Schema incompatible with {compatibility} mode: "
                    f"{'; '.join(violations)}"
                )
            new_version = latest['version'] + 1
        else:
            new_version = 1
        
        schema_json = schema.json()
        row = self.spark.createDataFrame([(
            source_system, new_version, compatibility,
            schema_json, datetime.now(),
        )], 'source_system STRING, version INT, compatibility STRING, '
             'schema_json STRING, registered_at TIMESTAMP')
        
        row.write.format('delta').mode('append').saveAsTable(self.REGISTRY_PATH)
        return new_version
    
    def get_latest(self, source_system: str) -> dict:
        """Get the latest schema version for a source system."""
        result = (self.spark.table(self.REGISTRY_PATH)
            .filter(col('source_system') == source_system)
            .orderBy(col('version').desc())
            .limit(1)
            .collect()
        )
        if not result:
            return None
        row = result[0]
        return {
            'version': row['version'],
            'schema': StructType.fromJson(json.loads(row['schema_json'])),
            'compatibility': row['compatibility'],
        }
    
    def check_compatibility(self, old_schema: StructType, 
                           new_schema: StructType, mode: str) -> list[str]:
        """Check if new_schema is compatible with old_schema under the given mode."""
        # Implementation in lesson 4
        pass

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: A schema registry is not a database of schemas — it is a coordination mechanism. The registry's value is not that it stores schemas (a Git repository can do that) but that it enforces compatibility rules at registration time, provides a single source of truth that all teams query, and enables impact analysis by linking schemas to their consumers. The registry's authority comes from its integration with the deployment pipeline: no schema change reaches production without passing through the registry's compatibility check. If the registry can be bypassed (by writing directly to the table without registering), it becomes a documentation system that gradually falls out of date — the same failure mode as golden schema files.

Module 1, lesson 5 introduced data products with manifest files that declared schema, SLA, and quality thresholds. Data contracts extend this concept from unilateral declarations (the producer publishes what they guarantee) to bilateral agreements (the producer and consumer agree on what the interface will be, and both sides have obligations). The distinction matters because a data product manifest can be ignored by the consumer (they never read it) and violated by the producer (they change the schema without updating the manifest). A data contract has enforcement: the producer's CI validates that the output matches the contract, the consumer's CI validates that the input matches the contract, and the platform's monitoring validates that the runtime data matches the contract. Violations produce alerts, not silent failures. GridUnion's data contracts are the organizational layer that transforms the schema registry (a technical tool) into a governance mechanism (an organizational practice).
The Contract Specification

# contracts/silver_de_meter_readings.yaml
# This contract is the binding agreement between the producer (de_germany team)
# and all registered consumers.

contract:
  version: 3          # incremented on every contract change
  status: active       # active | deprecated | sunset
  
  producer:
    team: de_germany
    pipeline: silver_meters_daily
    contact: '#de-germany-data'
  
  interface:
    fully_qualified_name: gridunion_prod.silver_de.meter_readings
    format: delta
    schema_registry_ref: unity_catalog  # schema managed by Unity Catalog
    partition_columns: [country, date]
    
    # Column-level contract: columns the producer guarantees to provide
    guaranteed_columns:
      - name: meter_id
        type: string
        nullable: false
        description: "Unique meter identifier. Never null. Format: CC-RRRRR-NNNNN"
      - name: daily_kw
        type: double
        nullable: true
        description: "Daily energy consumption in kilowatt-hours. Null if meter offline."
      - name: date
        type: date
        nullable: false
      - name: region
        type: string
        nullable: false
      - name: country
        type: string
        nullable: false
        allowed_values: ['DE', 'FR', 'NL', 'LU']
    
    # Columns the producer may add without a contract change
    # (backward-compatible additions)
    extensible: true
  
  sla:
    freshness: 'updated daily by 07:00 UTC'
    availability: '99.5% monthly'
    quality:
      null_rate_daily_kw: '< 0.5%'
      row_count_range: '350000-600000 per day'
      completeness: 'all 8 German regions present every day'
  
  consumers:
    - team: gold_billing
      columns_used: [meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, country]
      since: '2025-01-15'
    - team: analytics
      columns_used: [meter_id, daily_kw, date, region]
      since: '2025-03-01'
    - team: regulatory_de
      columns_used: [meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, country]
      since: '2025-02-01'
  
  change_policy:
    compatibility: backward
    deprecation_notice_days: 30
    breaking_change_requires: governance_committee_approval
The Contract Lifecycle

A data contract moves through four states: draft (the producer proposes the contract, consumers review), active (both sides accept the terms, enforcement begins), deprecated (the producer announces that the contract will change, consumers have a migration window), and sunset (the old contract is no longer honored, the new version takes effect). The lifecycle transitions are tracked in the contract's version history. At GridUnion, the draft-to-active transition requires at least one consumer to register (a contract with no consumers is pointless). The active-to-deprecated transition requires the producer to file a change request with the governance committee (lesson 6). The deprecated-to-sunset transition requires the deprecation period to elapse (30 days by default, extendable by governance committee if consumers need more time). Every transition is logged to the contract audit trail, which the regulatory team uses to demonstrate that schema changes follow a controlled process.

The contract specifies the interface, not the implementation. The guaranteed_columns section lists the columns that the producer promises to provide, with their types and nullability. The producer may add new columns (extensible: true) without changing the contract version, because backward-compatible additions do not affect consumers. The producer may not remove, rename, or retype a guaranteed column without a contract change (which triggers the deprecation process). This separation means that the producer can evolve the table's internal implementation (adding diagnostic columns, changing partition strategies, optimizing file formats) without affecting the contract. The consumer depends on the guaranteed interface, not on the table's physical structure. This is the same principle as API versioning in software engineering, applied to data tables.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Data contracts are APIs for tables. Just as a REST API has a versioned specification, a guaranteed response schema, an SLA, and a deprecation policy, a data contract has a versioned schema, guaranteed columns, freshness and quality SLAs, and a change policy. The contract decouples the producer's implementation from the consumer's expectations. The producer can refactor, optimize, and extend their pipeline as long as the contract's guaranteed columns, types, and SLAs are met. The consumer can build reliable downstream systems because they depend on a stable, versioned interface rather than on the producer's internal implementation details.

Compatibility rules define which schema changes can be deployed without breaking consumers and which require the deprecation process. The rules are not Spark-specific — they derive from the same principles used in Avro schema evolution, Protobuf wire compatibility, and REST API versioning. The four modes are backward compatibility (new schema can read old data: consumers using the new schema can read data written by the old schema), forward compatibility (old schema can read new data: consumers using the old schema can read data written by the new schema), full compatibility (both directions), and none (no compatibility guarantee — any change is allowed). The mode determines which changes are safe to deploy without consumer coordination. GridUnion's default is backward compatibility because it protects the most common scenario: consumers updating their code to handle a new schema version while old data (written before the change) still exists in the table.
Change Classification by Compatibility Mode

rows: Add a nullable column
Yes — new readers handle the new column; old data has null for the new column
Yes — old readers ignore the unknown column
Yes
Add a non-nullable column with a default
Yes — old data gets the default value
No — old readers do not know about the column and cannot validate the constraint
No
Remove a column
No — new readers expect the column but old data has it (wait, reversed: old readers expect the column but new data lacks it)
Yes — old readers see the column in old data; new data lacks it but old readers that do not reference it are fine
No
Rename a column
No — equivalent to removing the old and adding the new; breaks both directions
No
No
Widen a type (int → long)
Yes — old int values are safely read as long
No — old readers expecting int receive long, which may overflow or fail
No
Narrow a type (long → int)
No — old long values may overflow when read as int
Yes — old readers expected long and receive int (safe upcast)
No
Change nullability (nullable → non-nullable)
No — old data may have nulls that violate the new non-nullable constraint
Yes — old readers accepting nulls can handle non-null values
No
Reorder columns
Yes — Parquet is column-name-based, not position-based
Yes
Yes

blocks.content: Implementing Compatibility Checks

GridUnion's compatibility checker runs in two places: the schema registry's register() method (lesson 2) checks compatibility before accepting a new schema version, and the producer's CI pipeline runs the check before allowing a schema-changing PR to merge. The dual enforcement prevents both accidental changes (a developer who adds a non-nullable column without realizing it breaks backward compatibility) and intentional but uncoordinated changes (a developer who knowingly makes a breaking change without following the deprecation process). The CI check produces an actionable error message: 'Column customer_segment changed from nullable to non-nullable. This is not backward-compatible. To proceed, either make the column nullable or file a breaking change request (see docs/breaking-changes.md).' The error message includes the remediation path, not just the violation.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Compatibility rules are the schema governance equivalent of type checking in programming languages.
