blocks.caption: The Parquet reader reads the file from the end: the footer provides the metadata needed to skip irrelevant row groups and columns before reading any data. Column pruning skips entire column chunks. Predicate pushdown skips entire row groups.

blocks.content: Row Group Size Tuning

The row group size trade-off is selectivity versus overhead. Smaller row groups produce narrower min/max statistics: a 32MB row group covering 200,000 rows of region data might have min=BERLIN, max=BERLIN (if the data is sorted by region), enabling perfect skipping for non-Berlin queries. A 128MB row group covering 800,000 rows might have min=BERLIN, max=MUNICH, providing no skipping for BERLIN-only queries because the range contains many values. But smaller row groups mean more row group metadata entries in the footer, more seek operations when reading (each row group requires a separate I/O request on cloud storage), and more footer reads when listing files. GridUnion's tuning rule: if the table is Z-ORDERed on a low-cardinality column (like region), use smaller row groups (64MB) to preserve the tight statistics that Z-ORDER produces. If the table is not Z-ORDERed, larger row groups (128–256MB) are better because the statistics are wide regardless of row group size, and the reduced metadata overhead is more valuable than the marginal improvement in predicate selectivity.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: The Parquet file structure is a contract between the writer and the reader. The writer chooses the row group size, the encoding per column, the compression codec, and whether to write column statistics. The reader relies on these choices to skip data efficiently. A writer that produces poor statistics (wide row groups on unsorted data) or no statistics (statistics disabled for performance) forces the reader to scan more data than necessary. Senior engineers control both sides of the contract: the write-side Parquet configuration determines the structure, and the read-side predicate evaluation determines how well the structure is exploited. Optimizing only one side produces half the benefit.

Parquet stores data column by column, and each column can use a different encoding that exploits the column's data characteristics for maximum compression. A region column with 30 distinct values across 1 million rows stores 1 million full strings with plain encoding (30MB at 30 bytes/string) or 1 million 5-bit integers with dictionary encoding (625KB — a 48x reduction). A timestamp column with monotonically increasing values stores 1 million 8-byte values with plain encoding (8MB) or 1 million delta values (the difference from the previous timestamp) with delta encoding (~2MB — a 4x reduction because the deltas are small and compress well). A boolean column stores 1 million bytes with plain encoding or 1 million bits with bit-packing (125KB — an 8x reduction). The encoding choice is per-column and per-page, chosen automatically by the Parquet writer based on the data's characteristics. Understanding the encoding types lets you design schemas that encode efficiently and diagnose why one table compresses to 20% of raw size while another compresses to only 60%.
Encoding Types

rows: Dictionary (PLAIN_DICTIONARY)
Builds a dictionary of unique values and stores indices instead of values. Each page has a dictionary page followed by data pages of integer indices.
Low-cardinality columns: region (30), country (4), customer_type (4), tier (4). Falls back to plain if cardinality exceeds the page size.
region column: 30 values → 5-bit indices. 48x compression over plain encoding.
Delta (DELTA_BINARY_PACKED)
Stores the difference between consecutive values. The first value is stored fully; subsequent values store only the delta from the previous.
Sorted or monotonically increasing numeric columns: timestamps, auto-increment IDs, sorted meter readings.
timestamp column: monotonic 15-minute intervals → uniform deltas of 900 seconds. 4x compression.
Run-Length Encoding (RLE)
Stores consecutive repeated values as (value, count) pairs. Ten consecutive BERLIN values store as (BERLIN, 10) instead of BERLIN ten times.
Columns with long runs of the same value: sorted columns, partition columns (after Z-ORDER), boolean flags.
is_valid column after sorting: long runs of true followed by short runs of false. 50x compression.
Plain (PLAIN)
Stores each value directly in its native byte representation. No encoding overhead but no compression benefit.
High-cardinality columns with no pattern: meter_id (3.5M unique values), daily_kw (continuous doubles).
daily_kw column: random continuous values with no dictionary, no delta, no runs. Plain is the only option.
Byte Stream Split (BYTE_STREAM_SPLIT)
Splits floating-point bytes across streams (all first bytes together, all second bytes, etc.) for better compression of floating-point data.
Floating-point columns where byte-level patterns exist: scientific measurements, sensor readings.
SCADA voltage readings: 32-bit floats where the exponent bytes are similar across rows. 15% better compression than plain.

blocks.content: Compression Codecs

After encoding, each page is compressed with a codec that further reduces the byte size. The codec operates on the encoded bytes, not the raw values, so it benefits from the patterns that encoding creates (dictionary indices are small integers that compress well; delta values are small and repetitive). Spark supports four codecs for Parquet: Snappy (default, fast compression and decompression, moderate ratio of 2–4x), ZSTD (slower compression, faster decompression than Snappy, better ratio of 3–6x), LZ4 (fastest decompression, compression ratio similar to Snappy), and GZIP (slowest, best ratio of 4–8x). The choice affects both storage cost (better ratio = smaller files = less cloud storage) and read throughput (faster decompression = faster scans). GridUnion standardized on ZSTD for all Silver and Gold tables after benchmarking showed 25% smaller files than Snappy with only 5% slower scan throughput, saving approximately $2,400/month in ADLS storage costs across the 8TB Silver archive.
Diagnosing Compression Effectiveness

# Analyze encoding and compression effectiveness per column
# Use parquet-tools (pip install parquet-tools) or PyArrow

import pyarrow.parquet as pq

def analyze_parquet_encoding(file_path: str):
    """Report encoding and compression statistics for a Parquet file."""
    pf = pq.ParquetFile(file_path)
    metadata = pf.metadata
    
    print(f"File: {file_path}")
    print(f"Row groups: {metadata.num_row_groups}")
    print(f"Rows: {metadata.num_rows:,}")
    print(f"Columns: {metadata.num_columns}")
    print()
    
    for rg_idx in range(metadata.num_row_groups):
        rg = metadata.row_group(rg_idx)
        print(f"Row Group {rg_idx}: {rg.num_rows:,} rows, {rg.total_byte_size:,} bytes")
        
        for col_idx in range(rg.num_columns):
            col = rg.column(col_idx)
            ratio = col.total_uncompressed_size / col.total_compressed_size if col.total_compressed_size > 0 else 0
            print(f"  {col.path_in_schema:30s}  "
                  f"enc={str(col.encodings):30s}  "
                  f"codec={col.compression}  "
                  f"compressed={col.total_compressed_size:>12,}  "
                  f"uncompressed={col.total_uncompressed_size:>12,}  "
                  f"ratio={ratio:.1f}x")

# Usage: download a sample file and analyze
# analyze_parquet_encoding('/tmp/sample_silver_readings.parquet')
# Output shows which columns benefit most from encoding/compression
# and which columns are poorly compressed (candidates for schema redesign)

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Schema design determines encoding effectiveness. A region column with 30 string values dictionary-encodes to 48x compression. The same data stored as region_code (integer 1–30) is already compact and gains little from dictionary encoding. Conversely, a meter_id column with 3.5 million unique strings cannot dictionary-encode (the dictionary exceeds the page size) and compresses poorly. Storing meter_id as a structured format (country-region-sequence as separate integer fields) would enable delta encoding on the sequence component. The senior-level insight: encoding effectiveness is not just a Parquet configuration concern — it is a schema design concern. The schema you choose determines the compression you get.

Module 2, lesson 8 covered predicate pushdown from the optimizer's perspective: how Catalyst decomposes filters and pushes them to the data source. This lesson covers pushdown from the reader's perspective: how the Parquet reader uses the pushed predicates to skip data at three levels of granularity. The first level is row group statistics: the footer contains min, max, and null count for each column in each row group. The reader checks the predicate against these statistics and skips row groups that cannot contain matching rows. The second level is dictionary filtering: for dictionary-encoded columns, the reader checks whether the predicate's value exists in the row group's dictionary. If the value is not in the dictionary, the entire row group is skipped without reading the data pages. The third level is page-level bloom filters (Parquet 2.0+): a bloom filter per page that probabilistically tests whether the predicate's value appears in the page. Each level reduces the I/O further, and they compose multiplicatively.
The Three Skipping Levels

rows: Row group statistics
Compare predicate value against column min/max in footer
~128MB per row group (millions of rows)
High on unsorted data (wide min/max ranges). Low on sorted/Z-ORDERed data (narrow ranges).
0–95% depending on data order and selectivity
Dictionary filtering
Check if predicate value exists in the column's dictionary
Per row group (dictionary covers all rows in the row group)
Zero false negatives. Only applies to dictionary-encoded columns (low cardinality).
Additional 0–50% on top of row group skipping
Page bloom filters
Probabilistic test: is the value possibly in this page?
~1MB per page (thousands of rows)
1–5% false positive rate (configurable). Only available in Parquet 2.0+ with bloom filters enabled.
Additional 0–40% on top of row group and dictionary skipping

blocks.content: Row Group Statistics and Data Order

The effectiveness of row group statistics depends entirely on the data order within the file. If the region column is unsorted, a row group covering 800,000 rows will likely contain at least one row for every region, making the min/max range cover all 30 values. A predicate region = 'BERLIN' cannot skip any row group because every row group's range includes BERLIN. If the data is sorted by region (or Z-ORDERed on region), each row group contains a narrow range of region values. A row group with min=BERLIN, max=BERLIN can be skipped for any query that does not reference BERLIN, and a row group with min=HAMBURG, max=MUNICH can be skipped for a BERLIN query. GridUnion's Z-ORDER on the Silver readings table produces row groups with tight region ranges: the average row group spans 2–3 regions instead of all 30, enabling 80–90% of row groups to be skipped for single-region queries. Without Z-ORDER, the same query skips 0% of row groups.
Enabling Page-Level Bloom Filters

# Enable page-level bloom filters for specific columns
# Bloom filters add ~1% file size overhead but enable fine-grained skipping

# Write-side: enable bloom filters for the meter_id column
(df.write
    .format('delta')
    .option('parquet.bloom.filter.enabled#meter_id', 'true')
    .option('parquet.bloom.filter.expected.ndv#meter_id', '3500000')  # expected distinct values
    .option('parquet.bloom.filter.fpp#meter_id', '0.01')  # 1% false positive rate
    .mode('overwrite')
    .save('silver/readings/')
)

# Read-side: bloom filters are used automatically for pushed predicates
# Verify bloom filter usage in the Parquet reader's debug log:
# spark.sparkContext.setLogLevel('DEBUG')
# Look for: 'Bloom filter for column meter_id: value not present, skipping page'

# GridUnion enables bloom filters for high-cardinality join keys:
# - meter_id (3.5M distinct): bloom filter enables page-level skipping for point lookups
# - turbine_id (5,000 distinct): dictionary filtering is sufficient (low cardinality)
# - daily_kw (continuous): bloom filters not useful for range predicates

Bloom filters are most valuable for high-cardinality columns where dictionary encoding falls back to plain (because the dictionary exceeds the page size) and where point lookups are common (equality predicates on the column). GridUnion enables bloom filters on meter_id because the column has 3.5 million distinct values (too high for dictionary encoding), is frequently used in point lookups (join on meter_id), and the 1% false positive rate means 99% of non-matching pages are skipped. The bloom filter adds approximately 1% to the file size (the filter itself is stored in each page's metadata), which is negligible compared to the 40–60% scan reduction it provides for meter_id-based joins. Bloom filters are not useful for range predicates (daily_kw > 500) because the filter can only answer 'is this value present?' not 'is any value in this range present?'

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: The three skipping levels form a cascade: row group statistics provide coarse-grained skipping, dictionary filtering adds medium-grained skipping for low-cardinality columns, and bloom filters add fine-grained skipping for high-cardinality columns. Designing for maximum skipping means: Z-ORDER the data to produce tight row group statistics (level 1), let Parquet's automatic dictionary encoding handle low-cardinality columns (level 2), and explicitly enable bloom filters for high-cardinality point-lookup columns (level 3). The compound effect can reduce scan I/O by 95%+ for selective queries — reading 400MB instead of 8TB. No single optimization achieves this; it requires all three levels working together.

Spark's DataSource V2 API is the interface between the execution engine and the storage layer. Every data source — Parquet, Delta, Iceberg, JDBC, Kafka — implements this API to tell Spark how to read its data. The API defines how the source declares its schema, how it reports statistics (for the CBO), how it accepts pushed predicates (for pushdown), how it partitions the data for parallel reading, and how it creates readers for each partition. Understanding the V2 API lets you evaluate whether a custom data source is well-implemented (does it support pushdown? does it report accurate statistics?), diagnose performance issues that originate in the source rather than in the execution engine, and implement custom data sources for proprietary formats. GridUnion implemented a custom V2 data source for the SCADA telemetry format, a proprietary binary format that the turbine vendor provides and that cannot be read by standard Parquet or CSV readers.
The V2 Interface Hierarchy

rows: TableProvider
Entry point: resolves a table name or path to a Table object
inferSchema(), getTable()
Table
Represents a dataset. Declares capabilities (read, write, pushdown support).
name(), schema(), capabilities(), newScanBuilder()
ScanBuilder
Constructs a Scan by accepting pushdown, pruning, and limit options
pushFilters(), pruneColumns(), pushLimit(), build()
Scan
Represents a configured read. Reports size estimates and creates partitions.
readSchema(), estimateStatistics(), planInputPartitions()
InputPartition
Represents one unit of parallel read (one task). Declares data locality.
preferredLocations()
PartitionReader
Actually reads the data for one partition. Returns rows one at a time.
next(), get(), close()

blocks.content: How Pushdown Negotiation Works

When Catalyst pushes predicates to a V2 data source, it calls ScanBuilder.pushFilters(filters) with an array of filter objects (EqualTo, GreaterThan, In, etc.). The ScanBuilder inspects each filter and returns the filters it cannot handle. Catalyst applies the returned (non-pushed) filters as a post-scan Filter operator. The critical detail: the data source is responsible for the correctness of pushed filters. If the source claims to push a filter but returns unfiltered data, the query produces wrong results. Spark trusts the source's claim and does not verify it by re-evaluating pushed filters. This is why well-tested pushdown implementation is essential for custom data sources. GridUnion's SCADA data source pushdown implementation was tested with a suite of 50 queries that verify row counts with and without pushdown are identical, catching the bug where the BETWEEN filter was pushed incorrectly on timestamps with timezone offsets.
Statistics Reporting

The Scan.estimateStatistics() method returns a Statistics object containing the estimated byte size and row count of the scan's output (after pushdown and pruning). The CBO uses these estimates for join strategy selection and join ordering (Module 2). A data source that reports inaccurate statistics causes the same compounding estimation errors described in Module 2, lesson 2. GridUnion's SCADA data source initially reported the raw file size without accounting for pushdown filters, causing the CBO to overestimate the SCADA side of every join and choose sort-merge instead of broadcast. After fixing the estimateStatistics() method to account for the pushed predicates' selectivity, the CBO correctly chose broadcast for SCADA joins, reducing the join stage duration by 85%. The lesson: statistics reporting is not an optional V2 feature — it is the mechanism that enables the CBO to make correct decisions about joins involving the custom source.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: The DataSource V2 API is a negotiation protocol between Spark and the storage layer. Spark proposes optimizations (push this filter, prune these columns, estimate your size). The source accepts or rejects each proposal. The quality of this negotiation determines the performance of every query on the source. A well-implemented V2 source that supports pushdown, reports accurate statistics, and partitions data for parallelism performs like native Parquet. A poorly implemented source that accepts no pushdown, reports no statistics, and returns all data in a single partition performs like a sequential file scan — regardless of how well the execution engine is tuned. When a custom data source performs poorly, the diagnostic starts at the V2 API boundary: which optimizations did the source accept, and are its statistics accurate?

The mid-level track used Delta Lake as a feature-rich table format: ACID transactions, schema enforcement, time travel, MERGE operations. This lesson goes inside the implementation to understand how Delta achieves these features and what their performance costs are. Delta Lake stores data as Parquet files and adds a transaction log (the _delta_log directory) that records every change to the table. Each change — a file addition, a file removal, a schema change, or a metadata update — is recorded as a JSON entry in a sequentially numbered log file (000000000000000000.json, 000000000000000001.json, etc.). The transaction log is the single source of truth: to determine the current state of the table, a reader replays the log from the beginning (or from the most recent checkpoint) and constructs the list of active files. This log-based architecture provides atomicity (a failed write leaves no partial log entry), isolation (concurrent writers are serialized through optimistic concurrency), and durability (the log and the Parquet files are stored on cloud storage).
The Transaction Log Format

# Delta table directory structure
silver/readings/
├── _delta_log/
│   ├── 00000000000000000000.json    # Version 0: initial write
│   ├── 00000000000000000001.json    # Version 1: append
│   ├── 00000000000000000002.json    # Version 2: OPTIMIZE compaction
│   ├── ...
│   ├── 00000000000000000010.checkpoint.parquet  # Checkpoint at version 10
│   └── _last_checkpoint                # Points to the latest checkpoint
├── country=DE/date=2026-03-01/part-00000-abc123.parquet
├── country=DE/date=2026-03-01/part-00001-def456.parquet
└── ...

# Each JSON log entry contains actions:
# {"add":    {"path": "country=DE/date=2026-03-01/part-00000-abc123.parquet",
#             "size": 268435456, "partitionValues": {"country": "DE", "date": "2026-03-01"},
#             "stats": {"numRecords": 450000, "minValues": {"daily_kw": 0.1}, ...}}}
# {"remove": {"path": "old-file.parquet", "deletionTimestamp": 1711094400000}}
# {"metaData": {"schemaString": "...", "partitionColumns": ["country", "date"]}}
# {"commitInfo": {"timestamp": ..., "operation": "WRITE", "operationMetrics": {...}}}
Checkpointing

Replaying the entire transaction log from version 0 would be prohibitively slow for a table with thousands of versions. Delta addresses this with checkpoints: every 10 versions (configurable via delta.checkpointInterval), Delta writes a checkpoint file that contains the complete table state at that version — the full list of active files with their statistics. A reader loading the table starts from the most recent checkpoint and replays only the log entries after the checkpoint. For GridUnion's Silver meter readings table with 1,200+ versions (one per daily batch plus compaction operations), the checkpoint reduces the log replay from 1,200 JSON files to ~10 JSON files (the versions since the last checkpoint). The checkpoint itself is a Parquet file (for efficient columnar access to file-level statistics), enabling the reader to perform data skipping on the checkpoint during partition pruning: only the metadata for partitions that match the query's filter is read from the checkpoint.
Conflict Detection

When two writers attempt to modify the same table concurrently, Delta's optimistic concurrency control detects the conflict. Each writer reads the current table version (the latest log entry), performs its write, and attempts to commit by writing the next log version. If another writer has committed a version in the meantime, the commit fails and the writer must retry: it re-reads the new table version, checks whether its changes conflict with the intervening commits, and if not, retries the commit with the updated version number. Conflicts occur when two writers modify overlapping partitions or when a MERGE's match condition could produce different results depending on the other writer's changes. GridUnion's daily batch and streaming pipelines write to different partitions of the same Silver table (the batch writes yesterday's final data, the streaming writes today's near-real-time data), so they never conflict despite running concurrently. The billing team's end-of-month correction job, which updates historical partitions, occasionally conflicts with the compaction job's OPTIMIZE on the same partitions. The retry mechanism resolves these conflicts automatically within 1–2 retries.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Delta's transaction log is the mechanism that elevates Parquet from a file format to a table format. Without the log, a directory of Parquet files has no schema enforcement (any file with any schema can be placed in the directory), no atomicity (a failed write leaves partial files), no isolation (concurrent writes can produce inconsistent state), and no versioning (there is no concept of 'the previous version' of the table). The log adds each of these properties at the cost of an additional I/O per read (loading the log) and per write (appending to the log). For GridUnion's Silver table, the log read takes approximately 200ms (reading 10 JSON files since the last checkpoint). The 200ms cost buys ACID transactions, schema enforcement, time travel, and MERGE operations — capabilities that would otherwise require a database engine.

sections.title: Iceberg and Hudi — Table Format Comparison

blocks.content: Delta Lake is not the only table format. Apache Iceberg and Apache Hudi provide similar capabilities (ACID transactions, schema evolution, time travel) with different architectures and trade-offs. At enterprise scale, the table format choice affects every layer of the platform: which engines can read the data (Spark, Trino, Flink, Hive), which features are available (partition evolution, merge-on-read, incremental processing), and which ecosystem integrations exist (catalog services, governance tools, monitoring). GridUnion standardized on Delta Lake for the combined platform, but the Dutch subsidiary (DutchGrid Analytics) had been using Apache Hudi for their streaming pipeline, and the migration to Delta was one of the most complex integration tasks in the post-acquisition period. This lesson compares the three formats at the architectural level — not the feature-list level that marketing materials provide, but the trade-off level that affects operational decisions.
Architectural Comparison

rows: Metadata storage
Transaction log (JSON files + Parquet checkpoints) in the table directory
Metadata files (Avro) + manifest files (Avro) in the table directory, catalog pointer
Timeline (Avro) + metadata files in .hoodie directory
ACID mechanism
Optimistic concurrency with file-level conflict detection
Optimistic concurrency with snapshot isolation and manifest-level tracking
Optimistic concurrency with timeline-based conflict resolution
Partition evolution
Not supported natively (requires repartitioning the table)
Supported: change partition scheme without rewriting data. New partitions use the new scheme; old partitions keep the old scheme.
Supported via Hudi's metadata-based partition management
Schema evolution
mergeSchema (additive) and overwriteSchema (breaking)
Full schema evolution: add, rename, reorder, promote types. Column ID-based tracking survives renames.
Schema evolution with payload-based resolution
Merge-on-read
Not supported (copy-on-write only for MERGE operations)
Supported: writes produce delta files that are merged during reads
Core feature: MoR tables write delta logs that are compacted asynchronously
Engine support
Best on Spark/Databricks. Growing support in Trino, Flink, Hive.
Broadest engine support: Spark, Trino, Flink, Hive, Dremio, Snowflake, BigQuery
Good Spark support. Growing Trino/Flink. Hive support via Hudi's Hive sync.
Ecosystem integration
Deep Databricks integration (Unity Catalog, Photon, serverless). Open-source standalone.
Catalog-centric: designed around the Iceberg catalog API. Integrates with Hive Metastore, AWS Glue, Nessie.
Hudi-centric tooling: Hudi CLI, Hudi utilities for ingestion and compaction.

blocks.content: GridUnion’s Standardization Decision

GridUnion chose Delta Lake for three reasons. First, the platform runs on Databricks, which provides first-class Delta support with performance optimizations (Photon engine, predictive I/O) that are not available for Iceberg or Hudi on the same platform. Second, Unity Catalog's integration with Delta (schema enforcement, column masking, row filters, audit logging) provides the governance capabilities that Module 1 and Module 5 described — capabilities that would require additional tooling with Iceberg or Hudi. Third, Delta's simplicity: the copy-on-write model and the JSON-based transaction log are easier to debug and maintain than Hudi's merge-on-read compaction cycle or Iceberg's multi-level metadata hierarchy. The trade-off: Delta lacks Iceberg's partition evolution (a significant limitation addressed by the repartitioning techniques in Module 3, lesson 8) and lacks Hudi's merge-on-read (a performance optimization for write-heavy streaming tables that GridUnion mitigates with frequent OPTIMIZE compaction). The decision was documented in an ADR with the condition that GridUnion re-evaluates annually as the formats evolve.

The DutchGrid subsidiary's Hudi migration was driven not by Hudi's inadequacy but by the operational cost of maintaining two table formats on the same platform: two sets of maintenance jobs (Delta's OPTIMIZE/VACUUM vs Hudi's compaction/archival), two metadata management systems, two sets of reader configurations, and two teams' worth of operational expertise. Converging on one format reduced the platform team's operational burden by approximately 40% for the Dutch tables. The migration followed the dual-read approach (Module 3, lesson 7's view abstraction pattern adapted for format migration): a nightly job read the Hudi table and wrote it as Delta, and consumers were migrated one at a time to the Delta copy. The migration completed in 8 weeks.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Table format selection is a platform decision, not a pipeline decision. The format affects every tool, every team, and every operational process on the platform. Standardizing on one format eliminates the operational overhead of maintaining multiple formats, even if the chosen format is not technically superior in every dimension. The decision criteria should be weighted toward operational simplicity (one format to maintain, one set of tools to learn, one set of maintenance jobs to schedule) and ecosystem integration (does the format work well with the platform's catalog, engine, and governance tools?) rather than toward feature superiority in any single dimension. A format that is 80% as capable but 100% integrated with the platform is a better choice than a format that is 100% capable but requires a parallel operational stack.

Cloud object stores (S3, ADLS, GCS) are the dominant storage layer for Spark-based data platforms, but their performance characteristics differ fundamentally from local filesystems and HDFS. Understanding these characteristics is essential for tuning Spark's interaction with cloud storage. The three key differences: latency (a single GET request to ADLS takes 20–50ms compared to sub-millisecond for local disk), throughput scaling (cloud storage scales throughput by parallelism — 10 concurrent requests provide 10x the throughput of 1 request, up to the account's throttling limit), and metadata operations (listing a directory with 100,000 files requires 20 LIST requests at 50–200ms each, totaling 1–4 seconds). These characteristics mean that the file layout (number of files, file sizes, directory depth) affects performance as much as the data volume, and that the committer algorithm (how Spark finalizes a write) has a dramatic impact on write reliability and performance.
The Output Committer Problem

When Spark writes to cloud storage, each task writes its output to a temporary location. When all tasks complete, the committer moves the files to their final locations. On HDFS, this move is a metadata-only rename operation (instant). On cloud storage, there is no rename: the committer must copy each file to its final location and delete the temporary copy. For a write producing 200 files, the commit phase performs 200 copies and 200 deletes — 400 operations at 20–50ms each = 8–20 seconds of commit overhead, during which the write is not yet visible to readers. The default FileOutputCommitter (version 1) is worse: it writes to a temporary directory, commits each task's output individually, and then commits the entire job by moving files to the final directory. If the job fails during the commit phase, partial files may be left in the final directory. The DBIO committer (Databricks) and the S3A committer (Hadoop 3.3+) solve this by writing directly to the final path and using the Delta transaction log (or a manifest file) for atomicity, eliminating the copy-and-delete overhead.
Cloud Storage Tuning for Spark

# GridUnion's ADLS performance tuning for Spark

# 1. Increase read parallelism per executor (ADLS scales throughput with parallelism)
spark.conf.set('spark.hadoop.fs.azure.read.request.size', '8388608')    # 8MB read chunks
spark.conf.set('spark.hadoop.fs.azure.readahead.range', '16777216')     # 16MB readahead

# 2. Optimize listing operations (reduce the cost of directory enumeration)
spark.conf.set('spark.hadoop.fs.azure.list.max.results', '5000')        # max results per LIST
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.sources.parallelPartitionDiscovery.threshold', '32')  # parallel listing

# 3. Use the Delta log for file discovery instead of directory listing
# Delta tables enumerate files from the transaction log, not from directory listing.
# This makes Delta reads O(log_entries) instead of O(num_files) for file discovery.
# No configuration needed — this is automatic when reading format('delta').

# 4. Connection pool tuning (prevent connection exhaustion on high-parallelism clusters)
spark.conf.set('spark.hadoop.fs.azure.io.retry.max.retries', '5')
spark.conf.set('spark.hadoop.fs.azure.io.retry.backoff.interval', '1000')  # 1 second backoff

# 5. Write committer (avoid the copy-rename overhead)
# On Databricks, the DBIO committer is used automatically for Delta writes.
# On open-source Spark + ADLS, configure the committer explicitly:
spark.conf.set('spark.hadoop.mapreduce.outputcommitter.factory.scheme.abfss',
    'org.apache.hadoop.fs.azurebfs.commit.AzureBlobFileSystemCommitterFactory')

GridUnion's ADLS tuning produced a 35% reduction in scan latency (from readahead and parallel reads), a 60% reduction in file discovery time (from using Delta's transaction log instead of directory listing), and a 75% reduction in write commit time (from the optimized committer). The tuning was applied platform-wide through the shared Spark configuration repository (Module 1, lesson 7) and validated with a benchmark suite that measures read throughput, write throughput, and listing latency on a standard 10GB test dataset. The benchmark runs nightly and alerts on performance regressions, catching the ADLS SDK regression in version 12.18 that increased listing latency by 40% (the team pinned the SDK version until the fix was released).

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Cloud storage performance is a function of the API interaction pattern, not the raw bandwidth. A single sequential read stream from ADLS achieves 100–200MB/s. Ten parallel read streams from the same file achieve 1–2GB/s. But 10,000 small-file reads achieve only 50–100MB/s because the per-request latency (20–50ms) dominates the transfer time. The file layout determines the API pattern: large files enable high-throughput parallel streaming, small files force high-latency sequential requests. Every decision in this track — file size targets (Module 3), compaction (Module 3), partition count (mid-level Module 2), and codec selection (this module, lesson 2) — ultimately affects the cloud storage API pattern and therefore the I/O performance.

GridUnion's Silver meter readings table contains meter_address and customer_name columns classified as PII under GDPR. The regulatory requirement is that these columns are accessible only to authorized teams (the billing team and the regulatory team) and are unreadable by unauthorized teams (the analytics team and the ML team) even if those teams have SELECT access to the table. The mid-level track's access control (GRANT/REVOKE at the schema level) is too coarse: granting SELECT on the schema gives access to all columns, including PII. Two approaches provide column-level protection: column encryption (the PII columns are encrypted at rest, and only teams with the decryption key can read them) and column masking (the PII columns are dynamically replaced with masked values for unauthorized readers). Both approaches have performance implications, implementation complexity, and operational trade-offs that this lesson covers.
Parquet Modular Encryption

Parquet Modular Encryption (PME) encrypts individual column chunks within the Parquet file using AES-GCM-256. Each column can have its own encryption key, and the footer can be encrypted separately. A reader with the column key can decrypt and read the column; a reader without the key sees encrypted bytes that are unreadable. The encryption is transparent to Spark: the Parquet reader handles decryption automatically when the key is available. The performance impact is approximately 5–8% overhead for encrypted columns (AES-GCM encryption and decryption) and zero overhead for non-encrypted columns in the same file. GridUnion evaluated PME for the meter_address and customer_name columns but chose not to adopt it because the key management complexity (distributing decryption keys to authorized teams, rotating keys, handling key loss) exceeded the benefit for their specific threat model. PME is appropriate when the data files may be accessed outside the platform (for example, files shared with external partners or stored on less-trusted storage), where access control alone is insufficient.
Column Masking via Unity Catalog

-- GridUnion's column masking implementation using Unity Catalog
-- Authorized teams see the real value; unauthorized teams see the masked value

-- Create a masking function
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION gridunion_prod.functions.mask_pii(value STRING)
RETURNS STRING
RETURN CASE
    WHEN is_member('billing_team') OR is_member('regulatory_team')
    THEN value
    ELSE CONCAT(LEFT(value, 2), '***', RIGHT(value, 2))
END;

-- Apply the mask to the column
ALTER TABLE gridunion_prod.silver_de.meter_readings
ALTER COLUMN meter_address
SET MASK gridunion_prod.functions.mask_pii;

ALTER TABLE gridunion_prod.silver_de.meter_readings
ALTER COLUMN customer_name
SET MASK gridunion_prod.functions.mask_pii;

-- Result:
-- Billing team query:    SELECT customer_name FROM ... → 'Maria Schmidt'
-- Analytics team query:  SELECT customer_name FROM ... → 'Ma***dt'
-- The mask is applied dynamically at query time. The stored data is unmasked.

GridUnion chose column masking over encryption because it provides the required access control without the key management burden. The masking function is evaluated at query time: authorized teams see the real value, unauthorized teams see the masked value. The performance overhead is minimal (one function call per row for the masked columns) and is only incurred when the column is actually selected (column pruning skips masked columns that are not in the query's SELECT list). The trade-off: masking protects against unauthorized query access but does not protect against unauthorized file access. If an unauthorized user downloads the raw Parquet file from ADLS (bypassing Unity Catalog), they can read the unmasked PII. GridUnion mitigates this with ADLS-level access control: only the Spark cluster's service principal can read the Silver files, and individual users cannot access ADLS directly. This layered approach (Unity Catalog masking for query access + ADLS IAM for file access) provides defense in depth without the complexity of column encryption.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Column-level protection exists on a spectrum from simple (column masking via views) to complex (column encryption with per-column keys). The choice depends on the threat model: who are you protecting against? If the threat is unauthorized queries by authenticated platform users (the analytics team querying PII they should not see), column masking is sufficient and operationally simple. If the threat is unauthorized file access by actors outside the platform (a data breach, a partner receiving files with PII), column encryption is necessary because masking only applies at query time and does not protect the stored files. Most enterprise platforms start with masking (simpler, covers the internal threat) and add encryption later if the external threat model demands it.

Enterprise data platforms accumulate data in multiple formats over time: the original team used plain Parquet, the acquired subsidiary used Hudi, a third team ingested CSV from vendors, and the platform standard is Delta Lake. Migrating all data to the standard format is essential for operational simplicity (one set of maintenance jobs, one set of monitoring tools, one optimization strategy) but involves significant risk: format migration touches every byte of the table's data, every consumer's read path, and the table's metadata lineage. A botched migration can corrupt data, break consumers, or lose the transaction history. This lesson covers the three migration strategies — in-place conversion, dual-read migration, and incremental migration — with trade-offs and the consumer coordination required for each.
Three Migration Strategies

rows: In-place conversion (CONVERT TO DELTA)
Delta's convertToDelta() reads the existing Parquet/Hudi files, creates a Delta transaction log that references them (no data copy), and writes the initial checkpoint.
Seconds (metadata only)
Low for Parquet→Delta (no data change). Higher for Hudi→Delta (Hudi's compaction state is not preserved).
Used for all plain Parquet tables. 150 tables converted in 2 days.
Dual-read migration
A nightly job reads the source format and writes to a new Delta table. Consumers switch to the Delta table one at a time. The source table is decommissioned after all consumers migrate.
Zero (both tables available during migration)
Low (consumers switch on their own schedule; rollback = switch back to source)
Used for the DutchGrid Hudi→Delta migration. 8 weeks from start to decommission.
Incremental migration
New data writes to Delta. Historical data remains in the old format. A view unions the two sources. Historical data is migrated incrementally (one partition per day) until complete.
Zero (view provides a unified interface throughout)
Medium (the view must handle schema differences between the old and new format)
Used for the SCADA proprietary format migration where the full table was too large to convert in one batch.

blocks.content: GridUnion’s Hudi-to-Delta Migration

The DutchGrid subsidiary had 8 Hudi tables totaling 1.2TB, used by 3 Dutch pipelines and 2 cross-country pipelines. The migration used the dual-read strategy: a nightly job read each Hudi table, applied the GridUnion schema standards (column naming conventions, type standardization), and wrote to a parallel Delta table. Both tables were available during the migration period. The Dutch pipelines switched to the Delta tables over 4 weeks (one pipeline per week, with validation after each switch). The cross-country pipelines switched in weeks 5–6. The Hudi tables were decommissioned in week 8 after a final validation confirmed that no pipeline was reading from them. The migration's most complex aspect was not the data copy but the metadata alignment: Hudi's timeline-based versioning had to be mapped to Delta's transaction log versioning for the audit trail, and Hudi's merge-on-read delta logs had to be compacted before conversion (Delta does not support merge-on-read). The compaction step took 4 hours on a dedicated cluster and was the migration's most resource-intensive operation.

The migration produced two unexpected benefits beyond format standardization. First, the Delta tables were 18% smaller than the Hudi tables because the compaction and rewrite used ZSTD compression (GridUnion's standard) instead of Hudi's default Gzip, and the GridUnion schema standard eliminated 3 unused columns that the Dutch tables had accumulated over time. Second, the Delta tables were 25% faster to read because the Delta transaction log's checkpoint mechanism provided faster file enumeration than Hudi's timeline-based listing on ADLS. These benefits were not the primary motivation for the migration (operational simplicity was) but they demonstrated that format standardization pays for itself beyond the operational cost reduction.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Format migration is a platform lifecycle event, not a one-time project. Every acquisition, every vendor change, and every technology evolution introduces new formats. The dual-read strategy is the safest general-purpose approach because it introduces zero downtime, allows consumer-by-consumer migration on each team's schedule, and provides instant rollback (switch back to the source table). The key to smooth migration is consumer discovery: knowing every pipeline, report, and notebook that reads from the source table, and coordinating their switch to the new table. The data product manifest (Module 1, lesson 5) and the consumer registration (Module 5, lesson 3) are the tools that make this discovery possible. Without them, the migration team must manually hunt for consumers, and the decommission step risks breaking unknown dependencies.

This lesson synthesizes the module into GridUnion's complete storage architecture: the standard format, the Parquet configuration, the encoding and compression standards, the predicate evaluation strategy, the encryption and masking policy, the cloud storage tuning, and the format governance that ensures consistency across 4 countries, 6 teams, and 200+ tables. The architecture is not a static document — it evolves annually as the table formats add features (Delta's liquid clustering, Iceberg's row-level deletes), as cloud storage services improve (ADLS's new caching layer, S3 Express One Zone), and as the data volume grows (8TB today, projected 20TB in 2 years). Each evolution is evaluated against the same criteria: operational simplicity, ecosystem integration, and the total cost of migration.
The Storage Standards

rows: Table format
Delta Lake (all new tables)
Native Unity Catalog integration, Databricks performance optimizations, operational simplicity
Compression codec
ZSTD
25% smaller files than Snappy with 5% slower reads. Storage cost savings exceed the read overhead.
Row group size
128MB (default), 64MB (wide schemas > 100 columns)
Balance between predicate selectivity and metadata overhead. Smaller for wide schemas where per-column statistics benefit from narrower ranges.
Bloom filters
Enabled for high-cardinality join key columns (meter_id, turbine_id)
Page-level skipping for point lookups. 1% file size overhead for 40–60% scan reduction.
Column masking
Applied to all PII columns via Unity Catalog mask functions
Authorized teams see real values; others see masked. Simpler than encryption for the internal threat model.
Cloud storage tuning
ADLS with readahead=16MB, parallel reads, optimized committer
35% scan reduction, 60% faster file discovery, 75% faster write commits.
Compaction schedule
Daily bin-pack (recent partitions), weekly Z-ORDER (full table)
Prevents small-file accumulation from streaming. Z-ORDER refreshes clustering for region-based queries.
Vacuum retention
30 days (Silver), 90 days (Gold), 7 years (regulatory audit tables)
Balance between time travel availability and storage cost. Regulatory tables retain full history.

blocks.content: What Comes Next

Modules 6–7 complete the deep internals phase of the senior-level track. You now understand the Spark execution engine (DAGScheduler, TaskScheduler, shuffle, memory management, dynamic allocation, fault tolerance, multi-tenant scheduling) and the storage layer (Parquet internals, encoding, predicate evaluation, DataSource V2, Delta Lake internals, table format comparison, cloud storage, encryption). Together, these two modules provide the implementation-level understanding needed to diagnose any performance or reliability issue on the platform. Module 8 shifts to the enterprise operations phase with multi-tenant operations and security: Unity Catalog deep dive, row-level and column-level security, audit logging, secret management, network isolation, GDPR data deletion, and the compliance frameworks that govern data access in regulated industries. Where Module 7 addressed how data is stored and accessed at the physical level, Module 8 addresses who can access it and under what governance controls.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Storage architecture is the foundation layer of the data platform: every query, every pipeline, and every consumer interacts with the storage layer on every operation. The standards from this module — Delta as the format, ZSTD as the codec, bloom filters for join keys, column masking for PII, optimized committers for writes — apply to every table on the platform. A 1% improvement in storage efficiency, applied to 200 tables read by 50 pipelines daily, compounds into significant cost and latency savings. The governance that enforces these standards (the storage standards table above, validated in CI, audited quarterly) ensures that the improvements are realized platform-wide rather than in the few tables whose owners happen to have read this documentation.

chapters.title: Multi-Tenant Operations and Security
chapters.description: Design and operate a secure, multi-tenant Spark platform — Unity Catalog deep dive, row-level and column-level security, audit logging, data masking, and the compliance frameworks that govern data access in regulated industries.

blocks.content: Module 6, lesson 9 covered multi-tenant cluster scheduling: YARN queues, fair scheduling, and preemption. This lesson zooms out from the cluster level to the platform level: how should a multi-team data platform be architected for isolation? The three models — shared cluster with namespace isolation, dedicated clusters per team, and hybrid — each make different trade-offs between cost efficiency (fewer clusters = lower infrastructure cost), isolation strength (more separation = stronger security boundaries), and operational complexity (more clusters = more infrastructure to manage). The choice affects not just scheduling but also security (can one team's code access another team's data?), failure isolation (does one team's runaway query affect another team's pipeline?), and governance (can the platform team enforce standards across all teams?). GridUnion's post-acquisition platform needed to support 6 teams with different security requirements, different regulatory regimes, and different workload patterns while keeping infrastructure costs manageable.
The Three Models

rows: Shared cluster
One cluster, all teams share compute and storage. Isolation via Unity Catalog namespaces and YARN queues.
Low — a runaway query can affect other teams. Data isolation depends on catalog privileges.
High — one cluster to manage, resources shared across teams.
Low — one set of infrastructure, one monitoring stack.
Small platforms (< 3 teams) where trust is high and regulatory requirements are minimal.
Dedicated clusters
One cluster per team (or per business unit). Each team has its own compute, storage, and catalog.
High — complete isolation. One team's failure cannot affect another team.
Low — each cluster has idle capacity that cannot be shared. N teams × minimum cluster size.
High — N clusters to manage, N monitoring stacks, N sets of configurations.
Regulated environments where data isolation must be physical (separate infrastructure, not just logical).
Hybrid
Shared development cluster + dedicated production clusters. Or: shared compute with isolated storage.
Medium — production workloads are isolated; development is shared.
Medium — production cost is per-team; development cost is shared.
Medium — a few clusters with differentiated configurations.
Most enterprise platforms. GridUnion's choice.

blocks.content: GridUnion’s Hybrid Architecture

GridUnion uses three clusters. The production cluster runs all scheduled pipelines (daily batch, streaming, monthly reprocessing) under YARN queue management (Module 6, lesson 9). All 6 teams submit production pipelines to this cluster, but the queue topology ensures priority isolation (billing before analytics). The analytics cluster runs interactive notebooks, ad-hoc queries, and ML training jobs. It uses dynamic allocation aggressively (min=2, max=64) because workloads are bursty and unpredictable. The development cluster is shared across all teams for pipeline development and testing. It has limited resources (16 nodes) and no SLA guarantees. The production and analytics clusters are in separate VNET subnets (lesson 7) with firewall rules that prevent cross-cluster network access. Unity Catalog provides the logical isolation: every cluster connects to the same metastore, but each team's privileges determine which schemas and tables they can access. The physical separation of production and analytics prevents a runaway notebook query from consuming resources that production pipelines need.

The hybrid model was chosen over dedicated-per-team clusters because the cost analysis showed that 6 dedicated clusters (one per team, each with minimum 8 nodes) would cost $320,000/year in baseline infrastructure, while the hybrid model (3 clusters totaling 128 nodes with dynamic allocation) costs $210,000/year — a 34% savings. The savings come from resource sharing: the billing team's production pipeline uses 32 executors for 7 minutes at 6 AM, and those same nodes run the ML team's training job at 2 AM. Dedicated clusters would hold those nodes idle for 23 hours a day. The cost analysis also considered the operational overhead: 3 clusters require 3 sets of monitoring, 3 Spark version upgrades, and 3 security audits per year. Six clusters would require 6 of each. The operational burden is proportional to the cluster count, not the node count.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: The multi-tenancy model is a security-cost trade-off, not a technical decision. A shared cluster is cheapest but provides the weakest isolation. Dedicated clusters provide the strongest isolation but at the highest cost. The hybrid model provides adequate isolation for most regulatory requirements at a cost between the two extremes. The decision depends on the platform's specific regulatory environment: if the regulator requires physical infrastructure isolation (some financial regulators do), dedicated clusters are mandatory regardless of cost. If the regulator accepts logical isolation via catalog privileges and audit logging (most energy regulators do), the hybrid model satisfies compliance at lower cost.

Module 1, lesson 2 introduced Unity Catalog's three-level namespace (catalog.schema.table) for organizing data. This lesson covers Unity Catalog at the administration level: the metastore architecture, the privilege model, the identity federation mechanism, and the operational concerns that platform administrators face when managing a 200-table catalog across 6 teams. The mid-level track treated Unity Catalog as a table registry. At senior scale, Unity Catalog is the central governance service that mediates every data access on the platform: every query, every write, every schema change, and every privilege grant passes through Unity Catalog. Understanding its architecture lets you design privilege hierarchies that prevent escalation, configure identity federation that maps corporate SSO groups to catalog privileges, and diagnose the access issues that are the most common support request on multi-team platforms.
The Privilege Model

Unity Catalog's privilege model follows the principle of least privilege with inheritance. Privileges are granted at four levels: catalog, schema, table, and function. A GRANT at the catalog level inherits to all schemas within the catalog; a GRANT at the schema level inherits to all tables within the schema. The available privileges are: SELECT (read data), MODIFY (write data), CREATE (create objects within the container), and ALL PRIVILEGES (all of the above plus GRANT to others). The USAGE privilege is the gateway: to access a table, a user must have USAGE on both the catalog and the schema, plus SELECT on the table (or the schema). Without USAGE, the catalog or schema is invisible to the user — they cannot even see that it exists. GridUnion's privilege structure uses schema-level grants as the primary mechanism (Module 1, lesson 3), with table-level grants only for exceptions (a single table in a schema that one specific team should not see).
Identity Federation

GridUnion's 6 teams are defined as groups in the corporate Azure Active Directory (AAD). Each group maps to a set of Unity Catalog privileges: the de_germany_team AAD group is granted ALL PRIVILEGES on the silver_de schema, SELECT on the gold_billing schema, and USAGE on the gridunion_prod catalog. When an engineer authenticates via SSO, their AAD group memberships are resolved by Unity Catalog's identity federation, and the corresponding privileges are applied to their session. This federation means that onboarding a new engineer requires only adding them to the correct AAD group — no Unity Catalog configuration change is needed. Offboarding removes the group membership, and the engineer's access is revoked at the next session. GridUnion's security policy requires that privilege changes go through AAD group management (reviewed by the team lead), not through direct Unity Catalog GRANT statements. Direct GRANTs are prohibited for individual users and audited weekly for compliance.
Preventing Privilege Escalation

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: The most dangerous privilege in Unity Catalog is not ALL PRIVILEGES — it is the GRANT OPTION. ALL PRIVILEGES lets a team read and write their own data. The GRANT OPTION lets a team delegate privileges to other teams, bypassing the platform team's access control design. If the de_germany_team can grant silver_de access to the ml_team, the platform team's intended isolation (ML does not access raw Silver data) is circumvented. GridUnion restricts the GRANT OPTION to the platform_admin_team only. Every privilege change flows through the platform team's access review process, not through individual teams' ad-hoc grants.

Module 1's namespace design uses separate schemas per country (silver_de, silver_fr) to provide country-level data isolation. But some tables must span countries: the Gold billing table joins German and French data for cross-country totals, and the Platinum analytics table aggregates all 4 countries for demand forecasting. These cross-country tables cannot be split into per-country schemas without losing the cross-country aggregation that justifies their existence. Row-level security (RLS) provides an alternative: a single table containing all countries' data, with row filters that restrict each team's view to the rows they are authorized to see. The German regulatory team sees only German rows. The French DE team sees only French rows. The billing team sees all rows (they need cross-country totals). The filters are applied transparently — the query looks the same, but the results differ based on the querying user's group membership.
Unity Catalog Row Filters

-- Create a row filter function for country-level isolation
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION gridunion_prod.functions.country_filter(country_col STRING)
RETURNS BOOLEAN
RETURN CASE
    -- Platform admin and billing team see all countries
    WHEN is_member('platform_admin_team') OR is_member('billing_team') THEN TRUE
    -- Each country's team sees only their country
    WHEN is_member('de_germany_team') AND country_col = 'DE' THEN TRUE
    WHEN is_member('de_france_team') AND country_col = 'FR' THEN TRUE
    WHEN is_member('de_netherlands_team') AND country_col = 'NL' THEN TRUE
    WHEN is_member('de_luxembourg_team') AND country_col = 'LU' THEN TRUE
    -- Analytics and ML teams see all countries (approved by governance)
    WHEN is_member('analytics_team') OR is_member('ml_team') THEN TRUE
    -- Default: deny
    ELSE FALSE
END;

-- Apply the row filter to the cross-country Gold billing table
ALTER TABLE gridunion_prod.gold_billing.cross_country_totals
SET ROW FILTER gridunion_prod.functions.country_filter ON (country);

-- Now:
-- German regulatory team queries: sees only DE rows
-- French DE team queries: sees only FR rows
-- Billing team queries: sees all rows (cross-country totals)
-- The filter is invisible to the query — no WHERE clause needed
Performance Implications

Row filters are evaluated for every row returned by the query. For a table with 10 million rows, the filter function executes 10 million times. The performance impact depends on the filter's complexity and whether Catalyst can push the filter into the scan. Unity Catalog's row filters are implemented as SQL functions that Catalyst can optimize: the CASE expression is compiled into a filter predicate that Catalyst evaluates alongside the query's own WHERE clause. If the row filter references a partition column (country is a partition column on GridUnion's tables), Catalyst combines the row filter with the query's partition filter and performs partition pruning: the German regulatory team's query prunes to country=DE partitions before reading any data, incurring zero runtime overhead for the row filter. If the row filter references a non-partition column, the filter is evaluated post-scan, adding approximately 5–10% overhead for simple conditions. GridUnion's row filters always reference the country column, which is a partition column on every table that uses RLS, ensuring that the performance impact is negligible.
The View-Based Alternative

Before Unity Catalog row filters were available, GridUnion implemented country-level isolation using security views: a view per team that included a WHERE country = 'DE' filter. The German regulatory team queried gridunion_prod.gold_billing.cross_country_totals_de (a view) instead of the base table. This approach works but has three limitations: each team needs a separate view (6 teams × 10 cross-country tables = 60 views to maintain), the views must be updated when new countries are added or team assignments change, and engineers can accidentally query the base table instead of the view (bypassing the filter). Unity Catalog row filters eliminate all three limitations: one base table with one filter function, the function's logic is updated centrally, and the filter is applied automatically regardless of how the table is accessed. GridUnion migrated from security views to row filters over 4 weeks, decommissioning 42 security views and replacing them with 10 row filter functions.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Row-level security is the mechanism that enables cross-domain tables without sacrificing country-level isolation. The alternative — per-country tables for every cross-country analysis — fragments the data model and makes cross-country queries require explicit UNION ALL across country tables. RLS provides a unified table with an invisible filter that each user's access context determines. The key design decision is the filter function: it must reference a partition column for zero-overhead enforcement, it must cover every team explicitly (no implicit 'else allow'), and it must default to deny (unknown groups see zero rows). GridUnion's filter was reviewed by the security team and the governance committee before deployment.

Module 7, lesson 8 introduced column masking at the storage level. This lesson covers the full column-level security architecture: the masking functions, the governance policies that determine which columns are masked and for whom, the testing strategy that validates masking behavior, and the operational monitoring that detects masking bypasses. Column-level security is GridUnion's primary GDPR compliance mechanism for data-in-use: it ensures that PII columns (meter_address, customer_name, contact_email) are readable only by authorized teams and appear as masked values to everyone else. The security is transparent to the consumer: the same query returns real values for the billing team and masked values for the analytics team, with no code difference. The masking is enforced at the catalog level, so it applies regardless of how the data is accessed — through notebooks, pipelines, JDBC connections, or SQL endpoints.
The Masking Policy

rows: customer_name
PII — Personal name
First 2 chars + *** + last 2 chars
billing_team, regulatory_de_team
'Ma***dt' (original: 'Maria Schmidt')
meter_address
PII — Physical address
Street number only, rest replaced
billing_team, regulatory_de_team
'42 ***' (original: '42 Friedrichstraße, Berlin')
contact_email
PII — Email address
Local part first char + ***@domain
billing_team
'm***@gridunion.de' (original: 'mschmidt@gridunion.de')
meter_id
Quasi-identifier
Not masked (needed for joins)
All teams
'DE-BERLIN-00042' (unchanged)
daily_kw
Non-PII — Consumption data
Not masked
All teams with table access
'342.5' (unchanged)

blocks.content: Testing Masking Behavior

# GridUnion's masking validation test suite
# Runs nightly in the security test environment

import pytest

def test_billing_team_sees_real_pii(spark, billing_team_session):
    """Billing team should see unmasked PII values."""
    df = billing_team_session.sql(
        "SELECT customer_name FROM gridunion_prod.silver_de.meter_readings LIMIT 1"
    )
    value = df.collect()[0]['customer_name']
    # Real names do not contain '***'
    assert '***' not in value, f"Billing team sees masked value: {value}"

def test_analytics_team_sees_masked_pii(spark, analytics_team_session):
    """Analytics team should see masked PII values."""
    df = analytics_team_session.sql(
        "SELECT customer_name FROM gridunion_prod.silver_de.meter_readings LIMIT 1"
    )
    value = df.collect()[0]['customer_name']
    # Masked names contain '***'
    assert '***' in value, f"Analytics team sees unmasked value: {value}"

def test_masking_applies_to_all_access_paths(spark, analytics_team_session):
    """Masking must apply regardless of access method."""
    # Direct table read
    df1 = analytics_team_session.table('gridunion_prod.silver_de.meter_readings')
    # SQL query
    df2 = analytics_team_session.sql(
        'SELECT * FROM gridunion_prod.silver_de.meter_readings LIMIT 1'
    )
    # Both should show masked values
    for df in [df1, df2]:
        row = df.limit(1).collect()[0]
        assert '***' in row['customer_name'], \
            f"Masking bypassed via access path: {row['customer_name']}"

The masking test suite runs nightly using service accounts that impersonate each team's access level. The tests validate two properties: positive access (authorized teams see real values) and negative access (unauthorized teams see masked values). The test suite also validates that masking applies to all access paths: direct table reads, SQL queries, view-based queries, and JDBC connections. A masking bypass — where an unauthorized team sees real PII through an unexpected access path — is a GDPR compliance violation. GridUnion discovered one bypass during the initial deployment: a view that was created before the masking was applied cached the unmasked data in its definition, and subsequent queries through the view returned unmasked PII. The fix was to recreate the view after the masking was enabled. The test suite now includes a view-access test for every masked table.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Column masking is a policy enforcement mechanism that must be tested as rigorously as any production pipeline. A masking function that appears to work in manual testing but fails through a specific access path (a view, a JDBC connection, a cross-catalog query) creates a false sense of security that is worse than no masking at all. The nightly test suite is the verification mechanism that converts the masking policy from a documented intention to a verified control. Without automated testing, masking compliance degrades through configuration drift: new views, new access paths, and new column additions that are not covered by the masking functions.

Every data access event on the GridUnion platform must be logged for regulatory compliance. The German energy regulator (BNetzA) requires an audit trail that answers: who accessed which table, when, and what operation did they perform? The French regulator (CRE) additionally requires that the audit trail capture the query text for any access to customer-related tables. These requirements cannot be met by application-level logging (the pipeline's print statements) because they must cover every access path: notebooks, pipelines, JDBC connections, SQL endpoints, and ad-hoc queries. Unity Catalog's system audit log captures all access events at the catalog level, providing the comprehensive coverage that application-level logging cannot achieve. This lesson covers the audit infrastructure: what events to capture, where to store the audit data, how to query it for compliance reporting, and how to monitor it for security anomalies.
The Audit Event Schema

rows: Table access (SELECT)
User, table FQN, timestamp, access method (notebook/pipeline/JDBC), row count returned
7 years
BNetzA: who accessed meter data and when. CRE: query text for customer tables.
Table modification (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE)
User, table FQN, timestamp, operation type, rows affected, Delta version before/after
7 years
BNetzA: audit trail for every data change. SOX: change accountability for financial reporting tables.
Schema change
User, table FQN, timestamp, old schema, new schema, change type (add/drop/alter)
7 years
Module 5 schema governance: every schema change traceable to an authorized request.
Privilege change (GRANT/REVOKE)
User, grantee, privilege, object FQN, timestamp
7 years
BNetzA: access control changes must be traceable. GDPR: data access scope changes logged.
Authentication event
User, authentication method (SSO/token), timestamp, success/failure
2 years
General security: failed authentication attempts may indicate unauthorized access.
Masking evaluation
User, table, column, mask result (masked/unmasked), timestamp
3 years
GDPR: prove that PII masking was applied correctly for each access.

blocks.content: Querying the Audit Log

-- GridUnion's audit log is stored in an immutable Delta table
-- No UPDATE or DELETE is permitted; VACUUM retention is 7 years

-- Who accessed the German Silver meter readings in the last 30 days?
SELECT 
    user_name,
    event_type,
    COUNT(*) as access_count,
    MIN(event_timestamp) as first_access,
    MAX(event_timestamp) as last_access
FROM gridunion_prod.monitoring.audit_log
WHERE table_fqn = 'gridunion_prod.silver_de.meter_readings'
    AND event_timestamp >= current_date() - INTERVAL 30 DAYS
GROUP BY user_name, event_type
ORDER BY access_count DESC;

-- Detect anomalous access patterns: users accessing tables outside their normal scope
SELECT 
    user_name,
    table_fqn,
    COUNT(*) as access_count
FROM gridunion_prod.monitoring.audit_log
WHERE event_timestamp >= current_date() - INTERVAL 7 DAYS
    AND event_type = 'TABLE_ACCESS'
    AND table_fqn NOT IN (
        -- Tables the user normally accesses (baseline from the previous 90 days)
        SELECT DISTINCT table_fqn 
        FROM gridunion_prod.monitoring.audit_log 
        WHERE user_name = outer_log.user_name
            AND event_timestamp BETWEEN current_date() - INTERVAL 97 DAYS 
                                     AND current_date() - INTERVAL 7 DAYS
    )
GROUP BY user_name, table_fqn
HAVING access_count > 5  -- more than 5 accesses to a previously-unaccessed table
ORDER BY access_count DESC;

The anomaly detection query compares each user's current week's access pattern against their 90-day baseline. If a user accesses a table they have never accessed before (or rarely accessed), the query flags it for security review. GridUnion runs this query daily and sends the results to the security team's review queue. Most flagged accesses are legitimate (a new team member exploring the catalog, an analyst working on a new project), but approximately 5% are policy violations (an engineer using a personal account instead of their team's service account, or a notebook that was shared with the wrong team). The daily anomaly review takes 15 minutes and has caught 3 genuine policy violations in 12 months, each of which was remediated within 24 hours.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Audit logging serves two distinct purposes that require different retention and query patterns. Compliance logging answers the regulator's question: 'prove that access controls were enforced on date X for user Y.' This requires long retention (7 years), immutability, and queryability by user+table+date. Security monitoring answers the platform team's question: 'is anyone accessing data they should not?' This requires short-lookback anomaly detection with baseline comparison. Both purposes use the same audit data but different query patterns. GridUnion stores one audit table and builds two views: the compliance view (optimized for point-in-time queries by regulator request) and the security view (optimized for rolling-window anomaly detection by the security team).

GridUnion's pipelines access 7 external systems: PostgreSQL (customer CRM), Oracle (regulatory codes), 3 vendor SFTP servers (meter data), a REST API (grid topology), and Azure Key Vault (encryption keys). Each system requires credentials: database passwords, SSH keys, API tokens, and Key Vault access policies. The mid-level track stored credentials in environment-specific YAML files. At enterprise scale, YAML-stored credentials create three problems: credentials in files are visible to anyone with repository access (a GitHub permission change exposes production passwords), credentials in files cannot be rotated without redeploying the pipeline (a password rotation requires a code change, a PR review, and a deployment), and credentials in files have no audit trail (there is no log of who accessed the credential or when). Secret management systems solve these problems by storing credentials in a dedicated, access-controlled vault with rotation support and access logging.
Secret Management Options

rows: Databricks Secret Scopes
Native: dbutils.secrets.get(scope, key). Secrets are redacted in logs and notebooks.
Manual rotation via API. No automatic rotation.
Access logged in Unity Catalog audit log.
Primary for all Databricks-native credentials (ADLS keys, JDBC passwords).
Azure Key Vault
Via Key Vault-backed secret scopes or direct SDK access.
Automatic rotation with rotation policies. Integrates with Azure AD.
Full access log in Key Vault audit events.
Used for credentials that must auto-rotate (encryption keys, service principal secrets).
HashiCorp Vault
Via Vault SDK or environment variables injected by the orchestrator.
Automatic rotation with lease-based secrets. Dynamic database credentials.
Comprehensive audit backend.
Evaluated but not adopted (Databricks secret scopes + Key Vault cover GridUnion's needs).
Environment variables
spark.conf.set() or os.environ[].
No rotation support. Requires process restart.
No audit trail.
Prohibited in production. Used only in development for local testing.

blocks.content: GridUnion’s Secret Architecture

# GridUnion's secret access pattern in production pipelines
# Never hardcode credentials. Never read from YAML. Always use the secret scope.

def get_jdbc_config(spark, system_name: str) -> dict:
    """Retrieve JDBC credentials from the Databricks secret scope.
    
    The scope is organized by system: 'gridunion-{system}-{env}'
    Keys are: 'url', 'user', 'password'
    Secrets are managed by the platform team and rotated quarterly.
    """
    env = spark.conf.get('spark.gridunion.environment', 'production')
    scope = f"gridunion-{system_name}-{env}"
    
    return {
        'url': dbutils.secrets.get(scope=scope, key='url'),
        'user': dbutils.secrets.get(scope=scope, key='user'),
        'password': dbutils.secrets.get(scope=scope, key='password'),
    }

# Usage in the pipeline
jdbc_config = get_jdbc_config(spark, 'crm_postgres')
df_customers = (spark.read
    .format('jdbc')
    .option('url', jdbc_config['url'])
    .option('user', jdbc_config['user'])
    .option('password', jdbc_config['password'])
    .option('dbtable', '(SELECT * FROM crm.customers WHERE country = \'DE\') AS t')
    .load()
)

# The password is never visible in:
# - The notebook output (Databricks redacts secrets)
# - The Spark UI (redacted in the configuration display)
# - The pipeline logs (redacted by the logging framework)
# - The Git repository (no credentials in code or config files)

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Secret management is the security control with the highest cost of failure and the lowest cost of implementation. A leaked production database password exposes customer PII to unauthorized access — a GDPR violation with financial penalties. The cost of proper secret management (Databricks secret scopes + Key Vault) is approximately $200/month for Key Vault and 2 hours of platform team setup per external system. The cost-benefit ratio is extreme: $200/month prevents a potential multi-million-euro regulatory penalty. GridUnion's security audit checks for credential leakage in three places: Git repository scanning (automated by GitHub's secret scanning), notebook output scanning (weekly), and Spark configuration display scanning (nightly). Any credential found outside the secret scope triggers an immediate rotation and a security incident.

Catalog-level access control (Unity Catalog privileges) and data-level protection (row filters, column masking) secure data at the application layer. Network isolation secures data at the infrastructure layer: ensuring that the Spark cluster can only communicate with authorized systems and that unauthorized networks cannot reach the cluster. The two layers are complementary: application-layer security prevents authorized users from accessing unauthorized data, and network-layer security prevents unauthorized networks from reaching the platform at all. GridUnion's network design creates three security zones: the production zone (production and analytics clusters, ADLS storage, and the Unity Catalog metastore), the integration zone (connections to external systems: PostgreSQL, Oracle, vendor SFTP servers), and the corporate zone (user workstations, VPN access for notebook sessions). Traffic between zones is controlled by Azure Network Security Groups (NSGs) with explicit allow rules.
GridUnion’s Network Topology

blocks.title: GridUnion network security zones
blocks.caption: The three security zones are connected by controlled pathways. The production zone communicates with ADLS via private endpoints (no public internet exposure). The integration zone is reachable only via VPC peering with port-restricted NSGs. The corporate zone accesses only the Databricks workspace UI via HTTPS.

blocks.content: Private Endpoints for Cloud Storage

GridUnion's ADLS storage account is configured with a private endpoint in the production VNET and public network access disabled. This ensures that the Parquet files (including unmasked PII data) are accessible only from within the production zone's VNET, not from the public internet. Even if an attacker obtains a valid ADLS access token, they cannot reach the storage endpoint from outside the VNET. The private endpoint configuration adds approximately 5% latency to ADLS requests (the traffic routes through the Azure backbone rather than the public internet path) but eliminates the attack surface of publicly accessible storage. GridUnion's security assessment classified ADLS private endpoints as a mandatory control for any storage account containing PII data. Non-PII storage accounts (development test data, public reference datasets) may use public endpoints with SAS token authentication.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Defense in depth requires controls at every layer: application (Unity Catalog privileges), data (row filters, column masks), and network (VNET isolation, private endpoints).
