blocks.content: The Streaming Operational Runbook

items: Streaming query stopped (alert: query not active for > 2 minutes): Check the driver log for exceptions. Common causes: driver OOM during checkpoint commit, Kafka broker unavailable, Delta table locked by OPTIMIZE. Restart the query; the checkpoint ensures exactly-once recovery.

Backpressure detected (alert: input rate > processing rate for 3+ batches): The auto-scaler should handle this within 5 minutes. If backpressure persists after scaling, check for data skew in Kafka partitions (one partition may have disproportionate traffic). Rebalance Kafka partitions or increase maxOffsetsPerTrigger.

State size growing unboundedly (alert: state > 2x expected maximum): The watermark is not advancing. Check the event timestamps: if timestamps are in the far future (clock skew) or the far past (stale events), the watermark cannot advance past them. Filter out events with timestamps more than 1 hour from the current time.

Late data rate elevated (alert: > 1% for 30 minutes): The source's delivery latency has increased. Check the Kafka consumer lag metrics. If lag is high, the consumer is falling behind and events arrive after the watermark. Consider extending the watermark or investigating the Kafka infrastructure.

Exactly-once audit failure (alert: Kafka count != Delta count): Stop the streaming query. Check for uncommitted Delta transactions (orphaned files from a failed batch). If duplicates: the deduplication pipeline (lesson 7) may have a watermark gap. If data loss: the checkpoint may have advanced past data that was not successfully written. Investigate the checkpoint and Delta transaction log.

blocks.content: What Comes Next

Modules 8–9 complete the first two-thirds of the enterprise operations phase. Module 8 secured the platform (multi-tenancy, access control, audit logging, compliance). Module 9 extended the platform to near-real-time processing (Kafka integration, stream-stream joins, state management, exactly-once delivery, streaming monitoring). Module 10 — the final module of the senior-level track and the final module of the entire PySpark curriculum — addresses the organizational and operational frameworks that sustain the platform over years: data lineage at platform scale, capacity planning, SLA governance, incident management, data quality at 200+ tables, platform deprecation and sunsetting, team structures, disaster recovery, and the enterprise governance framework that ties the entire 30-module, 300-lesson curriculum together.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: A streaming platform is as reliable as its weakest operational practice. The Kafka-to-Delta pipeline's exactly-once guarantee is sound. The RocksDB state store's performance is excellent. The stream-stream join's watermark-bounded state is well-calibrated. But if the monitoring does not detect backpressure before it becomes an unrecoverable backlog, if the schema migration runbook is not followed during a source change, if the exactly-once audit is not run daily, or if the auto-scaler does not respond to load spikes, the streaming platform fails despite the correctness of its individual components. Streaming reliability is an operational discipline, not a technical feature.

chapters.title: Enterprise Governance and Capacity Planning
chapters.description: Establish the organizational and operational frameworks that sustain a multi-team Spark platform over years — data lineage, capacity planning, platform SLA governance, and the team structures that make data engineering reliable at enterprise scale.

blocks.content: Data lineage is the directed graph that traces every data element from its source to its final consumer: which tables were read, which transformations were applied, which tables were written, and which downstream tables, reports, and dashboards depend on the result. At mid-level, lineage was implicit — one team's pipeline was simple enough that the engineer could trace the data flow in their head. At GridUnion scale, 50 pipelines feed 200+ tables consumed by 40 reports, 20 notebooks, and 6 teams. No single person can trace the flow from the French meter vendor's CSV file through Bronze, Silver, Gold, and Platinum to the German billing report that depends on French cross-country totals. Lineage makes this flow explicit, queryable, and actionable. The lineage graph is the foundation for impact analysis (lesson 2), capacity planning (lesson 3), SLA attribution (lesson 4), incident diagnosis (lesson 5), and deprecation safety (lesson 7). Without lineage, every governance process requires manual investigation; with lineage, governance processes are automated.
Lineage Capture from Spark Query Plans

Spark's query execution produces a physical plan that contains the table scan operators (which tables were read) and the write operator (which table was written). The lineage capture system extracts this information after each pipeline run by parsing the plan's scan and write operators. Each scan produces a lineage edge: (source_table, pipeline, target_table). A pipeline that reads silver_de.meter_readings and silver_de.meter_metadata to write gold_billing.cross_country_totals produces two edges: (silver_de.meter_readings → gold_billing.cross_country_totals) and (silver_de.meter_metadata → gold_billing.cross_country_totals). The edges are stored in a lineage Delta table with columns for source_table, target_table, pipeline_name, pipeline_team, capture_timestamp, and the query plan hash (to detect when the lineage changes because the pipeline's code changed). GridUnion's lineage table has approximately 350 edges connecting 200 tables through 50 pipelines.
The Lineage Graph

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, collect_set, array_distinct

def capture_lineage(spark, pipeline_name, pipeline_team, query_df, target_table):
    """Capture lineage edges from a DataFrame's query plan.
    
    Extracts source tables from the physical plan's scan operators
    and records edges to the lineage Delta table.
    """
    plan_str = query_df._jdf.queryExecution().executedPlan().toString()
    
    # Extract table references from FileScan operators
    source_tables = []
    for line in plan_str.split('\n'):
        line = line.strip()
        if 'FileScan' in line and 'delta' in line.lower():
            # Extract the table path from the scan operator
            path_match = extract_table_path(line)  # custom parser
            if path_match:
                source_tables.append(path_match)
    
    # Write lineage edges
    from datetime import datetime
    edges = [(src, target_table, pipeline_name, pipeline_team, datetime.now())
             for src in set(source_tables)]
    
    if edges:
        edge_df = spark.createDataFrame(
            edges,
            'source_table STRING, target_table STRING, pipeline_name STRING, '
            'pipeline_team STRING, captured_at TIMESTAMP'
        )
        edge_df.write.format('delta').mode('append').saveAsTable(
            'gridunion_prod.monitoring.lineage_graph'
        )

def query_downstream(spark, table_fqn, max_depth=5):
    """Find all tables downstream of a given table, up to max_depth hops."""
    lineage = spark.table('gridunion_prod.monitoring.lineage_graph')
    
    visited = set()
    frontier = {table_fqn}
    all_downstream = []
    
    for depth in range(max_depth):
        if not frontier:
            break
        downstream = (lineage
            .filter(col('source_table').isin(list(frontier)))
            .select('target_table', 'pipeline_name', 'pipeline_team')
            .distinct()
            .collect()
        )
        new_tables = set()
        for row in downstream:
            if row['target_table'] not in visited:
                visited.add(row['target_table'])
                new_tables.add(row['target_table'])
                all_downstream.append({
                    'table': row['target_table'],
                    'pipeline': row['pipeline_name'],
                    'team': row['pipeline_team'],
                    'depth': depth + 1,
                })
        frontier = new_tables
    
    return all_downstream

The lineage graph enables two query patterns that are essential for governance. Forward lineage (query_downstream) answers: 'if silver_de.meter_readings changes, which downstream tables, pipelines, and teams are affected?' This powers impact analysis (lesson 2) and deprecation safety (lesson 7). Backward lineage (the reverse query) answers: 'where does the data in gold_billing.cross_country_totals come from, through which pipelines, and from which original sources?' This powers incident diagnosis (lesson 5) and regulatory audit (Module 8, lesson 9). GridUnion's lineage graph is refreshed after every pipeline run (the capture function runs in the pipeline's finally block), so it reflects the actual data flow, not the intended flow documented in design documents. If a pipeline is refactored to read from a different source, the lineage updates automatically on the next run.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Lineage is the governance substrate: it is the data structure that makes every other governance process possible. Impact analysis, SLA attribution, incident diagnosis, deprecation safety, and capacity planning all query the lineage graph. A platform without lineage is a platform where every governance question requires manual investigation — 'who is affected by this change?' becomes a Slack thread instead of a query. The investment in lineage capture (approximately 1 second of overhead per pipeline run for plan parsing and edge storage) pays for itself the first time an impact analysis that would have taken 2 hours of manual investigation completes in 5 seconds.

Impact analysis is the practice of determining, before making a change, which components will be affected by the change and which teams must be notified. At GridUnion scale, changes are frequent: 3–5 pipeline deployments per week, 1–2 schema changes per month, occasional infrastructure changes (Spark version upgrades, cluster resizing, storage migration), and periodic data quality incidents that degrade upstream tables. Each change has a blast radius: the set of downstream tables, pipelines, reports, and teams that will see different behavior after the change. The impact analysis tool uses the lineage graph (lesson 1), the data product consumer registry (Module 1, lesson 5), and the schema contract registry (Module 5, lesson 3) to compute the blast radius automatically. The tool's output is a structured report that lists every affected component, the nature of the impact (data freshness, schema change, quality degradation), and the contact person for each affected team.
Types of Changes and Their Propagation

rows: Schema change (additive)
Propagates through pipelines that use SELECT * (absorbed by explicit column lists)
Limited: only pipelines using SELECT * on the changed table
Schema drift detector (Module 5, lesson 8) + cascade analysis (Module 5, lesson 7)
Schema change (breaking)
Propagates to every downstream pipeline and consumer that references the affected column
Potentially platform-wide: a Silver column removal affects all Gold and Platinum tables that use it
Contract compatibility checker (Module 5, lesson 4) + lineage forward query
Data quality degradation
Propagates downstream: if Silver quality drops, Gold aggregates become wrong, Platinum features degrade
All downstream tables and consumers, depth-unlimited
Quality gate failure alert + lineage forward query to identify affected consumers
Pipeline delay
Propagates through the dependency graph: if Silver is late, Gold is late, Platinum is late
All downstream pipelines with dependency-aware scheduling (Module 1, lesson 6)
SLA monitoring + dependency graph traversal to attribute the delay to the root cause
Infrastructure change (Spark upgrade)
Potentially affects every pipeline through plan changes (Module 2, lesson 6)
Platform-wide: every pipeline may produce a different execution plan
Plan fingerprint comparison (Module 2, lesson 6) across all pipelines

blocks.content: The Automated Impact Report

def generate_impact_report(spark, changed_table, change_type, change_details):
    """Generate a structured impact analysis report for a proposed change.
    
    Combines lineage graph, consumer registry, and contract registry
    to produce a comprehensive blast radius assessment.
    """
    # 1. Forward lineage: all downstream tables
    downstream = query_downstream(spark, changed_table, max_depth=5)
    
    # 2. Consumer registry: all registered consumers of the changed table
    consumers = (spark.table('gridunion_prod.monitoring.ownership_registry')
        .filter(col('consumers').contains(changed_table))
        .collect()
    )
    
    # 3. Contract check: does the change violate any contract?
    contract_violations = check_contract_impact(spark, changed_table, change_details)
    
    report = {
        'changed_table': changed_table,
        'change_type': change_type,
        'change_details': change_details,
        'downstream_tables': len(downstream),
        'downstream_teams': list(set(d['team'] for d in downstream)),
        'contract_violations': contract_violations,
        'notification_list': build_notification_list(downstream, consumers),
        'risk_level': assess_risk(change_type, len(downstream), len(contract_violations)),
    }
    
    # Log the report
    log_impact_report(spark, report)
    
    # If high risk, require governance committee approval
    if report['risk_level'] == 'high':
        send_alert(
            f"High-risk change proposed on {changed_table}: "
            f"{len(downstream)} downstream tables, {len(contract_violations)} contract violations. "
            f"Governance committee review required."
        )
    
    return report

GridUnion runs the impact analysis tool as a pre-merge CI check for every PR that modifies a pipeline's schema-affecting code (DDL statements, schema definitions, column references). The CI check computes the blast radius and adds a comment to the PR: 'This change affects 7 downstream tables across 3 teams. 0 contract violations. Risk level: medium. Notification list: [billing_team, analytics_team, regulatory_de_team].' The PR reviewer uses this information to decide whether the change needs the full breaking change process (Module 5, lesson 6) or can proceed with a lightweight notification. Changes with zero downstream impact (a new internal column that no consumer references) proceed without notification. Changes that violate a contract require the full deprecation process. The CI integration ensures that no change reaches production without an assessed blast radius — the impact analysis is not optional, it is a deployment gate.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Impact analysis shifts the cost of coordination from incident response (discovering affected components after the change) to change planning (discovering affected components before the change). The cost of pre-change analysis is small (a 5-second lineage query and a CI comment). The cost of post-change incident response is large (2–14 hours of multi-team diagnosis, as GridUnion experienced during the French vendor schema crisis in Module 5, lesson 1). The impact analysis tool is the mechanism that converts 14-hour incidents into 5-second CI checks.

Capacity planning forecasts the compute and storage resources the platform will need 6–12 months from now, based on data growth projections, new pipeline additions, and workload pattern changes. Without capacity planning, the platform either over-provisions (wasting 30–50% of the cloud budget on unused resources) or under-provisions (hitting resource limits during peak periods, causing SLA breaches). GridUnion's annual capacity planning process produces a 12-month resource forecast that feeds into the cloud budget request, the cluster sizing decisions, and the storage allocation plan. The forecast is not a guess — it is a model built from 12 months of historical pipeline metrics, projected data volume growth from the business plan, and the resource requirements of new pipelines in the approved roadmap.
The Capacity Model

def build_capacity_forecast(spark, planning_months=12):
    """Build a 12-month capacity forecast from historical metrics and growth projections.
    
    Returns monthly projections for compute (executor-hours), storage (TB), and cost ($).
    """
    # 1. Historical baseline: average monthly resource consumption over the last 12 months
    metrics = spark.table('gridunion_prod.monitoring.pipeline_metrics')
    monthly_baseline = (metrics
        .filter(col('process_date') >= add_months(current_date(), -12))
        .withColumn('month', date_trunc('month', col('process_date')))
        .groupBy('month')
        .agg(
            spark_sum('duration_seconds').alias('total_compute_seconds'),
            spark_sum('input_row_count').alias('total_rows_processed'),
        )
        .orderBy('month')
        .collect()
    )
    
    # 2. Growth rate: linear regression on the last 12 months
    compute_values = [r['total_compute_seconds'] for r in monthly_baseline]
    monthly_growth_rate = compute_linear_growth_rate(compute_values)  # % per month
    
    # 3. Storage growth: current size + daily growth rate × days
    storage_current_tb = get_total_storage_tb(spark)
    storage_daily_growth_gb = get_daily_storage_growth_gb(spark)
    
    # 4. New pipeline additions from the approved roadmap
    new_pipeline_compute = get_roadmap_compute_estimate(spark)  # manual input from teams
    
    # 5. Project forward
    forecast = []
    for month_offset in range(1, planning_months + 1):
        compute = compute_values[-1] * (1 + monthly_growth_rate) ** month_offset
        compute += new_pipeline_compute.get(month_offset, 0)
        storage = storage_current_tb + (storage_daily_growth_gb * 30 * month_offset / 1024)
        
        # Convert to cost
        compute_cost = (compute / 3600) * config['cost_per_executor_hour']  # $0.25/executor-hour
        storage_cost = storage * config['cost_per_tb_month']  # $23/TB/month for ADLS
        
        forecast.append({
            'month': month_offset,
            'compute_executor_hours': round(compute / 3600),
            'storage_tb': round(storage, 1),
            'compute_cost': round(compute_cost),
            'storage_cost': round(storage_cost),
            'total_cost': round(compute_cost + storage_cost),
        })
    
    return forecast
The Annual Budget Request

GridUnion's capacity forecast is presented to the finance department as a 12-month cost projection with three scenarios: baseline (current growth rate continues, no new pipelines), planned (current growth plus the approved pipeline roadmap), and high-growth (current growth plus the roadmap plus a 20% buffer for unplanned workloads). The three scenarios produce a cost range rather than a point estimate, giving the finance department flexibility in budget allocation. The planned scenario is the budget request; the high-growth scenario is the maximum that the platform team may spend without additional approval. GridUnion's 2026 capacity forecast: baseline $2.1M, planned $2.5M, high-growth $3.0M. The actual 2025 spending was $2.3M, 8% above the planned scenario (due to the ML team's unplanned demand forecasting expansion that consumed more GPU compute than estimated). The 8% overrun was within the high-growth buffer, requiring no budget escalation.

The capacity model is recalculated quarterly to incorporate actual growth data and adjust the forward projection. The quarterly update compares the actual resource consumption against the forecast for the current quarter: if actual exceeds forecast by more than 15%, the remaining quarters' projections are revised upward. If actual is below forecast by more than 15%, the projections are revised downward (and the surplus is either returned to the corporate budget pool or reallocated to platform improvements). This rolling forecast model prevents the common anti-pattern of annual budget requests that are either dramatically over-estimated (safe but wasteful) or under-estimated (resulting in mid-year emergency budget requests that disrupt the finance planning cycle).

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Capacity planning is the financial governance of the data platform. Every architectural decision in this track has a cost: more executors for bucketed joins, more storage for Delta time travel retention, more compute for streaming pipelines, more infrastructure for disaster recovery. The capacity model quantifies these costs and projects them forward, connecting the architectural decisions to the budget that funds them. A platform architect who designs a system without capacity modeling is like an architect who designs a building without a budget — the design may be technically excellent but financially unsustainable. GridUnion's governance committee reviews every architectural proposal with the capacity model's cost projection: 'this streaming pipeline will add $3,200/month to the compute budget. Is the business value sufficient?'

The mid-level track defined SLAs per pipeline: the Silver pipeline must complete by 7 AM, the Gold pipeline by 7:30 AM. At platform scale, SLAs are defined per data product rather than per pipeline, because the consumer cares about data freshness (when can I read the data?) not pipeline duration (how long did the pipeline take?). A pipeline that completes in 5 minutes but writes to a table that consumers cannot query for another 25 minutes (because of compaction locks, replication lag, or cache invalidation) delivers 30-minute freshness despite 5-minute pipeline duration. GridUnion's SLA governance defines freshness SLAs per data product, tracks freshness as the elapsed time between the data's event timestamp and the timestamp at which the table becomes queryable, and attributes SLA breaches to the responsible component (pipeline, compaction, replication, or cache) using the dependency graph.
SLA Tiers

rows: Platinum SLA
< 1 minute (near-real-time)
99.9% monthly
< 0.01% duplicate rate, < 0.1% null rate
Every 30 seconds
Streaming Silver for the grid balancing system. Operations dashboard consumption feed.
Gold SLA
< 60 minutes (batch)
99.5% monthly
< 0.5% null rate, row count within expected range
Every 5 minutes
Daily Silver and Gold batch tables. Billing reports. Regulatory exports.
Silver SLA
< 4 hours (best-effort batch)
99.0% monthly
< 1.0% null rate
Every 15 minutes
Platinum analytics tables. ML training features. Ad-hoc analysis tables.
Bronze SLA (untiered)
No freshness target
Best-effort
Data is ingested as-is from source
Daily
Bronze raw ingestion. Development tables. Experimental pipelines.

blocks.content: SLA Attribution

When a data product's freshness SLA is breached, the platform must determine which component caused the breach: the pipeline itself (it ran slowly), an upstream dependency (the upstream table was late, delaying the downstream pipeline), the infrastructure (the cluster was at capacity and the pipeline waited in the queue), or the maintenance layer (a compaction job locked the table during the pipeline's write window). GridUnion's SLA attribution system traces the delay through the dependency graph (Module 1, lesson 6): it starts with the breached data product, walks backward through the lineage to find the pipeline that produces it, checks the pipeline's start and end times against the SLA window, and if the pipeline completed on time, checks the next component in the chain (compaction, replication, cache refresh). The first component that exceeded its budget is identified as the root cause. The responsible team receives the SLA breach notification, not the team whose data product was late.

GridUnion's SLA governance committee meets monthly to review SLA performance across all data products. The review examines: the number of SLA breaches per tier per team (trended over 6 months), the root cause distribution (what fraction of breaches are pipeline-caused vs infrastructure-caused vs dependency-caused), and the corrective actions taken for recurring breaches. A data product that breaches its SLA 3 times in a quarter triggers a mandatory architecture review: is the SLA realistic for the current data volume and pipeline complexity? Should the data product be downgraded to a lower SLA tier? Does the pipeline need optimization (Module 2–4) or additional resources (Module 3, capacity planning)? The architecture review produces an action plan with specific changes and a target date. The governance committee tracks the action plan's execution and verifies that the breach frequency decreases after the changes are deployed.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: SLA tiers create a priority hierarchy that the entire platform enforces: Platinum SLA data products get cluster resources before Gold, Gold before Silver, and Silver before untiered. The tier determines not just the freshness target but the monitoring frequency, the escalation speed, and the resource priority. Assigning every data product to a tier forces the product owner to make a commitment (I guarantee this freshness) and gives the platform team the authority to prioritize resources accordingly. Without tiers, every team claims their pipeline is 'critical,' and the platform team cannot arbitrate resource contention. With tiers, the tier assignment is the arbitration: the billing table is Platinum SLA, the analytics feature table is Silver SLA, and the resource priority follows.

Data platform incidents differ from software application incidents in three important ways. First, data incidents are often silent: a pipeline that produces wrong numbers does not crash — it completes successfully with incorrect output. The billing team discovers the error when their monthly report shows a 15% discrepancy, not when a pipeline fails. Second, data incidents propagate: an error in the Silver layer produces wrong Gold tables, which produce wrong Platinum tables, which produce wrong dashboard numbers. By the time the error is discovered, the blast radius includes every downstream consumer. Third, data incidents require recovery, not just fixes: after the pipeline bug is fixed, the incorrect data must be corrected. A software bug fix deploys new code and the problem is resolved. A data bug fix deploys new code and then reprocesses the affected data to overwrite the incorrect output — a second operation that the software world does not have. These three differences mean that data incident management requires its own process, distinct from the standard software incident response.
The Data Incident Response Process

items: Detection: The incident is detected via monitoring (SLA breach, quality gate failure, anomaly detection), consumer report (a team notices wrong numbers), or audit (the daily reconciliation or exactly-once audit catches a discrepancy). Detection latency is the time between the incident occurring and the team becoming aware. GridUnion's target: < 15 minutes for Platinum SLA products, < 2 hours for Gold SLA products.

Triage: The on-call engineer determines the incident's scope using the lineage graph and the impact analysis tool (lesson 2). How many tables are affected? How many teams are consuming incorrect data? Is the incident ongoing (the pipeline is still running with the bug) or historical (the bug has been fixed but incorrect data remains)? The triage produces a severity classification: SEV-1 (Platinum SLA breach or data corruption affecting billing/regulatory), SEV-2 (Gold SLA breach or quality degradation), SEV-3 (Silver SLA breach or minor quality issue).

Containment: For ongoing incidents, stop the affected pipeline to prevent further incorrect data production. For downstream propagation, notify consumer teams to halt their pipelines or add defensive filters. For data corruption, use Delta time travel to restore the affected table to its pre-incident version (Module 4, mid-level, lesson 7).

Root cause analysis: Identify the specific code change, data change, or infrastructure change that caused the incident. Use the lineage graph to trace the error to its source. Use the plan fingerprint comparison (Module 2, lesson 6) to detect plan regressions. Use the schema drift detector (Module 5, lesson 8) to detect upstream schema changes.

Fix and recovery: Deploy the code fix through the standard CI/CD process (Module 7, mid-level). Reprocess the affected data using the backfill strategy (Module 5, mid-level, lesson 4). Verify the reprocessed output against the expected values. Notify consumer teams that the data has been corrected.

Post-mortem: Conduct a blameless post-mortem within 5 business days. Document the timeline, the root cause, the blast radius, the recovery steps, and the corrective actions to prevent recurrence. Publish the post-mortem to the platform-wide incident log.

blocks.content: The Blameless Post-Mortem Template

GridUnion's post-mortem template asks five questions. What happened? (A factual timeline from detection to resolution, with timestamps and actions.) What was the impact? (Which tables, teams, reports, and business processes were affected, and for how long? Quantify: 'Gold billing table contained incorrect cross-country totals for March 1–3, affecting $2.4M in invoice calculations.') Why did it happen? (The root cause chain: 'A Python UDF was added to the Silver pipeline without updating the memory overhead configuration. The UDF consumed off-heap memory that exceeded the container limit. The container was killed by YARN, producing a partial write that Delta's transaction log committed because the task retry succeeded with different data.') Why was it not prevented? (Which control should have caught the incident and why it did not: 'The CI pipeline does not test memory overhead for UDF-containing pipelines. The quality gate on the Silver table checks row count but not value correctness.') What will we change? (Specific, actionable corrective actions with owners and deadlines: 'Add a CI test for memory overhead when UDFs are present (owner: platform team, deadline: March 20). Add a value-level quality check for cross-country totals (owner: billing team, deadline: March 25).')

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: The blameless post-mortem is the mechanism that converts incidents into platform improvements. Without post-mortems, the same incident recurs because the systemic cause is never addressed. With post-mortems, each incident produces specific corrective actions that strengthen the platform's defenses. GridUnion's 18 post-mortems over 14 months have produced 47 corrective actions, including: adding the schema drift detector (Module 5, lesson 8, triggered by the French vendor schema crisis), adding the plan stability test suite (Module 2, lesson 6, triggered by a Spark upgrade regression), and adding the exactly-once audit (Module 9, lesson 5, triggered by a JDBC duplicate incident). The platform is measurably more reliable today because each incident made it stronger.

The mid-level track implemented quality gates per pipeline: null rate checks, row count checks, and value range checks that run between stages and block the write if any check fails. At platform scale, quality must be monitored across 200+ tables owned by 6 teams with different quality standards, different check implementations, and different response times. A quality degradation in one team's Silver table propagates to another team's Gold table, and the consuming team may not know that the quality issue exists until their own downstream consumers report wrong numbers. Platform-scale data quality requires three capabilities that the mid-level quality gates do not provide: centralized quality rule management (one place to define and view all quality rules across all tables), cross-table quality correlation (detecting that a Silver quality drop caused a Gold quality drop, not just that both dropped), and quality SLAs in data contracts (the producer guarantees a quality level, and the consumer can rely on it without running their own checks).
The Quality Rule Registry

GridUnion's quality rule registry is a Delta table that stores every quality rule applied to every table on the platform. Each rule has a rule_id, a table_fqn, a rule_type (null_rate, row_count_range, value_range, referential_integrity, uniqueness, custom_sql), a rule_definition (the specific check: 'null_rate(daily_kw) < 0.005'), a severity (blocking or warning), and an owner team. The registry enables three operations that per-pipeline quality gates cannot: platform-wide quality dashboarding (how many rules pass across all 200 tables?), rule consistency auditing (do all Silver tables have the same baseline quality checks?), and quality gap detection (which tables have zero quality rules?). GridUnion's quality audit revealed that 15 of 200 tables had zero quality rules — all created by the analytics team during rapid prototyping without going through the data product registration process. The tables were either brought under quality governance or deprecated.
Cross-Table Quality Correlation

When multiple quality checks fail simultaneously across related tables, the root cause is usually a single upstream event. GridUnion's quality correlation system groups simultaneous failures by their lineage relationship: if silver_de.meter_readings fails a null rate check and gold_billing.cross_country_totals fails a row count check within the same processing window, and the lineage graph shows that the Gold table depends on the Silver table, the system classifies the two failures as a single correlated incident with the Silver failure as the root cause. Without correlation, the on-call engineer receives two independent alerts and may investigate them separately, doubling the diagnosis effort. With correlation, the engineer receives one correlated alert: 'Quality failures on silver_de.meter_readings (null rate) and gold_billing.cross_country_totals (row count) are correlated via lineage. Investigate the Silver table first.' GridUnion's correlation reduces the average number of quality alerts per incident from 3.2 (uncorrelated) to 1.0 (correlated), reducing alert fatigue and focusing the engineer on the root cause.
Quality SLAs in Data Contracts

Module 5, lesson 3's data contract includes a quality section that specifies the producer's quality guarantees: 'null_rate(daily_kw) < 0.5%, row_count per day between 350,000 and 600,000, all 8 German regions present every day.' These quality SLAs are not aspirational — they are monitored and enforced. The quality rule registry stores the contract's quality rules, the quality monitoring pipeline evaluates them after every batch, and a quality SLA breach triggers the same escalation process as a freshness SLA breach (lesson 4). The consumer team depends on the quality SLA: the billing team does not run its own null rate check on the Silver table because the Silver producer's contract guarantees < 0.5% null rate. If the guarantee is breached, the producer team is responsible for the fix, and the quality SLA breach is attributed to the producer in the monthly governance report.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Data quality at platform scale is a governance problem, not a technical problem. The technical components (null checks, row counts, value ranges) are simple. The governance components (who defines the rules? who monitors them? who is responsible when they fail? how are failures correlated across tables?) are complex. The quality rule registry, the cross-table correlation, and the quality SLAs in data contracts are the governance mechanisms that transform per-pipeline quality gates into platform-wide quality assurance. The shift from 'each team checks its own data' to 'the platform monitors quality for everyone and attributes failures to the responsible team' is the same shift that Module 1 described for ownership: centralize the governance, distribute the responsibility.

Platforms accumulate technical debt in the form of unused tables, orphaned pipelines, and deprecated APIs that nobody decommissions. At GridUnion, the post-acquisition integration imported 80 tables from 3 subsidiaries. Eighteen months later, 23 of those tables have zero registered consumers (they were replaced by consolidated GridUnion tables or were never actually needed). Each orphaned table consumes storage ($0.50/GB/month), compaction compute (the automated maintenance job does not check for consumers), and operational attention (the monitoring dashboard includes them in the quality and freshness checks). Deprecation is the disciplined process of identifying unused resources, notifying any remaining consumers, providing a migration path, and eventually removing the resource. Without a deprecation process, the platform grows indefinitely: every table ever created persists, every pipeline ever deployed continues running, and the operational burden grows faster than the value the platform delivers.
The Deprecation Lifecycle

rows: Active
Indefinite
Table is maintained, monitored, and governed. Quality SLAs enforced.
Consumers read freely. New consumers register.
Deprecation announced
Day 0
Table's data product manifest updated to status: deprecated. Consumer notification sent.
Existing consumers informed. No new consumer registrations accepted.
Migration support
90 days
Producer provides migration guidance: which replacement table to use, schema mapping, query rewrites. Producer maintains both old and new tables.
Consumers migrate to the replacement. Producer verifies each consumer's migration.
Soft sunset
90–180 days
Table is still queryable but no longer maintained: no quality monitoring, no compaction, no freshness SLA.
Remaining consumers see degrading data quality and are warned.
Hard sunset
Day 180
Table is dropped (after a final backup to cold storage for regulatory retention). Pipeline is decommissioned.
Any remaining consumer's queries fail. The failure is expected — they were notified 180 days ago.

blocks.content: Identifying Deprecation Candidates

def find_deprecation_candidates(spark, inactive_days=90):
    """Identify tables with no consumer activity in the last N days.
    
    Uses the audit log to detect tables that are not being read.
    Cross-references with the ownership registry for consumer lists.
    """
    # Tables with no SELECT events in the audit log for N days
    audit = spark.table('gridunion_prod.monitoring.audit_log')
    active_tables = (audit
        .filter(col('event_type') == 'TABLE_ACCESS')
        .filter(col('event_timestamp') >= date_sub(current_date(), inactive_days))
        .select('table_fqn').distinct()
    )
    
    all_tables = spark.table('gridunion_prod.monitoring.ownership_registry')
    
    candidates = (all_tables
        .join(active_tables, all_tables.table_name == active_tables.table_fqn, 'left_anti')
        .select('catalog', 'schema', 'table_name', 'owner_team', 'registered_at')
    )
    
    result = candidates.collect()
    print(f"Deprecation candidates ({inactive_days} days inactive): {len(result)} tables")
    for r in result:
        age_days = (datetime.now() - r['registered_at']).days
        print(f"  {r['catalog']}.{r['schema']}.{r['table_name']}  "
              f"owner={r['owner_team']}  age={age_days}d")
    
    return result

GridUnion runs the deprecation candidate finder quarterly as part of the governance committee's platform health review. The quarterly review typically identifies 3–5 tables that have become inactive since the previous review. The table's owner team is notified and asked to confirm: is the table still needed? If the team confirms it is no longer needed, the deprecation lifecycle begins. If the team believes the table is still needed but has no active consumers (perhaps it is used quarterly for reporting, and the reporting period has not occurred during the 90-day window), the table is exempted from deprecation with a documented justification. The quarterly cadence ensures that deprecation is a routine process, not a crisis-driven cleanup. The 180-day total lifecycle (90 days migration + 90 days soft sunset) is long enough that even the most infrequent consumers have time to migrate, while short enough that the platform does not accumulate years of orphaned resources.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Deprecation is platform hygiene. A platform that only adds resources and never removes them grows until the operational burden (monitoring, maintenance, compliance auditing) exceeds the platform team's capacity. The deprecation lifecycle provides a safe, predictable process for removing resources: 180 days of notice, explicit consumer migration, and a final backup for regulatory retention. The process's safety encourages the governance committee to approve deprecation rather than avoiding it out of fear of breaking something. At GridUnion, the quarterly deprecation review has decommissioned 23 tables in 18 months, reducing storage costs by $14,000/year and removing 23 entries from the monitoring dashboard, the quality rule registry, and the compaction schedule.

The technical architecture of a data platform is constrained by its organizational structure. Conway's Law — systems mirror the communication patterns of the organizations that build them — applies directly: a platform built by one centralized team produces a monolithic architecture with a single catalog, a single deployment pipeline, and centralized ownership. A platform built by autonomous domain teams produces a federated architecture with per-team catalogs, per-team deployment pipelines, and distributed ownership. Neither extreme is optimal at enterprise scale. A fully centralized team becomes a bottleneck: every pipeline change requires the central team's review, and the team cannot keep up with 6 teams' demands. A fully federated structure produces inconsistency: each team invents its own standards, its own monitoring, and its own naming conventions, and the platform becomes ungovernable. GridUnion's organizational evolution illustrates both extremes and the hybrid model that resolved them.
Three Organizational Models

rows: Centralized platform team
One team builds and maintains all pipelines, all tables, all infrastructure.
Consistent standards, unified architecture, single point of expertise.
Bottleneck for pipeline development. Domain knowledge is shallow (the platform team does not understand each domain's data intimately).
Small platforms (< 20 pipelines) where one team can handle the volume.
Embedded data engineers
Each domain team has data engineers who build and maintain their own pipelines. A small platform team provides shared infrastructure.
Deep domain knowledge. Fast iteration (no dependency on a central team).
Inconsistent standards unless enforced by the platform team. Duplication of effort (each team builds its own monitoring, testing, deployment).
Medium platforms (20–50 pipelines) where domain specialization is critical.
Data mesh (domain ownership)
Each domain owns its data products end-to-end: schema, pipeline, quality, SLA. The platform team provides the self-service infrastructure (catalog, monitoring, CI/CD templates).
Maximum domain autonomy. Platform team focuses on infrastructure, not pipelines.
Requires mature domain teams with data engineering skills. Risk of divergence if the platform's self-service tools are not comprehensive.
Large platforms (50+ pipelines) with mature engineering teams.

blocks.content: GridUnion’s Evolution

GridUnion started with a centralized platform team (4 engineers) that built all pipelines. Within 3 months of the acquisition, the pipeline backlog exceeded 6 months: the central team could not build pipelines fast enough for 6 teams' demands. The first evolution was to embed data engineers in each domain team (the 3 subsidiary DE teams retained their engineers, who now built pipelines on the GridUnion platform). The embedded model solved the throughput problem but created a consistency problem: the 3 subsidiary teams used different naming conventions, different testing patterns, and different deployment processes. The second evolution was to shift to a hybrid data mesh model: each domain team owns its data products (schema, pipeline, quality, SLA), and the centralized platform team (now 6 engineers) provides the shared infrastructure: the Unity Catalog configuration, the CI/CD templates, the monitoring dashboard, the quality rule registry, the lineage capture, and the governance processes from this track. The platform team does not build pipelines; it builds the platform that makes pipelines buildable.

The hybrid model works at GridUnion because the platform team enforces standards through infrastructure rather than review: the CI templates include the quality checks, the naming convention validator, the contract compliance test, and the plan fingerprint comparison. A domain team that follows the CI template automatically complies with the platform standards. The platform team's enforcement mechanism is the CI pipeline, not the code review: the platform team does not review every pipeline PR (that would recreate the centralized bottleneck) but the CI pipeline validates every PR against the platform's standards (which scales automatically). The platform team's effort is proportional to the number of standards (which grows slowly) not to the number of pipelines (which grows quickly).

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: The platform team's role is not to build pipelines but to make the correct behavior automatic. Every standard in this track — naming conventions (Module 1), ownership contracts (Module 1), schema compatibility (Module 5), plan stability (Module 2), quality rules (this lesson) — is enforced through infrastructure (CI checks, monitoring alerts, registry validations) rather than through manual review. This infrastructure-first enforcement model scales: adding a new team requires providing them with the CI template and the registry access, not adding a reviewer to every PR. The platform team's success metric is not the number of pipelines they built but the number of standards that are enforced automatically.

A disaster recovery (DR) plan protects the data platform against events that take down an entire region: a cloud provider outage, a data center failure, a network partition, or a data corruption event that affects the primary storage. The DR plan defines two key metrics for each data layer: the Recovery Point Objective (RPO, the maximum acceptable data loss measured in time — how much data can we afford to lose?) and the Recovery Time Objective (RTO, the maximum acceptable downtime measured in time — how quickly must the platform be operational again?). The RPO and RTO vary by data layer and SLA tier: the streaming Silver table (Platinum SLA) has a tighter RPO/RTO than the historical archive (no SLA). The DR plan is the most expensive governance mechanism on the platform (cross-region replication costs $8,000/month for GridUnion) and the least frequently used (GridUnion has never activated the full DR plan), but its existence is a regulatory requirement and an executive assurance.
RPO and RTO by Data Layer

rows: Streaming Silver (Platinum SLA)
< 5 minutes
< 30 minutes
Continuous cross-region Delta replication via Change Data Feed. Standby streaming cluster in secondary region.
$4,800/year (replication) + $12,000/year (standby cluster)
Batch Silver and Gold (Gold SLA)
< 24 hours
< 4 hours
Daily snapshot replication to secondary region. Rebuild from snapshot + replay today's batch.
$3,600/year (snapshot storage + transfer)
Platinum analytics
< 24 hours
< 8 hours
Daily snapshot. Can be rebuilt from Gold tables if snapshot is unavailable.

< 48 hours

$0 (source retention covers the RPO)

blocks.content: The DR Activation Process

GridUnion's DR activation follows a three-phase process. Phase 1 (detection and decision, < 15 minutes): the platform team detects the regional outage through cloud provider health dashboards and cross-region health probes. The on-call engineer escalates to the platform team lead, who makes the activation decision based on the provider's estimated resolution time. If the estimated resolution exceeds the platform's RTO, DR is activated. Phase 2 (failover, < 30 minutes): the streaming cluster in the secondary region is activated and connected to the Kafka brokers (which are multi-region). The batch cluster in the secondary region is started and pointed to the replicated Delta tables. The Unity Catalog is updated to point to the secondary region's storage accounts. DNS or service endpoint routing is updated to direct consumers to the secondary cluster. Phase 3 (validation, < 60 minutes): the streaming pipeline's exactly-once audit verifies that no data was lost during the failover. The batch pipeline runs a reconciliation against the last known-good checkpoint. The operations dashboard is verified to show current data.
DR Testing

GridUnion conducts a DR drill every 6 months. The drill simulates a regional outage by blocking network access to the primary region's ADLS storage and activating the secondary region's infrastructure. The drill validates that the failover process completes within the RTO targets, that the replicated data is current within the RPO targets, and that all Platinum and Gold SLA data products are queryable from the secondary region. The drill also measures the failback process: returning to the primary region after the simulated outage is resolved. Failback requires replicating any data written during the DR period back to the primary region, which can take several hours for the streaming tables that accumulated data in the secondary region. The most recent drill (Q4 2025) completed failover in 22 minutes (within the 30-minute target) and failback in 3.5 hours. The drill revealed one issue: the monitoring dashboard's connection strings were hardcoded to the primary region and required manual update during failover. The corrective action was to parameterize the connection strings through the configuration repository (Module 1, lesson 7).

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Disaster recovery is insurance: expensive to maintain, never used in the expected case, and invaluable in the unexpected case. The $22,200/year cost of GridUnion's DR infrastructure protects against a scenario that has a low probability (regional cloud outages are rare) but a high impact (a multi-day platform outage would breach every SLA, violate regulatory requirements, and halt billing operations). The DR drill is the verification that the insurance actually works: an untested DR plan is not a plan but a hope. The 6-month drill cadence balances the cost of the drill (4–6 hours of the platform team's time plus the secondary region's compute cost) against the risk of the plan becoming stale (configuration changes, new tables, new pipelines that are not covered).

This is the final lesson of the senior-level track and the final lesson of the entire PySpark curriculum. It synthesizes the 10 senior-level modules into GridUnion Continental's enterprise governance framework: the integrated system of technical controls, organizational processes, and operational practices that sustains a multi-team data platform over years. The framework is not a summary of what was taught — it is a description of how the components interact as a system. The namespace hierarchy (Module 1) provides the structure that the schema contracts (Module 5) formalize. The schema contracts reference the lineage graph (this module, lesson 1) for impact analysis. The impact analysis feeds the SLA attribution (lesson 4) that the governance committee reviews monthly. The committee's decisions drive the capacity plan (lesson 3) that funds the DR infrastructure (lesson 9). Each component depends on and reinforces the others. Removing any component weakens the entire system.
The Ten Pillars of Platform Governance

blocks.content: The Governance Committee

GridUnion's governance committee is the organizational embodiment of the framework. The committee meets monthly and includes: the platform team lead (chair), one representative from each of the 6 teams, the data steward (regulatory compliance), and a rotating observer from the executive leadership. The monthly agenda follows a fixed structure: SLA performance review (lesson 4 metrics), quality dashboard review (lesson 6 metrics), capacity forecast update (lesson 3 projections), deprecation candidate review (lesson 7 candidates), open architectural proposals (new tables, new streaming pipelines, partition changes, schema changes that require committee approval), and incident review (lesson 5 post-mortems from the past month). The committee's decisions are documented in meeting minutes and linked to the relevant ADRs, contracts, or incident records. The minutes are published to the platform-wide wiki, ensuring transparency.
300 Lessons. 171 Hours. From Zero to Platform Architect.

The PySpark curriculum spans three tracks. The junior-level track (100 lessons, 43 hours) taught the foundations: DataFrames, transformations, joins, aggregations, I/O, and the Spark execution model. The mid-level track (100 lessons, 55 hours) taught production engineering: execution plans, partitioning, join optimization, Delta Lake, pipeline patterns, testing, CI/CD, memory management, performance monitoring, and streaming foundations. The senior-level track (100 lessons, 73 hours) taught platform architecture: multi-team governance, optimizer internals, partition strategy at scale, advanced join architecture, schema registries, execution engine internals, storage layer internals, multi-tenant security, advanced streaming, and enterprise governance. The NordGrid scenario evolved from 500,000 meters with 4 regions to GridUnion Continental's 3.5 million meters across 4 countries. The student evolved from someone who writes their first DataFrame to someone who designs the platform that makes DataFrames manageable for 50 engineers across 6 teams.

blocks.title: 300 lessons. 171 hours. From zero to platform architect.
blocks.content: The governance framework is not the destination — it is the operating system of the platform. The technical skills from 30 modules of PySpark content (DataFrames, plans, partitions, joins, schemas, shuffle, Parquet, Delta, streaming) are the building blocks. The governance framework (ownership, contracts, lineage, SLAs, incidents, capacity, deprecation, team structures) is the organizational software that coordinates the building blocks into a system that 6 teams operate reliably across 4 countries under 4 regulatory regimes. Technology determines what the platform can do. Governance determines whether it does it reliably, consistently, and sustainably over years. Both are essential. Neither alone is sufficient.
